body:
According to the spec calling `isSessionSupported()` can potentially reject the promise and then execute the rejection handler with a `DOMException` instead.

It seems the only cause for this exception is the mentioned security error:

> If the requesting documentŌĆÖs [origin](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/origin.html#concept-origin) is not allowed to use the "xr-spatial-tracking" [permissions policy](https://immersive-web.github.io/webxr/#permissions-policy), [reject](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#reject) promise with a "[SecurityError](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#securityerror)" [DOMException](https://webidl.spec.whatwg.org/#idl-DOMException) and return it.

Regarding this definition, the PR is definitely an improvement.

body:
Note that in lightmap_fragment.glsl.js we have the inverse behavior, multiplying the value by PI if `PHYSICALLY_CORRECT_LIGHTS` is not defined. This is to cancel out the effect of `BRDF_Lambert(diffuseColor)` which is applied by default in most other materials (which multiplies the diffuseColro by `RECIPROCAL_PI`).  **MeshBasicMaterial** does not apply BRDF_Lambert to the diffuseColor so we need to do the inverse for the lightmap to be "physically correct"

We probably do not want to change how a **MeshBasicMaterial** with no lightmap behaves so we instead apply this here, only to lightmaps, and only when `PHYSICALLY_CORRECT_LIGHTS` is defined. We could change the surrounding code a bit such that we could `#include <lightmpmap_fragment>` unchanged but it would slightly complicate the common case of a **MeshBasicMaterial** with no lightmap.

body:
Ok, I see! 

> ...my understanding is that some of them would need to be converted back to non-meshopt formats (e.g. byte normalized back to float for vertex positions)

It looks like you're part way to implementing https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/20474. No need to convert anything back to float32, I think we just need to detect when we're writing accessors in a format that the core specification doesn't allow, and append `KHR_mesh_quantization` to `extensionsUsed` and `extensionsRequired`. Loading a meshopt-compressed model with GLTFLoader will not leave any meshopt-specific data in the resulting scene, but does leave data that depends on quantization.

Up to you whether you'd prefer to keep the changes in this PR, I'm okay with it either way!

author: bergden

body:
There's no reason that world lines have to be handled in screen space. The only reason the screen space lines used the camera is because it's required based on how they're displayed. Picking effectively happens in 2D and given the way they're rendering proper picking is effectively undefined outside of the camera clip space. It's definitely a special case for the screen space line variant.

This isn't the case with the world unit lines, though. They're basically rendered as capsules. Meshes, for example, don't have special handling when they're behind the camera and don't consider the camera at all. This enables rays that are not derived from a camera and mouse coordinate to be used for raycasting (ie vr controller rays outside of view or rays for game mechanics outside of view)

The built in [GL line raycasting](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/1c553914ace73127cc67660597806b2c7c013548/src/objects/Line.js#L160-L189) doesn't use a camera and includes support for a threshold which effectively means they're treated as capsules. I think that's more along the lines of what I imagine for world unit raycasting.

body:
Really glad to see how this has come along! A few thoughts other thoughts:

1. In docs it should be very explicitly noted that the `Color.r/g/b` members are _not_ in a reliable color space. Not only because of this legacy mode support but in the future if other wider gamut color spaces are supported then it will depend on the current working color space.

2. Should functions like `Color.convertLinearToSRGB` log a deprecation warning when the legacy color path is enabled since they're inconsistent with the new color storage model?

3. I assume we'll only be changing `WebGLRenderer.outputEncoding` to `sRGBEncoding` by default once this legacy mode is disabled by default?

Overall I'm happy with what this has evolved in to. The API surface is consistent (aside from the Color.r/g/b fields) which was my original concern when improving the color spaces for the project.
author: gkjohnson

body: Saying that "Color Management" is disabled by setting `ColorManagement.enabled` equal to `false` doesn't entirely feel correct. In order for color to truly be "unmanaged" here textures need to not be transformed before being passed into the shader which is not how three.js currently works or works after this PR. I've mentioned before that what happens currently feels closer to operating in an sRGB color space but even that isn't a completely accurate depiction of what's currently happening. Three.js' current handling of color and textures is a bit of it's own thing currently. It seems best to refer to it as a "legacy" mode rather than trying to adopt the "Unmanaged Color" term and full deprecate it later. If we really want an option for unmanaged color in the future then more changes to textures will need to be made.
author: gkjohnson

author: donmccurdy

body:
I think I understand your concerns on this, but I am not sure how best to address it. If we think of this in terms of modes or workflows, there are several, and we may or may not want to support them all. Input colors and textures may come from various color spaces. In each mode ...

1. **Color Managed (NEW)**: Colors and textures are converted to the Linear-sRGB working color space during upload or processing.
2. **Non Color Managed**: Colors and textures are _not_ converted to the Linear-sRGB working color space automatically. This workflow does not exist in three.js today (textures are always converted if marked sRGB), and we may or may not want to add it.
3. **Current / "Legacy"**: Textures are converted to Linear-sRGB during upload; colors are not modified. This is the current behavior, and there are 2-3 likely reasons a user might choose it:
  - a. User is not familiar with color management, uses `outputEncoding=LinearEncoding`, and is effectively using a _gamma_ workflow. This is something I want to discourage and eventually deprecate.
  - b. User is comfortable with color management, uses `outputEncoding=sRGBEncoding`, understands color literals must be provided in Linear-sRGB, and is effectively using a _linear_ workflow.
  - c. User's project does not require lighting, and/or is compositing 2D layers with blending that does not require a linear ('scene referred') working color space. Any color space conversions (especially the additional precision required for internal Linear-sRGB render targets) is just unwanted performance overhead for this user, so they might prefer to do everything in a display-referred color space like sRGB. Sprite-based experiences like [Oat the Goat](http://oatthegoat.co.nz/) might be good examples.

I believe (3c) and (3b) are valid use cases that we should not forget. Perhaps we push these users toward (2) instead of (3), but I don't think we can expect them to be happy adopting (1). So our choice of naming (`ColorManagement.enabled` or otherwise)should be respectful of that.

Ideas:

- Push group (3b) toward option (2), deprecate (3a) and (3b), name the flag `ColorManagement.enabled`
- Don't support (2), name the flag something that invites (3b) and (3c) but discourages (3a) else, like:
    - `Color.enableTransform = false`
    - `ColorManagement.sourceColorSpace = LinearSRGBColorSpace`
    - ...
author: donmccurdy

body: Sounds good to me!
author: donmccurdy

body: Fine, but remember there may be better approaches than clamping. I would say that topic needs investigation, if you haven't done it already.
author: WestLangley

body:
For the use case @gkjohnson is describing (like an reusable exporter, or perhaps retrieving a color to display in a color picker) the advantage of providing a target color space is that it's more robust against different contexts:

1. color management might be disabled ŌĆō see 3b and 3c above; the value is already sRGB
2. working color space might (in the future) be something other than linear-srgb, like ACEScg

With an API like `color.getRGB( target, sRGBColorSpace )` an exporter can reliably get a color in the expected color space, regardless of other application state.
author: donmccurdy

author: WestLangley

body:
My point is, it should be `color.copy( source_color, fromColorSpace, toColorSpace );` The args are not changed; "this" is.

Also, how do you convert from one color space to another with out specifying both color spaces?
author: WestLangley

body:
> Also, how do you convert from one color space to another with out specifying both color spaces?

The way I'm imagining this is that values in Color are stored in the working color space (declared on ColorManagement.workingColorSpace) always and I don't think we should betray that assumption by converting to a different color space in place on a color object. Then it's never ambiguous as to what a Color is stored in and it can be read and set in different color spaces using the various API methods.

The `getRGB( target, colorSpace )` pattern also aligns with other functions like `getHSL`.
author: gkjohnson

body:
Remaining values in the encoding enum are:

- THREE.LinearEncoding
- THREE.sRGBEncoding
- THREE.BasicDepthPacking
- THREE.RGBADepthPacking

I'm not really familiar with the packing types... would they remain as encodings, or become something else? Setting that aside, removing `texture.encoding` we would have:

- `texture.colorSpace = SRGBColorSpace | LinearSRGBColorSpace`
- `renderer.outputColorSpace = SRGBColorSpace | LinearSRGBColorSpace`

I agree these are clearer and more correct names, but am not sure whether to bundle all of this into one three.js release or not.

author: gkjohnson

body:
My vote is to call the current behavior "Legacy" in some way and then in the long run implement a non color managed mode if we feel it's needed. At the least I just don't think we can use terminology that implies the current three.js behavior is the same as unmanaged color. But I do feel the "Legacy" mode should be deprecated and removed at some point.

I agree that user group "A" is should be discouraged in the long term. And I think user group "C" is really best suited by a true non color managed work flow. Regarding user group "B" --

> On further thought, users in group (3b) cannot use workflow (2), because their textures still need to be converted...

I don't think an unmanaged color workflow would be the easiest path for user group B, anyway. The only thing user group B would have to do to migrate to the new color managed mode is change any place that colors are explicitly set to use an sRGB hex _or_ explicitly pass that the color is in the Linear sRGB color space. It's a migration task but it's not a big one. Realistically I think this is the case that most users are going to fall in to given the current three.js recommendations.

And in terms of converting textures I don't think that's the case as long as the textures are already sRGB. With unmanaged color "texture.colorSpace" and "renderer.outputColorSpace" would be entirely ignored so the lighting calculations be off but the final displayed color would look reasonable as long as sRGB colors are passed into the Color constructor and textures are originally stored in sRGB color space.
author: gkjohnson

body:
We might be down to aesthetics at this point. ­¤Öé I think any of these names are correct enough, and I'm happy to go with any of them. The only downside I see to `RawData` or `NonColorData` is that they don't have the same suffix as other `*ColorSpace` enums, but that's fine with me. Compare the four "in context":

```
texture.colorSpace = null;         // (a)
texture.colorSpace = NonColorData; // (b)
texture.colorSpace = RawData;      // (c)
texture.colorSpace = NoColorSpace; // (d)
```

author: WestLangley

body: Actually, I changed it here on purpose. Yes, this did catch an error, but more importantly, there's nothing special about 256. We've actually found most of our users preferring smaller envMaps to get reasonable HDR downloads (since they're PNGs internally). 256 is required for roughness >= 0.05, while 64 is for roughness >= 0.1; I consider that to be a very worthwhile trade in performance (both GPU cache and download) for the vast majority of users.
author: elalish

@elalish what are the roughness ranges for 32, 128 and 512?

| envmap size | roughness |
| --- | --- |
| 32 | ??? |
| 64 | >= 0.1 |
| 128 | ??? |
| 256 | >= 0.05 |
| 512 | ??? |

Once we complete the table we could add it to the docs:
https://threejs.org/docs/?q=pmrem#api/en/extras/PMREMGenerator
author: mrdoob

body:
`roughness = ( 2 ^ ( -0.5 * mip ) ) / 1.16`, where dimension is `2 ^ mip`. So: 

- 16: mip 4: roughness 0.21
- 32: mip 5: roughness 0.15
- 64: mip 6: roughness 0.11
- 128: mip 7: roughness 0.076
- 256: mip 8: roughness 0.054
- 512: mip 9: roughness 0.038
- 1024: mip 10: roughness 0.027

body:
I think this is equivalent (`gl.getExtension()` returns `null` when not available):

```js
const MultisampledRenderToTextureExtension = extensions.get( 'WEBGL_multisampled_render_to_texture' );
```
author: mrdoob

body: Yes, `get()` will return `null` if the extension is not available. However, there is actually a small difference. `get()` will log a warning when an extension is not available which can be quite confusing for users. Hence, it's better to always check if an extension is available via `has()` and only then request it via `get()`.
author: Mugen87

body:
IMO, it's best when `PMREMGenerator` uses `ShaderMaterial`. All changes to the renderer can then be reverted.

The idea of excluding `RawShaderMaterial` from the GLSL 3.0 conversion process was to give the user full control over the GLSL. Meaning devs write GLSL 3 OR GLSL 1 code. But the material never supports both at the same time. I don't think we need to change this policy for fixing `PMREMGenerator`.

`nodeType` should preserve the value defined by the user here `null == auto` in serialization, we have `getNodeType()` to define the dynamic type. 


> I do not see why uniform.value = 1; uniform.value = new Vector3() pattern could be ever used.

`UniformNode` is a transporter, it must adapt. It's not time to get the real `nodeType` in `set/get`, we're confusing stages here.

see `Object3DNode` it's set a `.value` per frame, `set/get` is not the most optimized way to deal with it, I think we need to fix this before merger.

body: It would be nice to document what the relationship is between attenuationColor and diffuseColor /  transmission here. Why are they different?
author: gkjohnson

body:
Because diffuse and attenuation color are part of different models and represent different things.

The diffuse color (the `color` material property in `three.js`) represents more or less an object's color. Or in other words it is the color you see when the object's surface is illuminated by pure white light.

`attenuationColor` is part of the BeerŌĆōLambert law. Monochromatic white light travels through the material and is changed during this process. `attenuationColor` defines the color that the white light is turned into after a certain distance.

I'm afraid this change is not right. If a render target is of type `SRGB8_ALPHA8`, WebGL automatically holds framebuffer data as sRGB. 

The line above only controls the use case when a _manual_ sRGB encode is required. That is only necessary when rendering to screen OR when using a render target in context of XR.

I can understand that we want to write the version detection code short but `navigator.userAgent.match( /Firefox\/([0-9]+)\./ )?.[ 1 ] < 98 === false` might be a little bit tricky especially for first readers? They may think why it isn't `navigator.userAgent.match( /Firefox\/([0-9]+)\./ )?.[ 1 ] >= 98`.

I know this detection code works fine because `navigator.userAgent.match( /Firefox\/([0-9]+)\./ )?.[ 1 ]` will be `undefined` on non-Firefox and `undefined < 98` will be `false`. But it took time a bit for me to understand.

(Sorry if my overthinking.)

And not a big preference, but personally I like adding a comment about likely when we can remove the version detection code https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23906#issuecomment-1101405508

By the way I didn't know that numbers and strings can be compared in JavaScript.

body: Be advised that image bitmaps are not automatically GC-collected when they are no longer referenced, and they require special handling during the disposal process.
author: WestLangley

body:
If you use an *ImageBitmap* as the texture's data source, you have to call [link:https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/ImageBitmap/close ImageBitmap.close]() at the application level to dispose of all CPU-side resources.

An automated call of *ImageBitmap.close()* in [page:Texture.dispose]() is not possible, since the image bitmap becomes unusable, and the engine has no way of knowing if the image bitmap is used elsewhere.
author: WestLangley

Yes I was trying to think of what to call it. All of the compute* functions modify the geometry in place while this one must generate a new geometry since `toNonIndexed` is called which generates a new geometry to work with.

I'm seeing that all the merge* and to* functions generate a new geometry as a result, though. How about something like `toCreasedVertexNormalGeometry` or `toCreasedNormalGeometry`?

author: donmccurdy

body: Oh I see - I didn't realize that. I don't really love the ambiguity of sometimes returning a new geometry and sometimes not. It seems most clear to consistently return one or the other imo but I'm not sure how strong my opinions are here - maybe others have thoughts? @mrdoob @Mugen87?
author: gkjohnson

body:
Ideally, the `compute*()` functions in `BufferGeometryUtils` should indeed modify the geometry in place and return a new instance. In this way, they behave similar to the methods of `BufferGeometry` (like `computeBoundingBox()`, `computeBoundingSphere()` or `computeVertexNormals()`). 

`to*()` methods should return _something new_. A new instance or a reference to a target object provided as a parameter.
author: Mugen87
donmccurdy

body:
> None of our computeFoo methods return a new geometry

Oh I see. I'm happy to change it to that - it just seems a bit odd considering geometry buffer lengths are more or less considered immutable now. If a geometry has been rendered and then one of these functions has been called on an index geometry then I believe `dispose` should be called on it before rendering again, no?
author: gkjohnson

Hm I'm not sure about that. @Mugen87 what do you think? 

If that's the case then perhaps these methods should be `toCreasedNormals( angle )` and `toMikkTSpaceTangents( invert )` instead.

body:
The renderer assumes that buffer attributes do not change in size after their first usage.

If a change in size is necessary, `BufferGeometry.dispose()` has to be called and a new geometry created.

Ok, how about this naming convention for methods returning a new geometry?

- toNonIndexed()
- toMikkTSpaceTangents( MikkTSpace, negateSign = true )

A few ideas for this new method:

- toCreasedNormals( creaseAngle = Math.PI / 3 )
- toSplitNormals( creaseAngle = Math.PI / 3 )
- toFaceNormals( creaseAngle = Infinity )
- toSmoothNormals( creaseAngle = 0 )

I get much better search results for "split normals" than "creased normals", so that might be my vote.

"Creased Normals" sounds a lot more descriptive and intuitive to me.

"Split" normals sounds and (from the blender results) looks like a geometry operation that results in this kind of creased look but it's not something I would think to look for without already knowing it existed. It definitely feels like a name that only really makes sense if you understand the algorithm or geometry restrictions in place that necessitate splitting vertices up in order to get this look.

Other than that the name change for the tangents function makes sense to me.

body:
`blitFramebuffer` cannot copy multiple color attachments (only the first to all of the other), so I created new FBOs to copy them individually in my original demo (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23300#issuecomment-1100666026).

Temporary FBOs aren't strictly needed here since three already has multi-sampled/regular FBOs handy, but we'd need to unbind/rebind their respective color attachments before/after the blit.

body:
Do you the place where exactly I can report it? I tried asking in webxr discord, but never got an answer.

I totally understand reluctance to insert a workaround for specific device in lib code, but it seems to work ok and doesn't seem to break anything, at least from a glance, otherwise I wouldn't even bother. That being said, there's no pressure from my side.

> AFAICS, getPose(inputSource.gripSpace) would be called twice in any event when no hand tracking is active.

Sorry, but I don't see how. There's 2 calls to `getPose` in `update`, first is for target ray `inputPose = frame.getPose( inputSource.targetRaySpace, referenceSpace );`, second is for grip: `gripPose = frame.getPose( inputSource.gripSpace, referenceSpace );`. I just swapped places them so the target ray can use gripPose instead.

I don't really understand how expensive this call can be, so maybe calling it twice is an option (especially that it will only be called if targetPose is null). If you think this is the way please let me know, I'll change the code

This sounds like an error in the runtime, it really should be reporting a separate target ray pose. That said, this doesn't seem like a terrible compromise for cases when the runtime is simply broken. Hopefully that doesn't happen too often.

Not critical, but if you wanted to get fancy you'd probably want to pitch the inputPose down about 20deg or so from the grip pose to make it a little more natural to point with. (Otherwise it feels like you're trying to point at stuff with a sword.)

body: If it's an error that's only showing up on a specific device or with a certain runtime then most likely the "correct" people to fix it is the vendor, but you can file an issue with Chromium regardless to ensure that we haven't over-specialized our OpenXR implementation to known runtimes rather than the spec.

body:
I'm having a difficult time understanding this method ŌĆō a few questions:

- Why `uids.set( true, this.uid ++ );` and `uids.set( false, this.uid ++ );`, What do those lines do?
- Since we don't seem to use the morph target object for anything more than a boolean check, could we replace it with a boolean? Possibly `isRelativeCopy` or something like that. 

Note that if the attribute were used both for the base geometry and a morph target, and the morph target were _relative_, we wouldn't need to copy it or do any of this. That's very unlikely (how could the same attribute be used for both relative and absolute data?) and so I'm not really worried about duplicate data in that case, but just to say that it's not the fact that the attribute "hasMorphTargets" that we actually care about here, it's the fact that we cloned the original and rewrote it as relative deltas.

Now that three.js supports relative morph targets, we could even put this in an external utility function.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Thanks for the feedback! Your suggestions make the code more straightforward. 

> Why uids.set( true, this.uid ++ ); and uids.set( false, this.uid ++ );, What do those lines do?

The idea is to maintain a set with two IDs per attribute. The first ID represents the original attribute, the second one the modified. AFAICS, not having this distinction is the root cause of #24074.

> Since we don't seem to use the morph target object for anything more than a boolean check, could we replace it with a boolean? Possibly isRelativeCopy or something like that

Yes, a boolean makes more sense. Added `isRelativeCopy`.

body:
­¤Äē Thanks for opening this PR @hybridherbst! We'd likely want to wait on merging anything until the extension is ratified, or nearly so. It's helpful to see how this might look for discussion in the meantime.

The features required by KHR_animation_pointer are pretty complex to support (nothing wrong with this PR, it is just a very complex extension in practice) and so I'll flag a few likely issues in comments below.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Supporting all properties may be a non-goal for three.js I think. Especially as animated extension properties come into the mix. We can try to support whichever properties seem helpful to three.js users, and are not overly complex to include.

No need to change these in the draft PR now, but a few things to flag:

- alphaCutoff animation can trigger shader recompilation 
- we have very limited support for UV transforms, all textures except aoMap must share the same transform. I wonder if we should avoid animating those properties until that is improved.
- metallicFactor seems unlikely to be animated much?
author: donmccurdy

body: I think animation is required to target TRS properties, not a matrix?
author: donmccurdy

body:
Similar to above, will have to call `camera.updateProjectionMatrix()` after any of these changes.

I'm not sure what I think about animation of the aspect ratio. glTF lacks any meaningful way to express "let the camera inherit the aspect ratio from the application", animating this property kind of seems like stacking blocks on top of something that is already not working properly.
author: donmccurdy

body:
One of the larger hurdles on this extension will be that glTF definitions do not translate 1:1 into three.js objects ŌĆö

- Materials will be cloned depending on the context in which they're used. The same glTF material applied to (1) points, (2) lines, (3) mesh with vertex colors, (4) mesh with vertex normals, (5) mesh with vertex tangents, will result in 5x copies of the material. 
- Lights and Cameras will be cloned if they're attached at >1 Node.
- Probably similar for KHR_audio if that gets ratified?
author: donmccurdy

body: The trouble with targeting animation to the UUID is that many users will clone the model before attaching one or more copies to their scene. The cloned objects will have new UUIDs, and so the same animation clips cannot target them. We prefer to target based on unique names for that reason, but glTF doesn't require unique names. And in this case (see above) we may need to animate multiple objects, even if only a single source glTF definition was animated. ­¤śĢ
author: donmccurdy

It is simple why it is useful to have another PRNG: for example Three.js scene consist with some shadowmap, in one implementation you create 2 textures, that's why UUID generator invoked 6 times `Math.random()` Ōä¢1, Ōä¢2 ... Ōä¢6, also scene in examples using `Math.random()` for generating random colors and position, seed is equal to Ōä¢7 and Ōä¢8. If later in core you changing amount of used textures, 3 for example because you using 1x1 texture first for some reason or changing somehow algorithm, you invoking `Math.random()` 9 times Ōä¢1 ... Ōä¢9, and later in example random gives another values (!), that is why pictures in examples are changing without good reason for this. body: Why not just `if ( drawCount < 0 )`? I think infinite `drawCount` is allowed?
author: LeviPesin

body:
I don't think here it is, since this value is basically passed directly to `gl.drawArrays`/`gl.drawElements`. See:

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGLRenderingContext/drawArrays
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGLRenderingContext/drawElements

Basically, this is the part of the code that tries to deduce `drawCount` if it was left unspecified (i.e. `Infinity`). The original code assumes that either `index` or a `position` attribute will be available, and uses these to bound the `drawCount`. This pull request aims to also make it possible to render geometries with neither `index` nor a `position` attribute, therefore the check for infinity becomes necessary.
author: filonik

body: I just did a quick test, and somewhat to my surprise, passing `Infinity` doesn't seem to cause any issues (aside from nothing being rendered, not surprisingly). However, I am still not sure if it is a good idea though. Is it specified somewhere that an infinite count is valid for `gl.drawArrays`/`gl.drawElements`? If nothing ends up being rendered when the count is `Infinity`, then we might as well bail out early...
author: filonik

body: Basically, my reasoning is that if the `drawCount` is still `Infinity` at this point, it means that it wasn't set explicitly in `drawRange` or `group`, or implicitly by `index` or a `position` attribute. In that case, I would consider the geometry to be empty/incomplete.
author: filonik

body:
That reasoning makes sense to me. Still, could you write the check like so:
```js

```

body:
I can remove the check for `Infinity`, but it won't fix the problem in #19430, since `_maxInstanceCount` will still be `undefined`. The problem does not come from the `drawCount === Infinity` check, but from this:

```
isNaN( Math.min( geometry.instanceCount, undefined ) )
```
author: filonik

body:
I am not sure I understand the use case for an `InstancedBufferGeometry` where the final `drawCount` ends up being  `Infinity`. (Note that I am specifically talking about the `drawCount` that will be passed to `drawArraysInstanced`.)

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGL2RenderingContext/drawArraysInstanced

Even when you are rendering multiple instances, you need to somehow specify what range of vertices you want to render multiple instances of. The correct way of handling an `InstancedBufferGeometry` without attributes like in #19430 is to specify the number of vertices (`drawRange.count`) and the number of instances (`instanceCount`) as illustrated in the example. Only specifying either one of both is not enough.

Maybe there's some other use case that I am not seeing...
author: filonik
Mugen87

body:
Yes, this reflects my understanding of things. Initializing `drawStart`/`drawEnd` as you suggest makes sense. I suppose in this instance I was being overly defensive, trying to account for the possibility of `drawRange` being set to `null`. If this can never happen your approach makes more sense, I will adopt it.

Otherwise, I have high confidence that the current code is correct, and that it is a simplification/improvement over the existing code. The only possible alternative that I see for checking `_maxInstanceCount` against `undefined` would be to always initialize it to `Infinity` in `setupVertexAttributes()`. However, I am not sure if that would really be an improvement...

body:
I don't think this line of code is right. `Material.prototype.copy.call( pointsMaterial, material );` only executes the `copy()` method of the `Material` class whereas `pointsMaterial.copy( material );` executes `PointsMaterial.copy()`. However, this will lead to `undefined` material properties if the given material is not of type `PointsMaterial`. 

So using `Material.prototype.copy()` was done on purpose. /cc @donmccurdy

body:
[GLSL specification](https://www.khronos.org/registry/OpenGL/specs/es/3.2/GLSL_ES_Specification_3.20.pdf) (p.120) states

> The function main is used as the entry point to a shader executable. A shader need not contain a
> function named main, but one shader in a set of shaders linked together to form a single shader
> executable must, or a link-time error results. This function takes no arguments, returns no value,
> and must be declared as type void:
> void main ()
> {
>     ...
> }
> The function main can contain uses of return. See ŌĆ£JumpsŌĆØ for more details.
> It is a compile-time or link-time error to declare or define a function main with any other
> parameters or return type.

So I think matching `void main()` should be practically safe.

body: Does it makes sense to have event listeners for `pointerdown` _and_ `click`? Isn't that redundant?
author: Mugen87

body:
Maybe like so?
```js
'select': 'pointerdown',
```
author: Mugen87

body:
They're different.

`click` is the event of `mousedown` followed shortly by `mouseup` (delay configured on os level) with minimal delta in cursor position (not sure, but in some implementations it's as long as you don't leave the clicked element).

You usually use `click` for UI interactions, and `mousedown`/`pointerdown` for video games or drag and drop interactions.

body:
> Setting NoBlending and premultipliedAlpha: true and transparent: true at the same time?

My point is: Since the shader implementation assumes premultiplied alpha, I would suggest to always set `premultipliedAlpha` to `true` for clarity.

Whether or not all combinations of `transparent` and `blending` make sense is something I would tackle in a different issue/PR.
author: Mugen87

body:
>Since the shader implementation assumes premultiplied alpha

The drawing buffer has premultiplied alpha, but the output of the shader may or may not have it.

You only need to set `material.premultipliedAlpha` if it is required to get the correct answer. Setting it when it is not needed just causes more confusion.

`.premultipliedAlpha` is a flag, just like `transparent`, to guide the renderer in setting the correct blending function. It only applies to the built-in blending functions.

`.premultipliedAlpha`  means the output of the _shader_ has RGB multiplied by alpha. In that case, it triggers the renderer to select a blending function _different_ from the typical one.

`.premultipliedAlpha` does not apply when `NoBlending` is set.

To understand these concepts, I'd start with understanding the `setBlending()` function in `WebGLState.js`.
author: WestLangley

body:
I'm aware how `premultipliedAlpha` is used in `WebGLState`. I'm just the opinion that less errors occur if `premultipliedAlpha` is always set to `true` for this particular case. Especially since nothing breaks even when no alpha blending is used.

In other words: We should not expect that users enable `premultipliedAlpha` if they blend the result of a copy pass. `webgl_postprocessing_backgrounds` shows that even examples don't do this right.

I would add an `intensity` attribute, not a `color` attribute.
```js
if ( intensity.length > 0 ) geometry.setAttribute( 'intensity', new Float32BufferAttribute( intensity, 1 ) );
```
I also would not scale it by 255. It likely is already in the desired units.
author: WestLangley

body:
> I would add an intensity attribute, not a color attribute.

An `intensity` attribute can't be used for vertex colors. It's important that user don't have to write custom shaders for this use case.

> I also would not scale it by 255.

The color values have to be in the range [0,1] when using a float color attribute.
author: Mugen87

body: The app can then do what it wants with the loaded data.
author: WestLangley

body:
You are assuming the intensity is used as a replacement for color information. It is a value returned from LiDAR.

Just load the data.
author: WestLangley

body:
It seems to be the common usage to treat intensity as color information (from https://github.com/isl-org/Open3D/issues/125):

> If you use point cloud with intensity, just copy the intensity value to all the rgb fields
author: Mugen87

body:
Also from https://www.mathworks.com/help/vision/ref/pcread.html

> Intensity property, stores the grayscale intensity for each point.
author: Mugen87

body:
LiDAR data is a special-use-case for PCD file format. PCD file format supports optional fields.

Intensity is an optional field, and I think intensity for LiDAR is nominally normalized to 1 or 100 -- not to 255 -- because it is not a color.

I do think this could be a great example -- just hardwiring it into the loader as a color doesn't seem right to me.

JMHO, though. :-)
author: WestLangley

body:
> because it is not a color.

Do you have a resource where this is documented? While researching the topic, I've only found that `intensity` is more a less an alternative to a RGB definition (XYZRGB vs XYZI). This also explains its range between 0 and 255.
author: Mugen87

body:
No, that is my point. It is neither documented nor standardized as far as I was able to determine.

I found [What is lidar intensity data?](https://desktop.arcgis.com/en/arcmap/10.7/manage-data/las-dataset/what-is-intensity-data-.htm) of interest. It is clear that the LiDAR data must be post-processed for it to be used as PCD data.

If you insist, maybe a note in the loader documentation that `XYZI` format requires intensity to be integer-valued and normalized to [0, 255].
author: WestLangley

body:
It's a good idea to add the supported PCD fields to the documentation. I'm fine with the suggested range however all files I have tested with represent the intensity as float values. The first data line of a respective PCD looks like so:
```
# .PCD v0.7 - Point Cloud Data file format
VERSION 0.7
FIELDS x y z intensity
SIZE 4 4 4 4
TYPE F F F F
COUNT 1 1 1 1
WIDTH 7816
HEIGHT 1
VIEWPOINT 0 0 0 1 0 0 0
POINTS 7816
DATA ascii
-0.096134186 -408.10934 13.66958 5.8790812
```
The files actually define `F` (and not `I`) as the data type of `intensity`.

I think I would not add any data type restriction to the docs. The range of intensity and the fact that the loader interprets the data as a grayscale color value should be sufficient.
author: Mugen87

body:
Oh, I meant whole-number, not integer format. Forget I mentioned that topic. :-)

The intensity is a float. I still think it is most reasonable to assume the value is in [0, 100] -- not [0, 255]. That is what is bugging me...
author: WestLangley

body:
I've changed my mind after reading this thread: https://answers.ros.org/question/232118/rviz-point-cloud-intensity-channel-how-does-it-relate-to-point-cloud-xyzi/

Relevant part:

> Intensity values don't have a unit and are device-dependent; that means the actual minimum and maximum depends on the device that produced them, and you cannot directly compare intensities from different sensor models. The only convention is that "higher value" means "more intense".

That means there is no reliable way to scale the intensity to [0,1]. It's just not right to rely on [0, 255] or [0, 100]. 

Let's continue the discussion in #24253.

body:
I think the API that aligns most with existing three.js patterns is to set a singular ies texture on a spot light like so:

```js
spotLight.iesProfileMap = await new IESLoader().loadAsync( './path/to/profile.ies' );
```

This is similar to the projection `map` field here on the spotlight object - which I didn't realize had been added previously:

https://threejs.org/docs/?q=spotlight#api/en/lights/SpotLight.map

My concern is that the [spotlight logic for handling maps and shadows](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/4c74ddc15b6a9eae758c0bc9c55c59fcf71d6464/src/renderers/shaders/ShaderChunk/lights_fragment_begin.glsl.js#L98-L110) is becoming increasingly complex. I'm wondering if there's a better way to handle conditional use of textures across multiple spotlights. An array texture would work but would require more complex updates to the renderer to perform efficiently. Perhaps @Mugen87 or @mrdoob  have some thoughts?
author: gkjohnson

body:
Thanks very much for that. 

Apologies, I had lost track of our earlier discussion. I think the constant index via `#define`s is a great workaround to avoid compatibility issues.

The proposal of

```js
spotLight.iesProfileMap = await new IESLoader().loadAsync( './path/to/profile.ies' );
```

seems good, much simpler.

Under the hood we can map IES profile paths to the profile index, which for 1D IES profiles is just an index into the horizontal or vertical line of the profiles texture. This also double as a caching mechanism to avoid ever loading and parsing IES profiles multiple times.

I'll do another pass over the code to implement the above and await feedback from @Mugen87 and @mrdoob on the [spotlight logic for handling maps and shadows](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/4c74ddc15b6a9eae758c0bc9c55c59fcf71d6464/src/renderers/shaders/ShaderChunk/lights_fragment_begin.glsl.js#L98-L110) issue.

body: Why is it necessary to call `activeTexture()` here when `bindTextureToSlot()` was executed right before?
author: Mugen87

body: It's possible that the version was bumped for an already bound texture.  If the texture was already bound to a slot,  BindTextureToSlot exits without calling activeTexture.  Fortunately activeTexture on the three.js side already caches which slot is active, so if we've bound the texture and then drop into here, we simply check the cache and exit out without duplicating the gl call.

author: Mugen87

body:
I think passing the entire array into normalize/denormalize as a huge 'type' argument was never ideal as a public MathUtils API... if this went back into MathUtils at some point, I think it is better to use types like IntType / ShortType. But since this is just internal, I suppose it does not matter so much. Any preference between:

- (A) limit visibility of the property, e.g. `__type` or `#type`
- (B) continue passing the array 

/cc @WestLangley
author: donmccurdy

body: I prefer B for now until there's a practical reason to change it. It makes it hard to see what the other meaningful changes are in this PR, as well
author: gkjohnson

body: TBH, I prefer any form of (A). Passing the array is horrible, IMO.
author: WestLangley

body:
imo passing a three.js enum can result in unnecessarily verbose user code with no value. If something like "A" is used I'd prefer to pass in something like the array type / constructor: `normalize( value, Int8Array )`.

A lot of the code I've been working with has been written to generally support array types within three. For example packing buffers into textures for three-gpu-pathtracer, generating data structures in three-mesh-bvh, etc. A lot of the code in three.js isn't designed to support these types of use cases which is fine but it would be nice to keep it in mind when adding new functionality to the system - these use cases have been the driver for #24515.

body:
It seems that "nextOffset" is set to the end of the final group, correct, and not the end of the full geometry? So the geometry will effectively be trimmed only on the end?

It might be a corner case but I don't think that's what I would expect. If geometry unreferenced by groups is going to be culled it should be done consistently (ie at the beginning and in the middle of the group ranges, as well). I think I'd expect all the geometry (vertices, indices, etc) to be merged the same way always with the groups being the only thing that's handled differently.
author: gkjohnson

body:
Agreed. That would be unexpected.

I've pushed an additional change which addresses this issue. At this point, I've considered these cases; am I missing any?
- Contiguous groups
- Incrementing materialIndex
- Non-incrementing materialIndex
- Overlapping group start/ends
- Gaps between group start/ends
- Groups that don't span the entire position attribute
- Groups that don't start at 0

Here are cases that I'm intentionally not dealing with. I expect these are "invalid" groups:
- group.start < 0
- group.count < 0
- group.materialIndex < 0
- Groups where `group.start + group.count >= count` (Group reaches past the end of the geometry's vertices)

body:
There is one issue with this implementation. Since there is now an explicit `update()` method, you potentially overwrite the `position` buffer attribute over and over again. This is not right. Instead of overwriting the attribute, it's more correct to just update the existing values.

Depending on the Octree, the resulting attribute might change in size so updating is not always possible. So you have to call `dispose()` on the geometry and create a new one with the new position attribute.
author: Mugen87

body:
Thank you, understood.
I wonder, is there a way to query the amount of WebGLBuffers, to verify that my fix (once done) does not still create a leak?
author: erasta

body:
No, you can only check the number of geometries but not the individual buffers. 

Update: Since `WebGLAttributes` uses a weak map, there should not be a memory leak. However, it is still better to not just overwrite the buffer and hope for the GC but explicitly free resources via `dispose()`.
author: Mugen87

body: If would simplify this and remove the check. When an Octree changes, it's usually a structural change resulting in a different number of vertices.

body: Just for my understanding: How does this change affect the non-VR usage of multisampled render targets? Is it still possible to save the contents of a multisampled render target as sRGB? Or are the data now always linear color space?
author: Mugen87

body:
Without this change, the multisampled renderbuffer would be allocated as sRGB while the texture is in RGB.
This will not only result in incorrect rendering (since you now render to sRGB), the blitframebuffer call will also fail because the color spaces don't match.
So, if this pass was broken and this change shouldn't affect content that was still working.

Let's follow the order of the DataArrayTexture constructor here...

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/d081c5a3501d272d19375fab1b01fedf9df29b22/src/textures/DataArrayTexture.js#L6

```javascript
constructor( mipmaps, width, height, depth ) { ... }
```

body: This is testing if the columns are orthogonal. Is this function reliable in practice given there will be round off?
author: WestLangley

body: It will give false positives, probably only will return false if the matrix is identity. The only solution I think of is testing against a very small angle.
author: yomboprime

body:
In the case of a false-positive it will cause the compute-intensive branch which, nevertheless, gives the correct results for full circles and ellipses.

In practice, for a pure rotation it does not give false-positives since rotation matrices have their conjugate-diagonal set to `sin ╬Ė -sin ╬Ė`.

The only case to worry about is a long chain of nested group matrix multiplications. If we should handle it, I can update the PR to test against some Epsilon, not zero. Is it a good idea?
author: nkrkv

body:
I think any arbitrary rotation can seem as skewed currently.

It is not elegant to introduce deltas but I guess the skewing can be detected with a big angle (1 or 2 degrees) as it is a very specific use case only for the ellipse.

- 'background': used by the renderer for `scene.background` (flat only)

body:
Would it be reasonable to estimate plane types using a `bounded-floor` reference space, and (at least for now) omit plane type detection in other reference spaces? I'm hoping that could be cheaper and simpler, as each plane can be simply compared against the reference space, without needing to iterate all vertices of all other planes. This also avoids possible race conditions for when a plane is detected, and its type estimated, relative to other planes appearing.

That said, I would like to try to support plane detection in other reference spaces too, eventually.

Given that plane dimensions are in meters, we could also perhaps make a reasonable estimate based on size and orientation, without necessarily comparing planes against one another.

If there's no clear approach that's both robust and fast, this might also be something we want to put into a utility class (`examples/jsm/webxr/XRPlaneUtils.js`?) to leave more room for exploring other approaches.

author: Mugen87

body:
I read through the glTF spec

https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html

If I understand correctly, to match the spec the multiple skinned meshes referring to the same skin (skeleton) shouldn't animate independently because

* Multiple nodes can refer to the same skin (skeleton)
* A skin consists of joint (bone) nodes which must be in a scene graph
* Key frame skinning animation is achieved by animating joint nodes

So, reusing the same skeleton instance without cloning matches the spec, I think?

body:
How about this: Instead of `material.map?.image`, let's keep `material.map !== null`. When the exporter later tries to access the image data, it reports a warning if no image data are defined and adds a default texture instead. This can be done by returning an empty 1x1 canvas in `imageToCanvas()`.

Trying to serialize a texture with no image data is definitely wrong and something that should be reported as a warning.
author: Mugen87

body:
The idea of using default image data is to be able do export _something_. Alternatively, we have to throw an exception and abort the export entirely.

Please keep in mind that the 3D object is malformed to begin with when it has textures with empty image data. The app should not allow to export such assets in the first place.

body:
Yes, I think you are right. Good catch, thanks.

If I'm right, `node` here can form three patterns for this `nodeDef` except for `nodeDef.children`.

1. `SkinnedMesh` (single primitive mesh)
2. `Group` - `SkinnedMesh` (multiple primitives mesh or single primitive mesh + other type objects eg. camera)
3. `Group` - `Group` - `SkinnedMesh` (multiple primitives mesh + other type objects eg. camera)

Flattening `Group` - `Group` to single `Group` for case 3 at https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/42c59b4d516fd204f5f760e1f7daefbbbf18c49c/examples/jsm/loaders/GLTFLoader.js#L3922-L3951 ? If we do, we can 

```
const children = node.isGroup ? node.children : [ node ];
for (let i = 0; i < children.length; i++) {
  ...
}
```
author: takahirox

body: Agreed, thanks!
author: donmccurdy

body: If we flatten the hierarchy further it'll break tools like https://github.com/pmndrs/gltfjsx that rely on predictable output structure. We _could_ make changes like that but I think we'd want to plan it well ahead. Lately I've been thinking it'd be better if we never auto-flattened the hierarchy and just output `Group - Group - Mesh` even if there are no multi-primitive meshes or other attached objects. Mostly to keep things simpler and more predictable.

body: Maybe we can use pinning for the actions (i.e. using the commit hash instead of the version)? Otherwise this can potentially leave the repository open to a supply chain attack in actions, with which the repository's secrets could be stealed, (not sure if there are any enabled in three.js).
author: LeviPesin

body:
If you trust the provider of the Action (in this case: GitHub) and its ability to put measures in place to prevent malicious take-overs, then it's fine to pin to a `@v2` tag. But if you want to be super secure, then you are indeed very welcome to [pin to the Action's commit SHA](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-guides/security-hardening-for-github-actions#using-third-party-actions). You could then even [use Dependabot to help keep your Action SHAs up to date!](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/dependabot/working-with-dependabot/keeping-your-actions-up-to-date-with-dependabot).

I'll leave it up to the three.js team to decide what to do here. Maintainers should feel free to edit the contents of the PR :slightly_smiling_face:

body: shouldn't this go under [`on.push.paths-ignore`](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions#onpushpull_requestpull_request_targetpathspaths-ignore) of the `codeql-code-scanning.yml` action?
author: marcofugaro

body:
This particular setting tells the CodeQL analysis knows which files not to analyse. This means that regardless of when the analysis runs (e.g. on a PR, or on a push, or on schedule), CodeQL won't bother analysing these files or flagging up any alerts for these paths. I've based that on [the existing `.lgtm.yml` configuration file in the repository](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/5368e6a206eba8afd93760a4b8c815e13a43c257/.lgtm.yml).

The [on.push.paths-ignore](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions#onpushpull_requestpull_request_targetpathspaths-ignore) is a setting that stops an Action (like the CodeQL analysis) from being triggered if changes to a _particular_ path are _pushed_. It can be useful to apply that in a CodeQL context, but it would mean that the CodeQL analysis would still flag up alerts when it is triggered by, for example, a PR being opened.
author: sj

body:
Without this line, you'll get an [extraordinarily large number of alerts](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25056/checks?check_run_id=9844725516) in the `manual/3rdparty/monaco-editor` subtree. The CodeQL analysis in LGTM automatically excluded minified files ([example](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/manual/3rdparty/monaco-editor/min/vs/basic-languages/markdown/markdown.js)), but it doesn't do that by default on GitHub.

But of course it's up to you! If you're interested in seeing these alerts and fixing them, then that's great :rocket:

> Can be optimized more with WEBGL_multi_draw.

To me this is a big question ... doesn't WEBGL_multi_draw constrain the number of objects per batch quite a bit? Which could still be the better choice, but probably means a larger number of smaller batches, ideally co-located for culling, and more complex batch management...

I suspect it'll take us some time to figure that out, so I'm inclined to do our experimentation in addons and not rush this into core right away.
author: donmccurdy

body:
I think maxGeometryCount and maxVertexCount are probably two parameters that users really need to provide an intentional value for. We don't give a default count for InstancedMesh either. I'd be fine with material or maxIndexCount maybe having default values though. How about:

```js
constructor( maxGeometryCount, maxVertexCount, maxIndexCount = maxVertexCount * 2, material = new MeshBasicMaterial() );
```
author: donmccurdy

body:
Earlier proposals had included a matrix argument here, like:

```javascript
addGeometry( geometry, worldMatrix )
```

Is there a reason you prefer to skip that? Or is it just something we'd wait and consider later?

I think most users will need to apply a matrix, and doing that internally might help cut down boilerplate. E.g. adding the same tree in several locations and scales, users will need to make two copies of the geometry and keep resetting and overwriting the second, it's a bit awkward.
author: donmccurdy

body:
just an idea ŌĆö it might be nice to have some getters...

- `.geometryCount`
- `.vertexCount`
- `.indexCount`

... so users can see how much space is left in the batch. The second two values would presumably change after removing a geometry and then calling `.optimize()`.
author: donmccurdy

body: nit: I think `removeGeometry` or `deleteGeometry` would be more consistent with other three.js APIs.

body: I like `.optimize()`. Or `.rebuild()` would also work.

body: In general ŌĆö I think it's OK (and possibly better) for these methods to hit runtime errors naturally on invalid input; we don't typically sanitize it, and I silently doing nothing may cause developers some headaches.

body:
I'm not sure this is the right fix for the problem. 

I wonder if it wouldn't be better to solve this issue on `Object3D` level. Meaning when a 3D object is added or removed to/from the scene graph, descendants receive an event as well since they are indirectly affected by this operation.
author: Mugen87

body:
I believe having this particular event logic in the `CSS2DObject` level provides a unique solution for objects that also contain HTML DOM references.

`CSS2DObject` types do not have descendants, so it makes sense to require event listeners to their parent rather than assigning events externally.
author: doppl3r

body:
> CSS2DObject types do not have descendants

Um, I don't think this assumptions is correct.
author: Mugen87

body:
You are right, my assumption was not true. I tried nesting a `CSS2DObject` within another `CSS2DObject` and they render HTML on top of each other (not pretty, but still optional). The solution I provided in this PR still work when ancestors invoke `clear()` or `removeFromParent()`.

Do you have any pseudo code in mind for adding events to descendants within the `Object3D` class?

body:
Can you please explain why the following part of the regex has been changed?
```
[link:([\w\:\/\.\-\_\(\)\?\#\=\!\~]+)
```
author: Mugen87

body:
Honestly not 100%, was a trial and error thing with some combination of reading up:
```
Note that some characters like :, -, @, etc. neither have a special meaning when escaped nor when unescaped. Escape sequences like \:, \-, \@ will be equivalent to their literal, unescaped character equivalents in regular expressions. However, in regular expressions with the unicode flag, these will cause an invalid identity escape error. This is done to ensure backward compatibility with existing code that uses new escape sequences like \p or \k.
```

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Regular_Expressions/Cheatsheet

I believe the current code is correct, and is already monomorphic.  The tree of children beneath an `Object3DMatrixData` exclusively consists of further `Object3DMatrixData` instances.

See:
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/6c9205fefdca0003c1b222bd501799d53b1837c3/src/core/Object3D.js#L525
author: diarmidmackenzie

`onBeforeMatrixUpdate()` and `onAfterMatrixUpdate()` are included in the Object3D prototype, so will always exist for any Object3D sub-class.

Agreed, in the case where `updateMatrixWorld()` is called directly on e.g. a SkinnedMesh, this will be invoked 3 times.

This is an extremely rare case, because `updateMatrixWorld()` is usually only invoked on the whole scene, rather than a single object.
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/14138#issuecomment-392210879

In any case, will update comment from "twice" to "multiple times" so that it is not inaccurate.

>Target vector _must be initialized_ with the vertex coordinates prior to the transformation

I think this is a red flag that the API needs to be revisited.
author: WestLangley

body: @Mugen87 Is the current API acceptable to you?
author: WestLangley

body:
Looking at how the method is used, the current implementation does make sense.

I suspect you want that `SkinnedMesh.boneTransform()` initializes the target vector by itself based on the given vertex index. The problem is that just reading the position buffer attribute isn't enough. A vertex might already be transformed like in `Mesh.getVertexPosition()`. So the bone transformation is something that happens on top.
author: Mugen87

body:
The transform can be applied to _any_ vector. Right?

I'd feel better if the method was named `SkinnedMesh.applyBoneTransform( index, vector )`.

Then it is obvious that the `vector` to be transformed must be initialized.

Previously, we have used `target` to refer to an uninitialized parameter.
author: WestLangley

body:
> The transform can be applied to any vector. Right?

The method makes the assumption that `index` and `target` are coupled. So `target` must represent a vertex of the geometry since the same vertex index is used to extract the respective skin indices and weights.

The issue is that the vertex might already be transformed by morphed target or another vertex displacement technique.

> I'd feel better if the method was named SkinnedMesh.applyBoneTransform( index, vector ).

I'm find with that. We probably want to use `vertex` instead of `vector`, see my previous comment.
author: Mugen87

body:
>The method makes the assumption that index and target are coupled. So target must represent a vertex of the geometry...

I do not think that is true. Any point can be transformed. (The point doesn't even have to be near the respective vertex, although it typically is.)

>The issue is that the vertex might already be transformed by morphed target or another vertex displacement technique.

In fact, that is what we allow.

I'll file a PR that changes the API to `SkinnedMesh.applyBoneTransform( index, vector );` -- if you agree.
author: WestLangley

body: Sounds good to me!

body: Does it make sense that `.y` should get special treatment regardless of the Euler order?
author: WestLangley

body:
Yep, `.y` is easier to calculate in the sense that it is always well-defined, for a very simple reason: In Proper symmetric Euler angles, asymmetric Tait-Bryan angles or even generalized Davenport angles, the second axis is **always orthogonal** to the other two (meaning that there is no `ZZY` order, for example).

`.x` and `.z`, on the other hand, are not always well-defined, depending on the value of `.y`, because the third axis can be found by rotating the first axis by a rotation around the second axis.

For Tait-Bryan / asymmetric sequences: 
- When `.y = -pi/2`, the first axis is aligned with the third axis, the system becomes underdetermined and only `.x + .z` is well-defined.
- When `.y = +pi/2`, the first axis is aligned with the inverse of the third axis, the system becomes underdetermined and only `.x - .z` is well-defined.
- In every other case, both `.x + .z` and `.x - .z` are well-defined.

In the article you can see, for example, that one of the formulas for `.y` is
```math
\theta_2 = 2 \operatorname{atan}_2\left(\sqrt{c^2 + d^2}, \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\right)
 ```
Which, for normalized quaternions, is always well-defined ( $\|q\|^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + d^2 = 1$ assures that if $a^2 + b^2$ is zero, then $c^2 + d^2$ is not).
author: evbernardes

body: `.y` is not the "second axis" in three.js. The ordering is specified by `Euler.order`.
author: WestLangley

body:
Ok so, maybe I misunderstood the definition here. I thought `.x`, `.y` and `.z` were just attributes for the first, second and third angles in the `Euler` instance (at least that is what would make more sense to me for a generalized approach with symmetric and asymmetric sequences).

In this case, for example, in the order `YXZ`, `.y` would be a rotation around the `X` axis.

Do you mean to say that, for example, the value in `.y` is always a rotation around the axis `Y`? (That would probably explain why symmetric and asymmetric sequences are not implemented together in three.js).
author: evbernardes

body:
>.y is always a rotation around the axis Y?

yes.

body:
The method is downloading is taken from https://www.chromium.org/getting-involved/download-chromium/. I assume that is the Canary builds.
From https://www.chromium.org/getting-involved/dev-channel/:
> The release channels for chrome range from the most stable and tested (Stable channel) to completely untested and likely least stable (Canary channel). You can run all channels alongside all others, as they do not share profiles with one another. This allows you to play with our latest code, while still keeping a tested version of Chrome around.

> * Stable channel: This channel has gotten the full testing and blessing of the Chrome test team, and is the best bet to avoid crashes and other issues. It's updated roughly every two-three weeks for minor releases, and every 6 weeks for major releases.
> * Beta channel: If you are interested in seeing what's next, with minimal risk, Beta channel is the place to be. It's updated every week roughly, with major updates coming every six weeks, more than a month before the Stable channel will get them.
> * Dev channel: If you want to see what's happening quickly, then you want the Dev channel. The Dev channel gets updated once or twice weekly, and it shows what we're working on right now. There's no lag between major versions, whatever code we've got, you will get. While this build does get tested, it is still subject to bugs, as we want people to see what's new as soon as possible.
> * **Canary build: Canary builds are the bleeding edge. Released daily, this build has not been tested or used, it's released as soon as it's built.**
> * Other builds: If you're extra brave, you can download the latest working (and that's a very loose definition of working) build from [download-chromium.appspot.com](https://download-chromium.appspot.com/). You can also look for a more specific recent build by going to [the Chromium continuous build waterfall](http://build.chromium.org/), looking at the number near the top under "LKGR", and then going to [this Google Storage bucket](http://commondatastorage.googleapis.com/chromium-browser-continuous/index.html) and downloading the corresponding build.
Note: Early access releases (Canary builds and Dev and Beta channels) will be only partly translated into languages other than English. Text related to new features may not get translated into all languages until the feature is released in the Stable channel.

I couldn't find a way to download e.g. the latest build from the dev channel. The commit numbers taken from OmahaProxy very often don't work.

> The release channels for chrome range from the most stable and tested (Stable channel) to completely untested and likely least stable (Canary channel). You can run all channels alongside all others, as they do not share profiles with one another. This allows you to play with our latest code, while still keeping a tested version of Chrome around.

> * Stable channel: This channel has gotten the full testing and blessing of the Chrome test team, and is the best bet to avoid crashes and other issues. It's updated roughly every two-three weeks for minor releases, and every 6 weeks for major releases.
> * Beta channel: If you are interested in seeing what's next, with minimal risk, Beta channel is the place to be. It's updated every week roughly, with major updates coming every six weeks, more than a month before the Stable channel will get them.
> * Dev channel: If you want to see what's happening quickly, then you want the Dev channel. The Dev channel gets updated once or twice weekly, and it shows what we're working on right now. There's no lag between major versions, whatever code we've got, you will get. While this build does get tested, it is still subject to bugs, as we want people to see what's new as soon as possible.
> * Canary build: Canary builds are the bleeding edge. Released daily, this build has not been tested or used, it's released as soon as it's built.
> * Other builds: If you're extra brave, you can download the latest working (and that's a very loose definition of working) build from [download-chromium.appspot.com](https://download-chromium.appspot.com/). You can also look for a more specific recent build by going to [the Chromium continuous build waterfall](http://build.chromium.org/), looking at the number near the top under "LKGR", and then going to [this Google Storage bucket](http://commondatastorage.googleapis.com/chromium-browser-continuous/index.html) and downloading the corresponding build.
Note: Early access releases (Canary builds and Dev and Beta channels) will be only partly translated into languages other than English. Text related to new features may not get translated into all languages until the feature is released in the Stable channel.
author: LeviPesin

body:
This change will produce a memory leak since the old normal buffer attribute is not properly disposed of. Once an attribute has been set, it's not correct to overwrite it. It's necessary to call `dispose()` on the geometry and create a new instance of `BufferGeometry`.

Besides, a length check is also not ideal since buffer attributes are not supposed to change in size. They are supposed to be fixed-size.

body: I'm afraid this change isn't right. An entry in the index buffer attribute represents a single index which has an item size of `1` (not `3` like a position or normal attribute).

body:
Hey, exporting as plain object breaks tree-shaking, we can't have that.

Having `export * as` is fine as it gets transpiled by rollup in the build step.
author: marcofugaro

body:
That's not the case.

Let's look into that.  There are a number of objects of this style.  This new code is what the old import statement gets trans-piled to.  I have a tree-shake confirmation project published.  Will link shortly.

This is being done in 2 parts.  https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/25185

There are mutually exclusive requirements and people have some confusion.  Let's write it out.
author: epreston

body:
Update: https://github.com/epreston/three-treeshake-coverage 

Please pretend you don't see the other bits.  Need an hour to cleanup my changes and commit them.
author: epreston

body:
Long story short:

> Having `export * as` is fine as it gets transpiled by rollup in the build step.

Yes, it becomes something which is very similar to the new code, because that statement is shorthand for "make an object for me".  Then it gets distributed and the final bundler (client application) which does the shake.  If we want to improve the shake, we have API changes.  If we want to retain the API, distribute nearly the same bundle, and enforce 2018, we change the code.

Currently the tools are configured for 2020.  Setting the tools to 2018 and enforcing it is the first step so we actually improve the tree-shake instead of masking it.
author: epreston

body:
As a confirmation take a look at what we ship now using the 2020 syntax.  I'm going to use the middle size one to reduce the reading.

The old 2020 line:

```js
export * as AnimationUtils from './animation/AnimationUtils.js';
```

Is currently being translated to:

```js
var AnimationUtils = /*#__PURE__*/Object.freeze({
	__proto__: null,
	arraySlice: arraySlice,
	convertArray: convertArray,
	flattenJSON: flattenJSON,
	getKeyframeOrder: getKeyframeOrder,
	isTypedArray: isTypedArray,
	makeClipAdditive: makeClipAdditive,
	sortedArray: sortedArray,
	subclip: subclip
});
```

This has been updated to:

```js
const AnimationUtils = {
	arraySlice: arraySlice,
	convertArray: convertArray,
	isTypedArray: isTypedArray,
	getKeyframeOrder: getKeyframeOrder,
	sortedArray: sortedArray,
	flattenJSON: flattenJSON,
	subclip: subclip,
	makeClipAdditive: makeClipAdditive
};
```

It gets translated to:

```js
const AnimationUtils = {
	arraySlice: arraySlice,
	convertArray: convertArray,
	isTypedArray: isTypedArray,
	getKeyframeOrder: getKeyframeOrder,
	sortedArray: sortedArray,
	flattenJSON: flattenJSON,
	subclip: subclip,
	makeClipAdditive: makeClipAdditive
};
```

Keeping it clean, the final bundler works its magics.  This is what I hope to demonstrate in the confirmation project.  Its possible we get slightly better tree-shake using the 2018 syntaxes.

In the old code, final bundler can only remove the whole object if its entirely unused, which will be created in any case (new or old code).  Tools can remove constants if they are entirely unused, so we have even ground.
author: epreston

body: I'm writing this all out because you are right, except if we use this particular 2020 shorthand syntax, which doesn't help the cause.
author: epreston

body:
> If we want to improve the shake, we have API changes.

Actually you're correct, the object in the final bundle doesn't get tree-shaken because it's an object, and budnlers can't tree-shake that. The only way to make it tree-shakable would be to do `export * from './animation/AnimationUtils.js';`, but I'm not sure maintainers want to go with this breaking change.

> except if we use this particular 2020 shorthand syntax, which doesn't help the cause.

There is no problem with this syntax, as it doesn't end up in production. What #25185 is all about is Optional chaining and Nullish coalescing (and of course class fields from ES2022).

I guess we can enforce ES2020 and disable just Optional chaining and Nullish coalescing.

The solution is also posted there -- I will file a PR soon.
author: LeviPesin

body:
> I don't think anyone wants to disable "Optional Chaining" that would drop a massive amount of code from the library and make things very robust. Seems like half of most classes are just checking for null / undefined.

By [Optional chaning](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Optional_chaining) I mean `?.` and currently it's only used on `GLTFExporter.js`.
Nullish coalescing is not used anywhere in three.js.

> I see this as the best solution, because as mentioned here [#25185 (comment)](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/25185#issuecomment-1422059604), the import/exports are not included in the enforcing.

Which solution ?  There's a few more bits to consider to enforce 2018, without setting the tools to enforce, but add extra config to "sort of" enforce 2018.  We can work towards it and we'll get there.

body: ES2020 + `no-nullish-coalescing` and `no-optional-chaining` would be the best solution.
author: marcofugaro

body:
The project you linked is for things that can't be polyfilled and trans-piled.  Keep in mind, Babel isn't in the toolchain.  So we don't have full polyfill and trans-pile.  Your idea has changed my mind about how much effort the next step will take.   I think it is awesome and we actually completely agree if you read what I wrote yesterday.

People keep confusing what is in the bundle vs what code is maintained.  It really is productive to have a separate discussion on the code we read and share.  Then, have another conversation about what is included in the bundle how that is presented.

At the moment, this project doesn't bundle to a language standard but a browser support standard.  The language features used look like a rough mixture of es6 which changes every quarter.  Talking about the bundle in terms of language features makes little sense when its defined in terms of browser support.

The issues I see coming up will be about perceptions, assumptions, and confusion.  The project has a standard, but it deviates from the standard, but something will fix it, then finally realising that is not what shipped.

I want to make people happy so they can produce the exact same output and remove 20 lines from the project.  If that needs to be a birds nest of tooling config, so be it.  We can make it so people can use a few 2020 features in a context of 2018.  Not keen on doing the work then having to remediate what has crept in.  Its easier to test and rationalise about with a stable base going forward.
author: epreston

body:

Would it not be better use just vars instead of `prop: var`?

```js
const MathUtils = {
	DEG2RAD,
```

author: sunag

body: It's exactly the same thing and gets translated to the code referenced.  This style allows the internal names to be changed (many modules import one or two directly bypassing this), while ensuring the documented API is always the same.
author: epreston

body: Last bit, when it comes to Terser and minification, these interface objects reduce the bundle size.  When the public API is used exclusively, the function names themselves can be condensed into a 2 character symbol, and used that way internally.  This marks the only spot where that 2 character replacement needs to appear in its full form.
author: epreston

body: I think it's cleaner than replicating the same names all times. For this specific case, I don't see the need to use it `prop:prop` instead of `prop`, or do we have some compatibility issue here?
author: sunag

body:
Minification issue, sorry, I wrote that out just as you were responding.  There's a reason many other objects in the library follow that pattern.

The alternative is if any function names change, you have to introduce this format to maintain the API, it will be created anyway if something is inline-d.  These functions are fairly common so I'm just trying to avoid creating foot-guns.
author: epreston

body:
> The alternative is if any function names change, you have to introduce this format to maintain the API, it will be created anyway if something is inline-d.┬Ā

`{ prop1, prop2, ... }` format is also used in other places throught the library.

body:
What's the goal of this feedback ?

> But why we would change a function name? 

API's and function names changing with 10 version support for old names seems to be the norm.  So much so, there's a file dedicated to it with new updates every release, `Three.legacy.js`.  

> { prop1, prop2, ... } format is also used in other places through-out the library.

I didn't write that other code.  I hope the tradeoffs and reasons were well considered.  Plenty of places where things are not as frequently imported / exported where it does not matter because it's not API.
