body: When we enable physically correct lighting, we should probably ensure the scene itself has a realistic scale and lights (specifically point and spot lights) have proper distances to the objects they lit.
author: Mugen87

body:
Values in the 0.5 ŌĆō 2 range, which we're frequently using in examples now, are not particularly helpful with `useLegacyLights = false`. That's comparable to a candle and (with appropriate exposure) might very dimly illuminate a small room. I might refer to that as a "unitless" workflow, because we're effectively trying to keep light values near 1, disabling attenuation, ignoring exposure, ignoring light units, and trusting that irradiance will generally stay close to the [0,1] range under those conditions.

Hundreds or thousands of candela would be more appropriate for real-world light sources. See [Blender's table of light powers](https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/latest/render/lights/light_object.html#power-of-lights) (approximations), and note that 12.57 lumen = 1 candela, assuming a point light. The conversion is different for other types.

Practically though, this is just a morphing blob, I don't know that we need or want to try to figure out "realistic scale"... we have about 200 examples to update, so I would prefer to keep the changes narrower for now. ­¤ść
author: donmccurdy

body: /cc @WestLangley does this sound correct, and are you OK with starting here?
author: donmccurdy

body:
My point is when I would implement the example from scratch with physically correct lighting in mind, I would not position the point lights that far away. I'm aware that candela has a totally different value range than our previous intensity values but even 50000 seems quite high considering the scene and its lighting. 

But yeah, I understand that updating 200 examples is a lot and reconsider distances from lights to their lit objects makes the migration more complex. 

I'm just thinking if we have to touch the examples in any event to make them physically more correct, I would reconsider scale, light distances, too,  and eventually fix #7636.
author: Mugen87

body:
> I'm just thinking if we have to touch the examples in any event to make them physically more correct, I would reconsider scale, light distances, too, and eventually fix https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/7636.

Although I'm not saying this has to be done all in once. I'm okay with changing the value of `useLegacyLights` and then just update the intensity so the example looks as before. With subsequent PRs, the scene can further be optimized like mentioned above.
author: Mugen87

body:
Remember, three.js is unit-less. Or, to say it another way, the user can assume any units.

If the user wants to use the SI light intensity units defined in the docs, then the user must use _meters_ for distance (1 unit equals 1 meter).

There is a difference between physically-based light _behavior_ (quadratic falloff) and physical light _units_.

What I did so far was to follow more the pattern of `sheen` and `specular`, since those seemed to be more similar to this extension in terms of the small amount of code added. body: During this week's PBR TSG it was noted that all other anisotropy systems we studied (Disney, MaterialX, Adobe ASM, Google Filament, Dassault Enterprise PBR) all agree that the bitangent roughness is lower and tangent roughness is higher, so setting alphaB to a greater value here is opposite the convention.  Sadly, there is no consistency among any of these systems as to what the exact formula is, and indeed this PR introduces a new anisotropic strength formula here.  I've attempted to [chart the different strength formulas](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1EMjwJOrSy5XfR3Vzug_M0w4CRhTcslawwXiYo1yOEb0/edit#slide=id.g2125a0cb896_0_0).

author: LeviPesin

body:
We can use this transition in our advantage:

* `WebGLRenderer` would be something like Three.js v1.0
  * Deprecated eventually
* `WebGPURenderer` would be something like Three.js v2.0
  * Not fully backwards compatible
  * Node based
  * WebGL fallback

I doubt we'll be able to support `ShaderMaterial` in `WebGPURenderer`...

Neither I know yet how `WebGPURenderer` imports should be.

Maybe `import { WebGPURenderer } from 'three-webgpu'`?
author: mrdoob

body:
> I doubt we'll be able to support┬ĀShaderMaterial┬Āin┬ĀWebGPURenderer...

In a new WebGLRenderer we wouldn't need ShaderMaterial, because everything would be possible to do with the Nodes system.
We should have WebGPURenderer and not-backwards-compatible WebGLRenderer, I agree with that -- but they should be two different and equal renderers, not one being a fallback for other.
author: LeviPesin

body:
So what @sunag was suggesting was to have something like:

```
                 - WebGPUBackend
WebGPURenderer -| 
                 - WebGLBackend
```

We could rename `WebGPURenderer` to just `Renderer` but I think it will be easier for devs to understand that `WebGLRenderer` is the current ecosystem and `WebGPURenderer` is the next ecosystem.

And that `WebGPURenderer` will have an internal WebGL backend/fallback to ensure maximum support.

Similar to how `WebGLRenderer` internally uses WebGL 2.0 by default and WebGL 1.0 as fallback.

The developer shouldn't have to know about these details.
author: mrdoob

body:
> Similar to how┬ĀWebGLRenderer┬Āinternally uses WebGL 2.0 by default and WebGL 1.0 as fallback.

This is possible because WebGL 2 is backwards-compatible with WebGL 1, so 'WebGL2Renderer" is compatible with "WebGL1Renderer".
It's better to let the developer to choose between WebGL and WebGPU renderers rather than hiding the implementation from them.

body:
@elalish It looks like there is an issue with this particular line of code. Users report about assets that throw a shader error now:
```
Program Info Log: Fragment shader is not compiled.


FRAGMENT

ERROR: 0:1384: 'vNormalMapUv' : undeclared identifier
ERROR: 0:1384: 'getTangentFrame' : no matching overloaded function found
ERROR: 0:1384: '=' : dimension mismatch
ERROR: 0:1384: '=' : cannot convert from 'const mediump float' to 'highp 3X3 matrix of float'


  1379: #endif
  1380: #if defined( USE_NORMALMAP_TANGENTSPACE ) || defined( USE_CLEARCOAT_NORMALMAP ) || defined( USE_ANISOTROPY )
  1381: 	#ifdef USE_TANGENT
  1382: 		mat3 tbn = mat3( normalize( vTangent ), normalize( vBitangent ), normal );
  1383: 	#else
> 1384: 		mat3 tbn = getTangentFrame( - vViewPosition, normal, vNormalMapUv );
  1385: 	#endif
  1386: 	#if defined( DOUBLE_SIDED ) && ! defined( FLAT_SHADED )
  1387: 		tbn[0] *= faceDirection;
  1388: 		tbn[1] *= faceDirection;
  1389: 	#endif
  1390: #endif
```
It seems not right to use `USE_CLEARCOAT_NORMALMAP` and `USE_ANISOTROPY ` at this point since `vNormalMapUv` is _only_ defined when a normal map is present (meaning when `USE_NORMALMAP` is defined).

What was the reason for adding both defines to this check?

An asset for reproduction is available here: https://discourse.threejs.org/t/shader-error-0-validate-status-false/53195
author: Mugen87

body: Ah, I may have missed that case. I did add [this](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25580/files#diff-fc94afb5da943fea9e01a4a16adf82b5433aa6d61af5855f296158a98ed4dcfdR1615) to ensure the tangents were defined, but perhaps a similar thing is necessary for the UVs? Anisotropy requires UV coordinates (and ideally tangents too), even if no normal map is present. I think it's perfectly reasonable to throw an error if anisotropy is requested without any UV channels being present (that's in the glTF extension spec too).

author: marcofugaro

body:
The difference is subtle, boring, but important.  Here's a summary of what I'm pointing out.  

The first report: bundle size.

You want to report on module bundle size and impacts that would be shipped.  People can plainly see its 1.1 MB looking at the built file.  Adding a terser step to minify this to be able to report 600 KB will confuse people.  Reporting like this doesn't match up to any shipped file, it does not indicate the build size of any use case, it's an ESM build which shouldn't be minified but has, the current minimised file is based on UMD.

The final bundler is responsible for minification for good reasons.  Chains of minification produce worse results on the final product, etc.  It is very helpful when you introduce a new feature that adds 20 KB then are able to point out that it will not impact build sizes with the report that follows.  We want to see that 20 KB here, not see it after tree-shake.

The second report: bundle size after tree shaking

This should combine the module build and module test file (not the source based test file) in a build, minify, tree-shake.  Everything a real application consumer would do, then report on that.  That looks like what it is still doing after this PR.

We don't want to minify the module bundle, then build it with the module test rig, minify again, and tree-shake.  The minification from the first transformation can hide minifications from the final pass.  We want it to all happen in a specific context of the test rig to get results that match what will happen in when we use it in our applications.  

This PR doesn't modify the test rigs so its not creating any issues.  The module test rig is still pointing to the build/three.module.js file because it's using the 'import' statement.  I'm writing this out for clarity.

What I wanted to also point out is the current treeshake test rig has a few issues.  It is too minimal to be practical.  It's in a state that answers a very specific question from whoever was working on it last.  It is not setup to be a general indication.  I think this report and PR will have the most value with better data.
author: epreston

body: Module does not get the terser mini-fication, the final result (test script and what it consumes gets minified together).
author: epreston

body: This would be just three.module.js. You can report on it from the build folder so you don't need to take a copy.  The test scripts are pointing to the build folder so this will not get used.
author: epreston

body: There's a good reason ESM module bundle is not shipped minified.  You want to report on bundle size, yes.  Not bundle size of a build type that should not ship.  You can report report off the built module in build because that is what is shipped, changes size, and what the one of the test scripts points to.  The other test script points directly to the source.
author: epreston

body: You have the right idea, use module, just don't minify with terser.
author: epreston

body:
Tools usually report the minified size and that's what I did here. "Bundle" implicitly means minified.
I think this is what @mrdoob meant when he asked for the bundle size to be reported.

author: marcofugaro

body: Minimised size of what ?  This is not what is minimised in the build.  There's a UMD minimised, but you don't do that with ESM (in the same way) because it defeats the purpose.  A report on the build size would be very helpful.
author: epreston

body: Tools report the minimised size when minimizing has some meaning.  Applying minification to a file that can never ship minimised in this way is not helpful.
author: epreston

body:
> you don't do that with ESM (in the same way) because it defeats the purpose

Why? ESM file can be minified in the same way as an UMD file.
author: LeviPesin

body: One solution is to find a different home for this file.  It's not used by the tree-shaking test rig.
author: epreston

body: No. It can be optimised slightly in the packaging process but not as aggressively without losing the benefits of using ESM.
author: epreston

body: Tools usually report a minified size of the minified size.  They don't create a random build type, minimise it, and report on it.  When tools like vite report on the ESM bundle size for a library, they are not reporting on the minimised size.  They only report minimised sizes when the target is an application and minification is applied.
author: epreston

body:
> No. It can be optimised slightly in the packaging process but not as aggressively without losing the benefits of using ESM.

The only thing that couldn't be minimised in ESM file are imports and exports which are negligible for a large library.
author: LeviPesin

body:
That is true of the final bundler doing the minification sure, anything is possible.  What you are pointing out makes tree-shaking very difficult and sub optimal.  It works but with bad outcomes.

Seriously, I'm getting the feeling you will take any position opposite to me with little thought.  Please accept my apology if I have created this situation.  My goal is only to help and collaborate.
author: epreston

body:
That's where the confusion is coming from:

> Tools usually report the minified size and that's what I did here. "Bundle" implicitly means minified.

Bundle does not implicitly mean minified in an ESM bundle.  This is explicitly not minified.  The three.js own build, or any other tool, does not report on the minified size of an ESM module build for a library project.  That happens in applications consuming libraries.
author: epreston

body: Reporting a minified, compressed size is accepted practice in JavaScript packages, because that's the number that matters to any consumer who cares about their bundle size. Let's close out that portion of the discussion and resolve this thread. I'll address the "random build type" bit in another comment.

body: This is exactly what allows to simplify -- `( - m ) % n` almost always equals `n - ( m % n )`, except when `m % n` is 0 -- then it is also 0. So alignment calculation simplifies from something like `r = m % n; return r === 0 ? m : m + n - r` to just `m + ( - m ) % n`. I suggest we turn it in a function like `MathUtils.align` if it's too hard to read.

body: Given an InterleavedBufferAttribute we'll need to use its associated [`.stride`](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=Interleaved#api/en/core/InterleavedBuffer.stride) for WebGPU's `arrayStride` property, not just infer stride from the element size. If we have a normal `BufferAttribute`, then I think layouts like `uint16x3` are just not allowed. GLTFLoader should create InterleavedBufferAttribute automatically here.
author: donmccurdy

body: Deeper improvements to the interleaved vertex layout have been suggested ŌĆö https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/17089. Those would be helpful for optimization purposes, but aren't necessary to get things working in WebGPU.
author: donmccurdy

body: The `.stride` is for the interleaved object, not for this specific field so I'm not sure how that's useful here. For example if the vertex is `16x3 position + 16x3 normal + 32x4 boneWeights + 1x4 boneIndices` then when we're here for `position` then `.stride = 16`
author: greggman

body:
> ... when we're here for position then .stride = 16

Not necessarily ŌĆö stride is entirely dependent on the vertex layout chosen by the author. We can't infer a 16x3 position attribute's stride without reading the interleaved buffer object. Position may be interleaved with normals, or interleaved with vertex data from another mesh, or only "interleaved" with its own padding. Each case would give a different stride for an otherwise identical InterleavedBufferAttribute.
author: donmccurdy

body:
We know it's 16x3 as the type is "`vec3`", and the arraybuffer is `Int16Array` or `Uint16Array`. We also know that WebGPU doesn't support 16x3 so it really needs to be 16x4. If that fails then it wasn't compatible with WebGPU in the first place. 

The actual stride in bytes from the gLTF file is already correct.
author: greggman

body:
I don't know if I'm making sense.

My point is

1. We know the code is asking for "vec3" of 16bit values
2. We know WebGPU doesn't support 3x16
3. We don't know at this level if changing it 4x16 is valid. There is no data at this level that will let us derive if it's valid to change 3x16 to 4x16 or not.
4. We do know that not changing it from 3x16 to 4x16 is incompatible with WebGPU. So, we just change it and cross our fingers.

I'm not sure what info you could add that would solve point 3 above. For example "vec3" of 16bit values with a stride of 8bytes does not tell you that it's valid to change this from 3x16 to 4x16 because the interleaved format might be 

```
struct {
  normal: vec3<int16>
  something: int8
  somethingElse: uint8
}
```
author: greggman

body: Agreed on all of that, and selecting 4x16. But to fully support KHR_mesh_quantization or arbitrary interleaved data we also need to give WebGPU the right "arrayStride", which the line of code above does not do. It could be 8 bytes, 16 bytes, or even 1024 bytes ... we can only get that information from buffer.stride.
author: donmccurdy

body:
The arrayStride, when from KHR_mesh_quantization is already correct given the spec requires it to be a multiple of 4.  

If it's from a user created InterleavedBufferAttribute(sp?) (not from gLTF) we could print an error `arrayStride must be multiple of 4`
author: greggman

body:
This line of code, above, is incorrect:

```js
let arrayStride = geometryAttribute.itemSize * bytesPerElement;
```

In addition to being a multiple of four, it must also match the actual stride in the actual data, which this line does not check. Additional padding may be present. It should be reading `buffer.stride` (which is already a multiple of four), not just computing the smallest convenient multiple of four.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Right: So the reason it's working for both of your files is that even though you passed `--vertex-layout separate` for one of the files, at least when it gets the that part of the code, it takes the `if` path

```
  arrayStride = geometryAttribute.data.stride * bytesPerElement;
```

for 2 of 5 attributes
author: greggman

body: In anycase, I wasn't planning on pursuing this PR. I was just sharing it because it worked for your two files and thought it might be useful info for whoever decides how to deal with this issue in the three.js WebGPURenderer
author: greggman

body: Ok, yes ŌĆö let's call this resolved, thanks! I'll just leave a note in the main thread that `buffer.stride` should probably be considered in the final PR.

I have invested considerable time trying to determine what is working, what bugs are preexisting, and what bugs are introduced in this PR.

Everything was working as intended in r125. (2 years ago).

1. First bug.

>When a normal map is present, the three.js convention, adhered to years ago, is for the back face to render the same, whether side is BackSide or DoubleSide.

In this PR, a back face normal map is rendered incorrectly when `material.side = BackSide` and tangents are present.

I believe this bug was introduced in #22146, as mentioned in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25580#issuecomment-1464746778.

2. Second bug.

>The shader is not compiling for me if flatShading is true and tangents and a normal map are present.

I believe this is a preexisting bug, too.

https://github.com/cx20/gltf-test/tree/master/tutorialModels/NormalTangentTest is one testbed. You can remove the normal attribute in the loader callback. Or you can just set `flatShading = true`.

body:
The PR looks great so far! The only thing that I have noticed is that some node-specific logic/semantic finds its way into the renderer.

The original idea was to make the renderer material-agnostic (as good as possible) so it does not rely on a specific material system. But now there are things like `getEnvironmentNode()` or `getFogNode()` (so node syntax) inside renderer classes.

Would it be possible to reduce the node related logic inside `WebGPURenderer` to a minimum? 

The idea would be to define more general interfaces in the renderer (e.g. `getEnvironment()`) and then inject a material specific object (e.g. the `nodes` object that we already used elsewhere) that provides the implementation. In our case it is node specific but in theory it could be something different, too.

The overall design goal is to avoid a too tight coupling of the renderer to the node material.
author: Mugen87

body:
I think it ideally should be something like this:
WebGLRenderer/WebGPURenderer/SoftwareRenderer/another renderer (doesn't know about nodes, takes shaders written in GLSL/WGSL/JS) -- NodeRenderer (a boilerplate "renderer" that knows how to work with nodes) and WebGLNodeBuilder/WebGPUNodeBuilder/JSNodeBuilder (converts node material to GLSL/WGSL/JS shader) -- NodeMaterial system -- Nodes system.
So here `getEnvironmentNode` and `getFogNode` could go exactly in the NodeRenderer.
author: LeviPesin

body:
Current goals are these:

1. `WebGPURenderer` should be able to replace `WebGLRenderer` (except `ShaderMaterial`).
2. `WebGPURenderer` should have a `WebGPUBackend` and a `WebGLBackend`.
3. This summer, the `<model-viewer>` project has to be able to replace `WebGLRenderer` with `WebGPURenderer`  easily so it can use WebGPU when available.
4. After replacing the renderer, `<model-viewer>`'s file size should stay relatively the same.

We should be careful with any additional work on the nodes system that gets in the way of achieving these goals.
author: mrdoob

body: Why we need WebGL backend for _WebGPU_ renderer? Why we can't make (almost) completely interchangeable WebGLRenderer and WebGPURenderer?
author: LeviPesin

body:
Because we can't ask developers to start using two renderers in their projects and do the feature detection themselves. Resulting in 2x bigger build sizes too.

The original goal of the nodes code was to solve the tree-shaking problem of the `WebGLRenderer` by replacing the non tree-shakeable `ShaderChunks` with the tree-shakeable `NodeMaterial`.

The fact that `NodeMaterial` is able to produce GLSL and WGSL makes it much easier to have the renderer support both back-ends..

And developers should not have to know any of these details.

It seems to me like the best plan is to say that... as long as their project doesn't use `ShaderMaterial`... we can promise them that they can start using `WebGPURenderer` in their projects if they want to get WebGPU performance benefits when available, yet the renderer will take care of the backwards compatibility for them when not available.

So, the community's developer experience I'm aiming for is this:

1. Same scene graph API.
2. The only thing that changes is the renderer.
3. Their projects will automatically support WebGL and WebGPU.
4. If `NodeMaterial` is able to keep its promise, their bundle sizes should be smaller.

We may need to create a separate npm package though... `three-webgpu` or something.

However, depending on WebGPU support, we could then remove the `WebGLBackend` at some point in the future.

Just how `WebGLRenderer` now uses WebGL 2 by default yet it is still able to use WebGL 1 when not available to make the developers life easier (instead of making them use a `WebGLRenderer` and a `WebGL2Renderer`).
author: mrdoob

body:
> Because we can't ask developers to start using two renderers in their projects and do the feature detection themselves.

It's one line of code. `const renderer = WebGPU.isAvailable() ? new WebGPURenderer() : new WebGLRenderer()`. We already make developers to do various things by hand, e.g. auto-changing aspect ratio when resizing -- why this can't be one more thing?

> Resulting in 2x bigger build sizes too.

Wouldn't WebGL and WebGPU backends result in almost the same increase? Also, a very significant part of the build is renderer-independent (like animations) or shader-related (which could be repalced by nodes and then shared by both renderers).

> And developers should not have to know any of these details.

Developers **should** know if their application uses WebGL or WebGPU. The application shouldn't automatically try to use WebGL if told to use WebGPU and vice versa.

It's more about naming -- I just say that we shouldn't go with WebGLBackend and WebGPUBackend of WebGPURenderer, we should rather stick to WebGLRenderer and WebGPURenderer.
Even creating something like TheRenderer that would have WebGL and WebGPU as backends would be less confusing.
author: LeviPesin

body: The WebGL to WebGPU transition strategy I've just described is the strategy I feel I can take responsibility for and has the least negative impact in the community and ecosystem.
author: mrdoob

body: I think having a universal renderer can help in many ways,one of the difficulties I have in porting NodeMaterial to WebGLRenderer is because it wasn't exactly developed for that purpose, so it still contains many hacks, and many features that we see today in WebGPURenderer we just don't see in WebGLRenderer because the implementation is very difficult without modifying the core, and would probably impact legacy projects.  I fell TSL will help reduce duplicate code. Currently, I'm working on backward compatible  and stabilizing the WebGPURenderer, but if we don't have a universal renderer like `THREE.Renderer()` it would be difficult to guarantee the compatibility of new features of WebGPURenderer like LightNode API ( IBL's and CustomLights ), NodeMaterial Particles, Skinning Instacing for example to WebGLRenderer.
author: sunag

body: I think this is exactly what I meant by NodeRenderer [here](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25750#discussion_r1155761766).
author: LeviPesin

body:
@LeviPesin Sorry, I didn't have time yesterday to address your questions.

> It's one line of code. const renderer = WebGPU.isAvailable() ? new WebGPURenderer() : new WebGLRenderer().

That's not just one line of code...

That line of code explodes the amount of reasons why something may not work in some random device/browser configurations. And everyone, especially us maintainers, will have to pay the consequences (debugging other people's code) until we burn out.

The more predictable we make the code the better. And remember, not everyone is as smart as you are.

> We already make developers to do various things by hand, e.g. auto-changing aspect ratio when resizing -- why this can't be one more thing?

Justifying making things harder because it's already hard is not how I design APIs... I try to make things simpler.

>> Resulting in 2x bigger build sizes too.
>
> Wouldn't WebGL and WebGPU backends result in almost the same increase? Also, a very significant part of the build is renderer-independent (like animations) or shader-related (which could be repalced by nodes and then shared by both renderers).

If `NodeMaterial` has been designed well, only shaders that the developer's project use will be included when producing a bundle. That's something we can't fix anymore with `WebGLRenderer` but `WebGPURenderer` gives us a chance to make it right for the next generation.

> Developers should know if their application uses WebGL or WebGPU. The application shouldn't automatically try to use WebGL if told to use WebGPU and vice versa.

That's fine. We can add a way to force a back-end.

But you're thinking about advanced developers, which may just be 10% of Three.js users. 90% of Three.js users have never written a shader.

> Even creating something like TheRenderer that would have WebGL and WebGPU as backends would be less confusing.

If we name it `THREE.Renderer` and in the future there's a new API, say `WebRaytracing`... then we will be forced to bloat `THREE.Renderer`.

`WebGPU` gives us a chance to clean up the scene graph API a bit. We do not have to make `WebGPURenderer` fully compatible, but we do have to make a `WebGL`  back-end for it, otherwise no one will use it because "it has not reached 99% support yet".
author: mrdoob
mrdoob

body:
> That line of code explodes the amount of reasons why something may not work in some random device/browser configurations.

A `const renderer = new WebGPURenderer()`, if WebGPURenderer will try to autodetect and use WebGL or WebGPU backend, will explode no less.

> If┬ĀNodeMaterial┬Āhas been designed well, only shaders that the developer's project use will be included when producing a bundle. That's something we can't fix anymore with┬ĀWebGLRenderer┬Ābut┬ĀWebGPURenderer┬Āgives us a chance to make it right for the next generation.

I mean that bundling something like WebGLNodeRenderer and WebGPURenderer would be almost the same file size-wise as bundling WebGPURenderer with WebGLBackend and WebGPUBackend.

> But you're thinking about advanced developers, which may just be 10% of Three.js users. 90% of Three.js users have never written a shader.

I don't think it's too advanced if a developer just wants to create WebGL-exclusive or WebGPU-exclusive program.

> If we name it┬ĀTHREE.Renderer┬Āand in the future there's a new API, say┬ĀWebRaytracing... then we will be forced to bloat┬ĀTHREE.Renderer.

> WebGPU┬Āgives us a chance to clean up the scene graph API a bit. We do not have to make┬ĀWebGPURenderer┬Āfully compatible, but we do have to make a┬ĀWebGL┬Āback-end for it, otherwise no one will use it because "it has not reached 99% support yet".

This is again a reason why separate renderers are better. If in future we will have, for example, WebRaytracingRenderer that tries to use WebRaytracing or WebGPU and WebGPURenderer that tries to use WebGPU or WebGL... It would be a terrible mess.

Feature detection via `WebGPU.isAvailable()` isn't too hard even for a non-advanced developer to do. At least the developer would understand then why their WebGL application breaks in WebGPU or vice versa.
author: LeviPesin
sunag

Perhaps if the backend was compatible with Raytracing as well like:

author: mrdoob

body:
> `const backend = WebGPU.isAvailable() ? new WebGPUBackend() : new WebGLBackend(); const renderer = new THREE.Renderer( backend );`

That's acceptable, I think -- but `const renderer = WebGPU.isAvailable() ? new WebGPURenderer() : new WebGLNodeRenderer()` is semantically the same but little bit simpler. But I don't oppose this approach.

That's basically the same `WebGPURenderer` will do internally.

```js
const backend = WebGPU.isAvailable() ? new WebGPUBackend() : new WebGLBackend();
```

Oh! I almost forgot...

this.outputColorSpace = null;
```

This default will let the renderer use `SRGBColorSpace` or `DisplayP3ColorSpace` automatically based on the user's display (eventually).
author: mrdoob

body:
> I think we can do this check: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23614#issuecomment-1434281946

Interesting! Couldn't we implement this check in the ctor call of the renderers? I suppose the user's display color space is not something that is going to change during app usage.

I just wonder how and when the renderer determines the output color space. If the default value is `null` and the user is not going to define the color space (which will be true most of the time), the renderer has to set the value eventually.
author: Mugen87

body:
Hmm...

I'm searching the internal usage of `outputColorSpace` in `/src` and setting in to `null` could indeed complicate things a bit.

The constructor could just do this too:

```js
const isP3Display = window.matchMedia( '(color-gamut: p3)' ).matches;

this.outputColorSpace = isP3Display ? DisplayP3ColorSpace : SRGBColorSpace;
```

I think that'd be the first parameter that we have that is dynamic like that but I think I'm open to it... ­¤żö
author: mrdoob

body: To me, that would be the most intuitive solution. I think it's understandable for users why the output color space is a) determined by the renderer and b) might vary from display to display.
author: Mugen87

body: Alright. Lets do that ­¤æŹ
author: mrdoob

body:
BTW: `DCI-P3` related features are tracked by the task 2.2 in #23614.

Looking at the spec, I wonder if defining `DisplayP3ColorSpace` is sufficient. Is it required to change the [WebGLRenderingContext.drawingBufferColorSpace](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGLRenderingContext/drawingBufferColorSpace), too? It accepts `srgb` and `display-p3` with `srgb` as the default.

When I change this property in any `three.js` example to `display-p3`, I can actually see a hue shift. I wonder now which setup (values of output color space and drawing buffer color space) is actually correct.

Let's enable color management by default first before going for the `DisplayP3ColorSpace` change. We need the color space conversion in any event to get the colors in `DisplayP3ColorSpace` right.

body:
Even if `encodings_fragment` were implemented for Display P3, I'm not sure yet when it should be turned on. The difference when enabling it for existing content is very subtle ŌĆö not an automatic improvement.

There are more impactful results when bringing in wide-gamut colors and textures in scenes designed for Display P3, particularly for unlit scenes. Or perhaps for rendering lit scenes in a wide-gamut working color space (not just wide-gamut output color space). But either of those will require more investigation.

For now I think we should not auto-detect ŌĆö SRGBColorSpace will be the right default for a while yet.

>Do we already support mipmaps on DataTexture

I wasn't able to find other code in 3js or any 3js examples that reference `DataTexture.mipmaps`. The [DataTexture docpage](https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/textures/DataTexture) doesn't mention the `mipmap` field. Of course, the [Texture docpage](https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/textures/Texture) does, but it's marked as "optional". What specific requirements were you thinking of re: "support"?

>does this PR render as expected?

Full disclosure: For my current use case, I actually wanted to load KTX2s into CubeTextures. After manually post-processing (splitting the data into six faces, etc.) all the DataTextures into CubeTextures (including the ones in `.mipmaps`), they do render correctly. body:
Thanks for the pointers! I brought up the assembly of `CubeTexture`s from the loaded `DataTexture`s mainly to say that this loading code works as expected, and renders properly in my context. Let's focus on this loader PR first and pick back up the `CubeTexture` discussion in a future PR.

That said, for the last few days I've been trying to put together [this 3js sample](https://emmanuellee.com/three.js/examples/webgl_loader_texture_ktx2.html) along with [this synthetic KTX2](https://emmanuellee.com/three.js/examples/textures/compressed/mipmaps_sample.ktx2) (contains mips of different solid colors) to test rendering of the `DataTexture` that's created from a KTX2 with mipmaps, but I've run into a few issues/questions with texture uploading.

1. When uploading a `DataTexture` with mipmaps, [this line](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/805f1865a7d3a51086c3e5f1037680e6e8bae1cf/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLTextures.js#L825) incorrectly looks for `width`, `height`, `data` in `mipmap` rather than in `mipmap.source` or `mipmap.image`. Is this a bug? I wonder if this codepath simply isn't used much. Below, when uploading a `DataTexture` without mipmaps, [this line](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/805f1865a7d3a51086c3e5f1037680e6e8bae1cf/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLTextures.js#L847) correctly references `image.width`, `image.height`, `image.data`. However, even after fixing that line to use `mipmap.image`, I get: `GL_INVALID_VALUE: Texture dimensions must all be greater than zero.`

2. It seems 3js expects a texture's base mip to be included as the first entry in its own `mipmaps` array. Is that right?
author: emmanueljl

body:
>  looks for width, height, data in mipmap rather than in mipmap.source or mipmap.image ...

A texture's mipmaps are not textures, they are plain objects containing `width`, `height`, and `data` entries for each mip. This code could be helpful as a reference, although it deals with compressed textures:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/49f7bf498e1b37a65b215a17820fb536f44ab2a1/examples/jsm/loaders/KTX2Loader.js#L518

Also ŌĆō it's not possible for two mips of a [2D, non-array] texture to have the same dimensions. I see this sample has 6 mips ("levels") with sizes [32px, 16px, 8px, 4px, 2px, 1px], from ktxinfo output:

```
pixelWidth: 32
pixelHeight: 32
pixelDepth: 0
layerCount: 0
faceCount: 1
levelCount: 6
```

Is that what you're expecting to find? The KTX2 file would need to be authored slightly differently to contain 6 faces of the same resolution for a cubemap.
author: donmccurdy

body: >A texture's mipmaps are not textures, they are plain objects containing width, height, and data entries for each mip. I see! Then the way my PR loads levels is wrong. I'll fix that up. >it's not possible for two mips of a [2D, non-array] texture to have the same dimensions. ~By this, do you mean that the correct resulting `DataTexture` from loading my KTX2 should be 32px and then have `mipmaps` of [16px, 8px, 4px, 2px, 1px] (as opposed to `mipmaps` of [**32px,** 16px, 8px, 4px, 2px, 1px])?~ Never mind, seems that the base mip should be included in its own mipmaps array. >Is that what you're expecting to find? The KTX2 file would need to be authored slightly differently to contain 6 faces of the same resolution for a cubemap. Yes, they are just POT squares of different solid colors to test with. Let's worry about cubemapping in a future PR.
author: emmanueljl

body:
> By this, do you mean ... mipmaps of [16px, 8px, 4px, 2px, 1px] (as opposed to mipmaps of [32px, 16px, 8px, 4px, 2px, 1px])

Oops, I just meant that we can't store a cube texture as mips like [32px, 32px, 32px, ...], the sizes have to descend. Sounds like we're on the same page there. The mipmaps should contain all of the texture's data, the 0th mip is the full-resolution data.

```js
material = new MeshStandardMaterial( { color: new Color( hex, SRGBColorSpace ), ... } );

body: I think this actually might be even something we would like to do in core's Vector2, Vector3, and Vector4... I.e. so that, for example, `new Vector2( 1 )` would produce `new Vector2( 1, 1 )` instead of `new Vector2( 1, 0 )`.
author: LeviPesin

body: @sunag What do you think?
author: LeviPesin

body: Interesting, I think it's cool, my only fear that it might break legacy projects.
author: sunag

body: Sounds good to me.
author: sunag

body:
I think that breaking changes would be only in cases that aren't really widely used:
* `new Vector2()` -- was (0, 0), remains (0, 0), nothing changes,
* `new Vector2( x )` -- was (x, 0), becomes (x, x), **changes**,
* `new Vector2( x, y )` -- was (x, y), remains (x, y), nothing changes,
* `new Vector3()` -- was (0, 0, 0), remains (0, 0, 0), nothing changes,
* `new Vector3( x )` -- was (x, 0, 0), becomes (x, x, x), **changes**,
* `new Vector3( x, y )` -- was (x, y, 0), remains (x, y, 0), nothing changes,
* `new Vector3( x, y, z )` -- was (x, y, z), remains (x, y, z), nothing changes,
* `new Vector4()` -- was (0, 0, 0, 0), remains (0, 0, 0, 0), nothing changes,
* `new Vector4( x )` -- was (x, 0, 0, 1), becomes (x, x, x, 1), **changes**,
* `new Vector4( x, y )` -- was (x, y, 0, 1), remains (x, y, 0, 1), nothing changes,
* `new Vector4( x, y, z )` -- was (x, y, z, 1), remains (x, y, z, 1), nothing changes,
* `new Vector4( x, y, z, w )` -- was (x, y, z, w), remains (x, y, z, w), nothing changes.

So really nothing changes from the current version except `new Vector2/3/4( x )`.

@mrdoob @Mugen87 What do you think? Could this be done in core?
author: LeviPesin

body:
CI was failing since examples expected float values when using `InterleavedBufferAttribute` and `BufferAttribute` which don't have `gpuType` configured. I'll try only checking against `gpuType` so this defaults to float, but it would be good to confirm expected behavior here.

For this to not be breaking, I'll need to add back special casing for `type === gl.INT || type === gl.UNSIGNED_INT` which correspond to `Int32BufferAttribute` and `Uint32BufferAttribute`, respectively.

This feels like an oversight of the API though since those two are the only ones that are automatically and exclusively bound as integers. It also seems unnecessary to have to match `gpuType` with the container rather inferring it from the attribute's array constructor, if that's feasible for interleaved data. I might propose `BufferAttribute.integer = false` like https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/21606#issuecomment-877731479 and inferring `gpuType` when set, and this can be a separate PR.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body: Previously we'd discussed `gpuType` only being able to be set to `IntType` and `FloatType` since the native type can be inferred from there based on the typed array (see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/21606#issuecomment-1534759990)

body:
Why we need this `backend/webgpu/`, why we cannot place it in the same folder?
`renderers/webgpu/WebGPURenderer.js` should use `renderers/webgpu/WebGPUBackend.js` and `renderers/webgl/WebGLRenderer.js` should use `renderers/webgl/WebGLBackend.js`. Or, if we are okay to do a breaking change, we can remove WebGLRenderer and WebGPURenderer and tell user to do `new Renderer( new WebGLBackend() or new WebGPUBackend() )`.
**There should not be** such thing as WebGPURenderer using WebGLBackend. This will extremely confuse the users.

body:
I think you have to use `getSize()` here. Otherwise the renderer gets a too large size when later using `setDrawingBufferSize()`. 

The problem is that the vector returned from `getDrawingBufferSize()` is the effective resolution meaning dimension * pixel ratio. When restoring the renderer's size via `setDrawingBufferSize()`, you have to apply width and height without the pixel ratio honored (because the pixel ratio is applied as the third parameter).
author: Mugen87

body: If the drawing buffer methods are too confusing from your point of view, consider to use a combination of `setSize()`/`setPixelRatio()` and `getSize()`/`getPixelRatio()`. Maybe the code gets easier to read with this.

author: donmccurdy

body:
The general approach is to move the camera such that the point under the cursor is visually consistent after zooming and the target is moved to accommodate this camera position movement.

However - when using non-screenspace panning and trying to zoom to cursor when the camera is looking parallel to the plane this portion of code fails because we're using the camera ray and plane to determine where to place the target. Any recommendations? Should we just disable zoom to cursor when the dot product of the plane normal and ray  is under some threshold? Then we can recommend that users limit the angles in these cases.
author: gkjohnson

body: Also presumably this shouldn't assume that the full screen is used for controls. Is there a preferred way to get the dom element offset and relative cursor coord?
author: gkjohnson

body: You could use the [`clientWidth` and `clientHeight`](https://stackoverflow.com/a/21064102) on the `canvas`
author: snowzurfer

body: I think we should make it so that when you're parallel and zoom "towards the sky", we pick an arbitrary distance away and use that as if we had collided with the terrain, then use the same code. I.e. dolly the camera in that direction
author: snowzurfer

body: How about caching this `ray`? I.e. creating it only once
author: snowzurfer
snowzurfer

body:
Unfortunately this won't work since the camera always has to face the target point. This means the camera will move up into the sky and then arc down towards the view point since the target has to stay on that plane - which isn't necessarily what someone would expect.

Zoom to cursor with map controls only really makes sense when limiting the camera control angles, imo, but we should have some approach that doesn't break here. Perhaps the arcing is okay in this case.

body: Yeah I plan to cache both ray and plane once understanding how to handle the corner case mentioned above.

body:
A so-called `materialCopy` that does additive blending is not copying. I think that is poorly-named.

Do you still think `premultipliedAlpha` is correct? I find trying to track the logic of the code in this file a bit daunting...

If you are not sure, I wonder if changing the behavior for users is worth it?

//

On a related note, I think the existing example has too much bloom effect, and looks better with this one-line change. IMO, there would be no need to change the parameters to try to match the previous look.
author: WestLangley

body:
> A so-called materialCopy that does additive blending is not copying. I think that is poorly-named.

Agreed. E.g. `materialBlend` sounds more appropriate.

> Do you still think premultipliedAlpha is correct?

Yes. The pass additively blends over the input. Considering how `CopyPass` is implemented, `premultipliedAlpha` should be set to `true`.

> If you are not sure, I wonder if changing the behavior for users is worth it?

Let me think about this. That is a valid point since even without the change the effect looks "right".

> On a related note, I think the existing example has too much bloom effect, and looks better with this one-line change. IMO, there would be no need to change the parameters to try to match the previous look.

Yeah, I think I got a little bit excited when `UnrealBloomPass` finally produces a correctly tone-mapped result in the right color space so I couldn't have enough bloom ­¤śģ . I'm fine with turning it down a bit.
author: Mugen87

body:
>Considering how CopyPass is implemented, premultipliedAlpha should be set to true.

We should set `.premultipliedAlpha` to true only if the shader outputs premultiplied RGB values. So, the question is: does it?
author: WestLangley

body: Yes, I think so. The opacity scales all color components, not just alpha.
author: Mugen87

body:
This effect was not written to properly accommodate the premultiplied output from RenderPass.

Given this PR causes breakage for users, and given the change here sill leaves inconsistencies in the code, I'd suggest doing nothing for now.
author: WestLangley

body:
> , and given the change here sill leaves inconsistencies in the code

On what inconsistencies are you referring to? Is there something else to fix?
author: Mugen87

body: But I agree the breakage isn't worth the overall change. If someone reports a bug related to blending that can be fixed by setting the `premultipliedAlpha` to `true`, we can reconsider the PR.
author: Mugen87

body: When implementing the fiddle for https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/24703#issuecomment-1584249800, I've noticed that the bloom is too strong given standard values like `1` for bloom strength. It seems enabling `premultipliedAlpha` as suggested in the PR produces a more consistent results.

body: I'm trying to convince myself that yes, but I think that currently is the better approach. I created a class to contextualize `LighingModel` but it creates more classes that can replaced by `.includes()`, basically if the user does not declare/assign `clearcoat` in `.constructVariants()` we can use `builder.includes( clearcoat )` to detect that `clearcoat` is not used in this material.
author: sunag

body: The syntax `shader( ( {}, stack, builder ) => {} )` is correct, but the `call` syntax should be just `.call( { ...inputs } )` like `FunctionCallNode` for the users and not `.call( {}, stack, builder )`. It because `call()` should return a `ShaderCallNode` and not run the function immediately. When it's called in `construct()` process, we can send `stack` and `builder` parameters, but if the user calls the shader() outside `construct()` like `material.colorNode = shader(..).call()` we would not be able to pass the `builder/stack` because it is in the wrong builder stage.

body: This is a change in convention. Previously, some of the loaders would trigger `normalizeSkinWeights()` after `SkinnedMesh` construction but now it is always done inside the constructor. This is required before the bone weights texture is created and is one less thing that has to be duplicated in loader code.
author: cstegel

body:
This is a change in behavior. It fixes a bug when loading models with >4 skin weights and is also necessary for computing the skin weight texture.

Previously, only the first 4 weights were used instead of the highest 4 among all the weights. Now, the weights are sorted across all buffers before normalizing to the first N that will actually be used.
author: cstegel

body:
Removing the Gamma Correction pass here results in a different visual:
See the comparison below : 

# With gamma correction pass
![With gamma correction pass](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/18304870/abe34b2d-4e24-402d-9a0b-25dd09ca6bd1)

# Without gamma correction pass
![Without gamma correction pass](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/18304870/48619642-6ff3-43ec-8bc9-f2c99dfa640a)

# Original example from threejs.org
![Original example from threejs.org](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/18304870/d985de65-1389-4aa2-801d-71d609cdbb58)

The fact that the LUT pass is fed with the gamma correction pass output is meaningful for the final result. The outputColorSpace occurs at the end of the pipeline. For this example, I think we should keep the intermediary gamma correction pass.
author: AxiomeCG

body:
This should catch the case when the last queried object being rendered is the last draw call in the pass.  In the draw call. The end is inserted when a new object is seen, to allow handling of multiple draw calls per object (array cameras etc). Otherwise the endOcclusionQuery call could be at the end of the draw method.

According to the docs you can't use a querySet index in multiple beginOcclusionQuery( index ) calls in the same pass, otherwise you could just have repeated begin/end pairs around each draw() call.

The docs state:

> Note that for best results you should always specify an [environment map](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html#api/en/materials/MeshStandardMaterial.envMap) when using this material.

So we don't say an env map is mandatory or required.
author: Mugen87

body: - https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26344
author: donmccurdy

body:
The specular reflectance of the material is non-zero and increases with metalness. This is consistent with the insulators and conductors the material is intended to model. The material is physically-based and requires an environment map for proper rendering -- not only for reflections, but also for proper ambient lighting.

Omitting the environment map is akin to omitting scene lighting.

Without an environment map, you get results like the screenshot above.

body:
I use custom cubemap mip levels generation for a more efficient environment reflection algorithm than Three.js has. Actually I use the same algorithm that Unreal Engine 5 uses for blended reflection captures.
I tried to implement the algorithm and realized that I can't set a specific mip level of a texture as a render target. The mip level is always locked to 0:

`_gl.framebufferTexture2D( _gl.FRAMEBUFFER, attachment, textureTarget, properties.get( texture ).__webglTexture, 0 );`

WebGL 2 supports rendering to any mip level according to the specification.
I removed the limitation in three.js and successfully implemented my algorithm. 

The existing WebGLRenderer.setRenderTarget function accepts 'activeMipmapLevel' parameter but actually it doesn't work! It just ignores the parameter. Actually it is a bug. There is no example in three.js that renders to a mip level, so this bug hasn't been noticed. It's a very very usefull functionality for advanced technics like custom mips filtering, for instance.

author: Mugen87

body: These unit tests are silly. They don't actually proof the correctness of the actual rotation. A non-normalized quaternion that is applied to the rotation algorithm can only serve as a kind of fingerprint for the formulas if the arbitrary numbers that get were put in, generate a certain output. As the formulas have changed, they don't provide the same output for non-normalized quaternions. All other unit-tests that work on actual versors work perfectly fine.

The problem with using `THREE.Clock` is that when users switch to another tab and then come back to the application after a while, you end up with a huge time delta value that leads to a visible jump. Compare the PRs version with prod to see this effect:

https://raw.githack.com/Issung/three.js/%2326471-orbitcontrols-framerate-independent/examples/webgl2_volume_instancing.html
https://threejs.org/examples/webgl2_volume_instancing

This issue can only be solved with another time class implementation like #17912.

The controls now also produce large time delta values when users enable - disable - enable auto rotation.

body:
I again suggest we should not do this.
WebGPURenderer running WebGL would be **extremely** confusing for the users.
We can make WebGPURenderer using WebGPUBackend, WebGLRenderer using WebGLBackend, and something like UniversalRenderer that uses WebGPUBackend and can fall through to WebGLBackend. Or we can just advise users to do `new Renderer( any backend they want to use )`.
author: LeviPesin

body: `webgpu_*` examples should also only showcase WebGPUBackend examples.
author: LeviPesin

body:
> I again suggest we should not do this. WebGPURenderer running WebGL would be **extremely** confusing for the users.

Do you mind elaborating with a couple of examples?
author: mrdoob

body:
We're doing this to move people to WebGPU without them having to worry that is not yet supported in Firefox, Safari, Android, iOS and Linux. Eventually these platforms will support WebGPU and then the WebGPU backend will be used automatically in their projects without them having to change anything.

Most of the developers using the library do not really know the difference between WebGL and WebGPU, neither should they. The people that know what they difference is will probably not have a hard time understanding that there's a temporary WebGL backend in place.

Having said that `WebGPURenderer` is still being worked on and it is not ready to replace `WebGLRenderer` just yet.
author: mrdoob

body:
> Do you mind elaborating with a couple of examples?

As a user, I expect that if I see a class named WebGPURenderer, it means it uses WebGPU -- i.e., I can write WGSL shaders for it, I can use bleeding-edge WebGPU features, I don't encounter WebGL bugs. If all out of sudden after many hours of debugging "Why this doesn't work?" I'll realize that all this time the name of the class was deceiving me and actually it was WebGL -- I would be extremely confused, to say the least.

> We're doing this to move people to WebGPU without them having to worry that is not yet supported in Firefox, Safari, Android, iOS and Linux.

This is a **too large thing** that people could "not worry about". They cannot expect perfect compatibility between WebGL and WebGPU! They **must** carefully plan if they are going to use WebGL, WebGPU, or both -- we shouldn't pack a mix between WebGL and WebGPU into a box titled WebGPU. At most we can only create some UniversalRenderer and make it explicit that it could use both backends and people should think about and test it very carefully.

> Most of the developers using the library do not really know the difference between WebGL and WebGPU, neither should they.

Both parts of this sentence are wrong. Developers **should** (and do) know that Three.js' WebGLRenderer is based on a graphics API called WebGL and WebGPURenderer based on a graphics API called WebGPU, and they **should** know (and we should make it very clear in docs and tutorials!) that these APIs **may not** be perfectly compatible.

> Having said that┬ĀWebGPURenderer┬Āis still being worked on and it is not ready to replace┬ĀWebGLRenderer┬Ājust yet.

This is why I hope the decision to have WebGPURenderer with WebGLBackend could still be overturned and we could consider other alternatives (such as an UniversalRenderer).

body:
> As a user, I expect that if I see a class named WebGPURenderer, it means it uses WebGPU -- i.e., I can write WGSL shaders for it, I can use bleeding-edge WebGPU features, I don't encounter WebGL bugs. If all out of sudden after many hours of debugging "Why this doesn't work?" I'll realize that all this time the name of the class was deceiving me and actually it was WebGL -- I would be extremely confused, to say the least.

That has a pretty easy solution.

When the renderer uses the WebGLBackend we should just console log  that the system doesn't support WebGPU and that the renderer is using WebGL2 as fallback and that some features may not be supported. 

Later on we can specify exactly which features are not supported when we know.
author: mrdoob

body:
> From what I understand the timeline would be this:
> 
> ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/502810/265753781-0719a5a2-5dd6-4d3d-ae10-4efa9617e1cc.png)

Haha!

That graph is missing the `CanvasRenderer` before `WebGLRenderer` and the `SVGRenderer` before that too.

Can't remember the name of the renderer class I picked when it was in ActionScript ­¤śü
author: mrdoob

body:
About the timeline -- I think the important thing there is that "WebGLRenderer -- WebGL 2/WebGL" was OK, because WebGL 2 was backwards compatible with WebGL. WebGPU isn't.

> That has a pretty easy solution.
>
> When the renderer uses the WebGLBackend we should just console log that the system doesn't support WebGPU and that the renderer is using WebGL2 as fallback and that some features may not be supported.
>
> Later on we can specify exactly which features are not supported when we know.

Okay. But I'd expect the feature list (supported in WebGPU but not in WebGL, bugs not presented in WebGPU but that presented in WebGL) to be quite large. And therefore if a user uses such a feature they generally expect that only WebGPU is used (as they may expect from the title)...

But mostly I'm arguing only about the name. If we just rename (current) WebGPURenderer to UniversalRenderer, or WebGPUWithWebGLFallbackRenderer, or anything like that, I'll be fine.
author: LeviPesin

body: I do not think `WebGPURenderer` should fall back to `WebGLRenderer`. I think the app should handle that.
author: WestLangley

body:
`WebGPURenderer` with the new material system follows a different direction than the previous renderers. So insisting to keep the same policy as before ignores the new project's focus.

The node material in combination with TSL will decrease the number of occasions where devs want to write native shader code. This will make it easier for the average users to develop good web experiences since they don't have to take care about fallbacks. The renderer and the material system will do this automatically. This supports the most important goal of the project: Make the 3D in the web easier to use.

So I definitely vote for an automatic fallback.
author: Mugen87

body:
> WebGPURenderer┬Āwith the new material system follows a different direction than the previous renderers.

But this is true not because it uses WebGPU or WebGL but because it is based on the Nodes system.

I'm not against an universal renderer (although I think most people wouldn't use it) but again, I think it shouldn't be named WebGPURenderer.

body:
Mostly, the error about `THREE.WebGLState: TypeError: Failed to execute 'texSubImage2D'...` in the OP, in which I can't get WebGLRenderer to interpret the given data as a cube texture. I'm finding it difficult to construct valid textures in these layouts, and I think having distinct classes with documented parameters might help.

I imagine we don't _technically need_ as many texture classes as we have already, and everything else could just be parameterization. And in theory I'm fine with fewer classes, but we'd need documentation of those parameters. I haven't been able to figure it out from reading the renderer code so far, other than that it's theoretically supported. ­¤ś░

The difference between DataTexture and CompressedTexture is pretty fuzzy to me, especially since CompressedTexture can use internal formats that aren't technically compressed, but neither name seems like it encompasses the purpose of the other.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Um, if I had to choose I would distinct between a normal texture class and a cube texture class. We already do that with `Texture` and ` CubeTexture` and I think it is consistent to transfer this policy to `DataTexture`. It's just important to me that we deprecate the "old" approach at some point and force users towards `DataCubeTexture`. Same for `CompressedCubeTexture`.

> Mostly, the error about THREE.WebGLState: TypeError: Failed to execute 'texSubImage2D'... in the OP, in which I can't get WebGLRenderer to interpret the given data as a cube texture.

Whether a texture is interpreted as a normal texture or cube texture depends on the shader. `WebGLUniforms` decides based on the uniform type what `setTexture*()` function is called from `WebGLTextures`. The given texture must be compatible to the respective sampler.

> The difference between DataTexture and CompressedTexture is pretty fuzzy to me, especially since CompressedTexture can use internal formats that aren't technically compressed, but neither name seems like it encompasses the purpose of the other.

`CompressedTexture` was initially introduced to easier implement a code path in `WebGLTextures` for calling the `compressed*` WebGL texture commands. There would be of course other ways to make that distinction (like checking the texture format) but I think using a separate class for that is fine. 

It's true that `CompressedTexture` can be configured with plain `RGBAFormat`. However, there is at least one use case where this is in some sense "required". `DDSLoader` supports loading S3TC textures that hold uncompressed RGBA data. Since the loader is derived from `CompressedTextureLoader`, it has to return an instance of `CompressedTexture`. Hence, `CompressedTexture` does support `RGBAFormat` (the only exception next compressed formats). If we want to stop that, `DDSLoader` either needs to return a data texture or we stop support for uncompressed S3TC data completely.
author: Mugen87

body:
> It's just important to me that we deprecate the "old" approach at some point and force users towards DataCubeTexture. Same for CompressedCubeTexture.

I see! Yes that is fine with me. I don't fully understand the old approach, but that's ok.

> Whether a texture is interpreted as a normal texture or cube texture depends on the shader.

I think one issue here, maybe, was `scene.background`. It could be a Texture or CubeTexture and we need to decide based on the texture, not just the shader. Perhaps NodeMaterial has similar cases?

It sounds like we're OK with adding these classes then, but just need to figure out how to ensure they work correctly?

body:
Sorry for the late review!

Would you mind undo all changes related to `add()` in this PR? It really should focus on `attach()` and not on other methods. If we change `add()` in this way, we also need to update `remove()` and this is too much for a single PR.

IMO, the optimization for #26740 is not really necessary since `attach()` should be used with objects which are already part of the scene graph. That means they have a parent and thus go through the existing code path and not the new optimized one. In order to keep the method simple as possible, I would leave this bit out for now.

If you want to extend the class from `BufferGeometry`, name it `SDFGeometry`.

Maybe you can also think of a different design. Something like:
```js
const generator = new SDFGeometryGenerator( renderer );

const geometry = generator.generate( res, func, bounds );

Since the generator relies on the renderer, materials, shaders and render targets, I would probably vote for this design in order to delimit the class from classic geometry generators which do not have such complex dependencies.

body:
Because `COLOR` is not an attribute name in either the glTF or `EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing` specifications, we probably shouldn't use it here ŌĆö non-standard semantics should be prefixed with an `_`.

We could either follow the vertex color naming convention, `COLOR_0`, or choose our own name prefixed with an underscore, like `_COLOR`. I'm leaning somewhat toward `COLOR_0`.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Okay since the spec suggests prefixing underscore for custom instance attribute, we can use `_COLOR`, or maybe `_COLOR_0` ?

`COLOR_0` is also fine, but should that be proposed in the spec as well, since it's not prefixed?

body:
A comment for potential future optimization. (So no need to do in this PR.)

As commented at

https://github.com/takahirox/three-gltf-extensions/blob/00e4233e4fc6c086f13c1c2de80ecc8a3c7eec29/exporters/EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing/EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing_exporter.js#L55

[the attributes are optional](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/extensions/2.0/Vendor/EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing/README.md#extending-nodes-with-instance-attributes) and we may be able to think of exporting an attribute only if it has non-default value. For example, it may not be too rare that all scales are default (1, 1, 1) and in such a case the exported file size can be reducted.

Another approach would be the exporter always exports all the attributes as this PR does, and the optimization can be done in external gltf post-processing pipeline.

body: Shouldn't this be compared with an epsilon?
author: LeviPesin

body: By epsilon, do you mean a small threshold value? That could certainly help when the camera and axis are not strictly parallel but are practically parallel. When this happens the resulting cross product is normalized making a seemingly random vector that is used as the reference for rotation. If we we were to use an epsilon (assuming I am understanding it correctly) we would probably need to update the line `this.rotationAxis.copy( this.eye );` in the in_plane rotation logic to be something like `_inPlaneRotation ? this.rotationAxis.copy( _unit[ axis ] ) : this.rotationAxis.copy( this.eye );` to ensure you rotate around the intended axis rather than `this.eye` which may be very slightly off-axis.

body:
What happens when you press a single key? Do you mind sharing screenshots of https://www.toptal.com/developers/keycode when you press `+` and `-`?

I don't think we can use `event.code` because plus and minus exist in so many keyboard layout variations.
author: Mugen87

When pressing just the `=` key:

```js
{
 "key": "=",

 "code": "Equal",
 "location": 0,

 "ctrlKey": false,

}
```

When pressing `shift + =`:

```js
{
 "key": "+",

 "location": 0,
 "altKey": false,
 "ctrlKey": false,

 "repeat": false
}
```

When pressing the `-` key:

```js
{
 "key": "-",
 "keyCode": 189,
 "which": 189,
 "code": "Minus",
 "location": 0,
 "altKey": false,
 "ctrlKey": false,
 "metaKey": false,
 "shiftKey": false,
 "repeat": false
}
```

I'm on a 2021 M1 Macbook Pro keyboard.
author: gkjohnson

body:
Thanks for sharing the data! Overall, I think the result on your computer is as expected. When using `event.key`, the controls react on whatever key or key combination produces a specific value. In this case plus and minus. 

Like mentioned in #26826, there are use cases where a relying on physical key positions is better like when developing a game that requires WASD or arrow keys input. Even if WASD keys are in a different location (e.g. when using AZERTY keyboards), gamers usually play with the same physical keys (see #21055). 

I believe plus and minus needs to be handled differently though because even in context of WASD layouts, plus and minus exists in different variations. So checking for the character via `event.key` should deliver more reasonable results.
author: Mugen87

body:
I didn't think the +/= key moved around all the much (same spots in QWERTY, AZERTY, DVORAK, etc) - though it looks like it's not in the common spot with a QWERTZ layout, at least.

I guess what I was getting at is that the demo seemed like it had an issue because the key didn't work until I tried shift. I think if I used an app that told me to zoom with - and + I'd think it was broken.

The only other option I can think of is supporting both + and = characters - but I understand that might be unacceptable. Just a suggestion.

body: Doesn't this line also need PURE annotation (also, why `MathUtils.DEG2RAD` requires destructuring)?
author: LeviPesin

body:
Most built-in JavaScript methods and classes are picked up as pure -- `Object.create` in motion controllers lib was the only exception I found.

I'm not sure why `MathUtils` needed destructuring. I noticed this with https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26912#discussion_r1349519605 and previously hardcoded the value until d5ce35ceec8ae5f9fb214f4456350da2e6f55d29. There might have been something similar with reads from `ShaderLib` and `UniformsLib` which I wrapped in IIFEs.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body: Perhaps a better example of https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26912#discussion_r1349771865. I refactored this from a static method back to a class in b9d5666c97b682e26aeb3467bc4cf8ed28d234ef, figuring that destructuring from `chevrotain` rather than member access would tree-shake and that seems to be the case. Why they're not equivalent for tools, I'm not sure. Perhaps it's accounting for possible getters? This case is regarded in https://esbuild.github.io/api/#tree-shaking-and-side-effects.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body: In any event, I like this solution more than the previous refactoring ­¤ść .
author: Mugen87
CodyJasonBennett

body: Explanation after further investigation in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/26912#discussion_r1352129525 -- member expressions can call getters, destructuring cannot. `Object.create` is also properly impure (motion controllers lib) since it mutates its arguments. This is consistent with how tools interpret built-ins.

Not sure how you're testing, but I've made a repro branch of what I mean:
https://github.com/marcofugaro/treeshake-test/tree/addons-test

As you can see `LottieLoader` and `lottie_canvas.module.js` are tree-shaken already.
I also tested `fflate.module.js`, `opentype.module.js`, `mmdparser.module.js` but I believe most of `jsm/libs` are already tree-shakeable.
author: marcofugaro
marcofugaro

author: marcofugaro

body: As per my last message, I explained the aforementioned issues and testing strategy in the PR description. I'm not testing any optimizers like Uglify or Terser (Vite optionally uses the latter), nor am I treating three as external in the event that `package.sideEffects` is interactive. Please understand that I'm regarding the lowest common denominator between minification strategies for creating a baseline for testing and the sake of observability, so even though tools like ESBuild and Rollup have access to package information for smarter traversal, others only work at the module level and can't afford such optimizations.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
Yeah I did read the description. My point is that in the real world, those issues don't prevent the tree-shaking of #26910.

Unlike the main bundle, we're not importing from a single file here. Since the `package.sideEffects` flag is present, the bundlers are able to completely discard the unused files, even if they have some side-effects in it.

I did a test with the full Addons.js from #26910 and as you can see Esbuild, Rollup and Webpack can successfully tree-shake the Addons.js file, even without minification (only three.module.js and FXAAShader.js are present in the final bundle):
https://github.com/marcofugaro/treeshake-test/blob/addons-test-full/dist/index-esbuild.js

My point is, the changes in `jsm/libs` and the IIFE wraps make maintaning the repo more difficult, while not providing any real improvement to tree-shaking.

Is it possible to quantify how much memory the engine would additionally allocate with enabled stencil buffer and e.g. a 5K framebuffer ?
author: Mugen87

body: Stencil uses 1 byte per pixel, so for rendering at 5K resolution that's about 15 megabytes. Whether that's a lot or not depends on perspective, but I think we should avoid this kind of waste. With WebGL it also can't be assumed that the user is focusing on one app at once, but rather it's quite common to have multiple open simultaneously.

As mentioned in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/26501#issuecomment-1657107116, the `three.js editor` is a pure scene editor. And it's not the feature of a scene editor to author skeletons/armatures or animations. This is usually done in a software like Blender. It's really no good strategy to mix the concepts of a scene editor and a digital content creation tool. Both pieces of software should focus on different things. 

Besides, I'm afraid the PR in its current form won't be much usable in production. Especially the assignment of fixed skin weights does not reflect how skinning is used in real world apps.

author: donmccurdy

body:
Thank you for the question.

The goal of this change is to ensure the uniqueness of node name. The previous implementation could return non-unique node name on a certain case. The issue is the array this.nodeNamesUsed doesn't memorize all of the node values because of early return on line 3529.

Here's an example, given two nodes:
- First node with name "triangle" contains two primitives, each without a name.
- Second node with name "triangle_1" contains one primitive without a name.

The loader will then process each primitive as follows:
In the old implementation:
```
[1] sanitizedName = "triangle"
    sanitizedName in this.nodeNamesUsed = False
    this.nodeNamesUsed["triangle"] = 0
    > return "triangle" 

[2] sanitizedName = "triangle"
    sanitizedName in this.nodeNamesUsed = True
    this.nodeNamesUsed["triangle"] = 1
    > return "triangle_1"

[3] sanitizedName = "triangle_1"
    sanitizedName in this.nodeNamesUsed = False
    this.nodeNamesUsed["triangle_1"] = 0
    > return "triangle_1"
```
This is causing issue, because there are two nodes named "triangle_1"

In the new implementation, here is the process:
```
[1] sanitizedName = uniqueName = "triangle"
    sanitizedName in this.nodeNamesUsed = False
    this.nodeNamesUsed["triangle"] = 0
    > return "triangle" 

[2] sanitizedName = uniqueName = "triangle"
    sanitizedName in this.nodeNamesUsed = True
    uniqueName = "triangle_1"
    this.nodeNamesUsed["triangle"] = 1
    this.nodeNamesUsed["triangle_1"] = 0
    > return "triangle_1"

[3] sanitizedName = uniqueName = "triangle_1"
    sanitizedName in this.nodeNamesUsed = True
    uniqueName = "triangle_1_1"
    this.nodeNamesUsed["triangle_1"] = 1
    this.nodeNamesUsed["triangle_1_1"] = 0
    > return "triangle_1_1"
```
This function will always create unique name for each node.

Hope this answer your question.
Let me know if you have any other concern.

Thank you.

body:
Compared to the previous version, `initialValue` is not used anymore and could be removed from `generateRotationTrack()`. 

Why is this parameter obsolete now?
author: Mugen87

body:
It was previously used in the `getKeyframTrackValues` function that created the final values table for the animation. It concatenated the curves values (x,y and z) and added the interpolated values by completing the non interpolated curves  by their previous known value (since the interpolation was done only on one value (x,y or z) and not the whole angle). The `initialValue` solely initialized the `prevValue` used to determine which value to add to the final values table. 
It is not useful anymore since I interpolate and create the values tables at the same time and for the whole angle (all x,y and z curves) at once. On line 2902, we see that I initialize a so called initialValue as well. the one for i=0 is equivalent to the initial value here.

Note that since this only defines a getter, setting `updateRange` is in fact broken in r159.

This broke our project and we were able to work around it easily, but I think it would be nice if the release notes for r159 were updated to mention the sudden breaking change for setting the value. As of right now, they only mention a new feature without mentioning any breaking changes:

> Add support for multiple update ranges. https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/27103, https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/27148, https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/27149 (@gkjohnson)

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/releases/tag/r159

(Or you could introduce a setter with a deprecation message.)

body:
Aha! Unfortunately, that `updateRange` does not appear on [this page](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/releases) when I search for it. I had to spend a few minutes trying to figure out why setting `updateRange` wasn't working, because nothing in the release notes actually mentioned the removal of the setter. While I understand the appeal of a condensed migration reference, it feels like the release notes themselves are sort of pretending that certain changes don't exist ŌĆö when in fact those are the most impactful changes for existing codebases.

Would it be reasonable to inline a copy of the migration changes into the release notes, or to include the breaking changes directly as bullets in the list of changes? I think this would make the release notes a lot more intuitive, since other projects include breaking changes in the release notes themselves.

author: Mugen87

body:
Maybe just for additional clarification:

Before: `getIBLIrradiance`, `getIBLRadiance` and `getIBLAnisotropyRadiance` were returning the environment intensity from the map with the scaling already applied inside the function before the value is returned. This had the drawback that only global scaling per material were possible.

Now: `getIBLIrradiance`, `getIBLRadiance` and `getIBLAnisotropyRadiance` are returning the unmodified environment intensity from the map. The scaling is then applied to their returned values after they are called in `lights_fragment_maps.glsl.js` (this is the only place that they are ever called inside ThreeJS). This has the advantage that we can define a pixel wise scaling per material (e.g. we use it in order to let the environment illumination affect only for specular parts of a material, which was not possible before).
author: TobiasNoell

body:
>we use it in order to let the environment illumination affect only for specular parts of a material, which was not possible before

This is a physically-based shader, and the _radiance_ and _irradiance_ are jointly-consistent and scale together.

If I understand correctly, the OP wants to modify the shader so he can use Node to violate the physics of light in his app and have the environment intensity apply only to radiance.
