body:
The idea was to add a more generic `envMapIntensityNode` slot to the node materials (since we already have a `envMapIntensity` property for the classic materials).

It is true that the user can decide to setup a specific combination of nodes as input for `envMapIntensityNode` that would then yield a result that is not strictly correct in a physical sense, but still achieves the intended result.

In any case, it would be the user that is setting up the node. If the user decides to apply a (physically incorrect) spatially varying scaling of the environment for a material, the user should be aware of what its intentions are, right?

If the `envMapIntensityNode` is ignored or a node combination that yields any uniform floating value, everything would still remain strictly physically plausible.
author: TobiasNoell

body:
This is an example what we are trying to achieve, to make it more practical:

We have a scene that is illuminated by point lights. This gives a nice appearance including shadows on the diffuse parts of the surface, but the chrome like specular parts look too dark:
![image](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/124624771/c7e78205-cc2d-46bd-b1d5-2c96656a954a)

To make the chrome look nicer, we setup an environment map for the scene. This makes the chrome parts look nice, but destroys the appearance of the diffuse parts:
![image](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/124624771/532af4c2-3d68-4ecd-bc4f-df36bd024f74)

To fix this, we want to scale down the effect of the environment map for the rough parts of the surface, but this is only possible with the changes this PR proposes:
![image](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/124624771/974a7d91-b1d6-4978-a43f-0c30420f31c8)
It is done by setting `material.envMapIntensityNode = THREENodes.pow(material.roughnessNode, THREENodes.float(4.0));`

It is true, that the last image is not strictly physically correct, but it looks the nicest out of the three ­¤śä 

All materials in the scene are node materials. If there is any better way to achieve the same I'm happy to hear it.

_One additional note regarding the violation of the physics of light:
It should be mentioned that also with the classical materials it is already possible to do this by setting
`material1.envMapIntensity = 0.5` and `material2.envMapIntensity = 2.0` (i.e. different scaling factors per material). The `WebGPURenderer` even allows entirely different environment maps per material._
author: TobiasNoell

body: It seems to be the case that "needsUpdate" needs to be called when "transparent" is toggled. Is this intended? This happens on normal Meshes in previous versions of three.js, as well.
author: gkjohnson

body:
> It seems to be the case that "needsUpdate" needs to be called when "transparent" is toggled. Is this intended?

Yes, because the `transparent` property controls the `OPAQUE` GLSL define. Without setting  `needsUpdate` to `true` when `transparent` changes, the shaders would not reflect the current material state.
author: Mugen87

body:
That's an implementation detail the user shouldn't have to know about, though - there are plenty of other material fields that don't require setting needsUpdate but trigger a rebuild, including `alphaHash`, `alphaTest`, etc:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLPrograms.js#L506-L507

_edit: Nevermind I see this is just for shader materials - but it would still be nice if this triggered a change if could be done performantly._

author: elalish

body:
it has been done before [here for example](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/master/examples/webgl_raycaster_bvh.html).

my idea is to start small with an "external" example and see if there's enough interest in the technology to justify a three.js "official" loader.

A "proper" integration would require the addition of the whole [decode](https://github.com/MONOGRID/gainmap-js/tree/main/src/decode) part of the code which includes shaders, materials, etc.

Plus our loaders are a bit "atypical": 
1. they require a `WebGLRenderer` to work: decoding is done in the GPU because it is doable and is order of magnitude faster than doing it in JS. Our first implementation was done in JS code but it had to loop the whole HDR Uint16Array and it was too slow for my tastes :) 
2. they return [an object](https://github.com/MONOGRID/gainmap-js/blob/main/src/core/QuadRenderer.ts) which contain
  a. a renderTarget where the texture is rendered, usable elsewhere with `renderTarget.texture`
  b. methods to obtain a `DataTexture` from the GPU, [if needed](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/27171#issuecomment-1806428518)

I'm not sure if three.js's itself wants to break established Loader *conventions* with this, for the moment we can keep these "external" and see if it works for users. 

Let me know otherwise!
author: daniele-pelagatti

body:
> Our first implementation was done in JS code but it had to loop the whole HDR Uint16Array and it was too slow for my tastes :)

How slow was that? ­¤żö

JS parsers tend to be much easier to maintain and improve by the community.
Plus can be used in environments without GPUs.
author: mrdoob

body:
Hi! Nice to speak to you, me and my company are **avid** Three.js users since the beginning, I'd like to take the opportunity to thank you all for the hard work of maintaining the best WebGL Library :rocket: 

> How slow was that? ­¤żö

it was more or less the same speed of the EXR or RGBM parsers you already have, all the decoder does is loop the whole `sdr_image(x, y)` and do simple math operations on the RGB values.

In my opinion, though, the JS decoder implementation kinda defeated the purpose of the library which is useful because gain maps finally allows scenes to have 4k or 8k HDR Environments (or high res HDR light maps) with decent file sizes without forcing the users to wait a long time. With 8k images the JS decoder parsed the image in **more time** than it took to download it and we felt it kinda ruined the fun.

> JS parsers tend to be much easier to maintain and improve by the community.
> Plus can be used in environments without GPUs.

I agree, we used to do the decoding on a worker before implementing the GPU solution.

I can bring back the JS code if you prefer but I'm not very fond of the results, I feel it could be an alternative to the GPU decoder but the default (and the loaders) would still be GPU based.
author: daniele-pelagatti

Could you share a URL to an example that uses the JS decoder so we can check the parsing times?

body: I'm a little confused on exactly what is being decoded where. We need to parse the metadata in JS so that we can find the two JPEG images, but those two bytestreams can be passed to the Browser to use its internal decoders. Then the only step left is to combine the two images, which does feel like a GPU thing since they need to be uploaded as textures anyway. Is that how this works currently, or is there some other architecture?
author: elalish

body:
> Could you share a URL to an example that uses the JS decoder so we can check the parsing times?

Sure, I'm in the process of restoring the old JS decoding loop, I'll let you know when it's done but it's a bit of a pain because I need to retrieve RGB values from two JPEG Uint8Arrays so I need to draw them in a canvas and retrieve their pixel data (let me know if you know any other faster way).

> I'm a little confused on exactly what is being decoded where. We need to parse the metadata in JS so that we can find the two JPEG images, but those two bytestreams can be passed to the Browser to use its internal decoders. Then the only step left is to combine the two images, which does feel like a GPU thing since they need to be uploaded as textures anyway. Is that how this works currently, or is there some other architecture?

the gain map reconstruction is done in roughly three steps:
1. retrieve 2 images + data (extract the gainmap and metadata), and this is done in JS, no problem
2. decode the 2 jpeg files 
3. an algorithm that works pixel per pixel the SDR image and "adds" the "recovery image" on top of it so that HDR data can be restored

The whole loop can be done in JS (as I said: separate images -> retrieve the RGB values of the 2 images -> restore loop -> return Uint16Array) or in the GPU (current solution).

If done in the GPU its almost instantaneous: 
1. step 1 is the only real work in JS
2. step 2 is done by the browser when you upload the 2 textures
3. step 3 is done in a shader.

If done in pure JS is not so fast: 
1. step 1 is the same as before
2. step 2 needs a draw the jpegs in canvas + retrieve pixel data (as previously said, I'm not sure if there's a more efficient alternative)
3. step 3 is done in a `for loop`,  takes a while and locks the main thread for several seconds while it loops an 8k texture (in my tests).

BTW In unrelated news: the black torus problem of the example with iOS + puppeteer seems to be a NaN propagation error of some sort in my decoding shader (sorry `PMREMGenerator` you where the herald of bad news but not the culprit), the example image I used uses the whole Half Float precision in the GPU and I think it overflows somewhere, I'm in the process of fixing the problem without sacrificing the full HDR range.
author: daniele-pelagatti

body:
> Simply normalizing the float depth is not enough to correctly sort, as the fractional part gets truncated - producing incorrect ties.

Can you elaborate on how the previous implementation is incorrect? `el.z / camera.far` converts the value to the normalized range [0, 1] and then `( 1 << 30 ) *` expands that to the full range of positive values represented by signed integers. Yes there are decimal values that will be truncated here when performing bitwise operations & int conversion but the values are truncated in this new approach when performing 32 bit float to a 64 bit float conversion, as well.

I agree the bit-casting approach is more elegant but again I think it's nice to have a single, easy-to-copy function for this case.
author: gkjohnson

body:
Yeah, we'll always get some precision loss, going from 64b to 32b, that is inevitable. But while you are correct that both methods can be used correctly with sufficient care, normalization is more error prone without proper understanding of what's being done. Also, the range specified `1 << 30` isn't the full range of neither signed nor unsigned int32.

```
1 << 30 // 1_073_741_824 - ~1/4th UINT32_MAX
-1 >>> 1 // 2_147_483_647 - INT32_MAX
-1 >>> 0 // 4_294_967_295 - UINT32_MAX
```

I don't mind reverting back to normalization, with the proper limits, I just thought bit-cast was even simpler.
author: sciecode

body:
I did test this and it does re-allocate a lot during "warm-up" until the max-value reaches a big enough value, akin to not passing an auxiliary buffer at all. We also, don't have means of decreasing this buffer size, unlike the external solution which accomodates for when the geometry count changes.

While I understand your intention, I can't say I necessarily agree. But I will adjust my changes to what you prefer.

_projScreenMatrix
   .multiplyMatrices( camera.projectionMatrix, camera.matrixWorldInverse )
   .multiply( this.matrixWorld );

In `WebGLRenderer`, `_projScreenMatrix` transforms from world space directly to clip space and maybe it would be less confusing to stick to this logic in `BatchedMesh`, too?

body:
While migrating the examples towards `setAnimationLoop()` I have noticed an issue in this example. The  `render()` function updates _both_ renderers but since `setAnimationLoop()` is called twice, the frame rate is double as high as expected.

How are apps supposed to use more than one `WebGPURenderer` with a single global animation loop? 

AFAIK, if the example calls `setAnimationLoop()` only for a one renderer, the internal state of the second one isn't correct (since `NodeFrame.update()` isn't called and `renderer.info` isn't updated).

If the example uses an animation loop per renderer, which animation loop does it use to update its "global state"? No matter which one you use, the second animation loop might use frame-late data.

Maybe we need a way to trigger the update logic in the `Animation` class from outside somehow...

Edit: This is not only issue with multiple renderers. Consider an app that manages its animation loop separately and can't use `setAnimationLoop()`. How does it update the state of the renderer?
author: Mugen87

body:
I'm also battling a bit with this problem as I was planning to introduce `renderer.xr.setAnimationLoop()` (Quest Browser can have immersive + non immersive at the same time).

Where do the physics run? ­¤żö

We'll probably have to create an issue for this.
author: mrdoob

body:
> ..If the example uses an animation loop per renderer, which animation loop does it use to update its "global state"? No matter which one you use, the second animation loop might use frame-late data...

This should not be the expected result, `Animation` has a frame counter per Renderer, regardless of the user using `setAnimationLoop()` as would happen in `WebGLRenderer`, this ensures that the user can still use `requestAnimationFrame` without harming the events per frame in the `Nodes`.

> While migrating the examples towards setAnimationLoop() I have noticed an issue in this example. The render() function updates both renderers but since setAnimationLoop() is called twice, the frame rate is double as high as expected.

I think this is behavior is correct if two renderers depend on using `setAnimationLoop()`, both must be isolated, and this conflict of Node updates should not exist as we have an `Animation` and `NodeFrame` class per Renderer in `WebGPURenderer`. It could just use one `setAnimationLoop` or one `requestAnimationFrame` for multiples Renderers, if this doesn't work it could be a bug.

Although it is an unusual approach to use more than one device, practically no engine duplicates devices, perhaps the user could solve this with `RenderTargets`, scissor, etc. if the renderer is working and declared in the same thread, in case of different threads having independent `setAnimationLoop` should be more useful.

> I'm also battling a bit with this problem as I was planning to introduce renderer.xr.setAnimationLoop() (Quest Browser can have immersive + non immersive at the same time).

Shouldn't `Renderer.setAnimationLoop()` manage these calls internally, and we only have one function for this event?
author: sunag

body:
Um, my feeling is the update loop in `Animation` should _only_ run if the user does use `setAnimationLoop()`.

If the user manages the animation loop on app level and not via the renderer, the user should tell the renderer when to update its internal frame counter, the timer and other frame related components. 

A separate `Info` object which is managed on app level and injected into the renderer (or multiple renderers) could be indeed one solution.

body:
I'm okay with adding `compareFunction` to the ctor signature. However, does it make sense to add a default parameter like for most other texture parameters? 

Maybe `LessEqualCompare` since this would make the creation of the default texture in #27252 nicer.
author: Mugen87

body:
Unfortunately if we do this it will enable by default the depth compare function in the `WebGLTextures` class for all depth textures.

Normally the default in `WebGL2` is indeed `gl.LEQUAL` but `gl.TEXTURE_COMPARE_MODE` should be set to `gl.NONE` in that case for the comparaison to not be enabled. Right now we don't have a `texture.compareMode`, only `texture.compareFunction`, and I believe it would just add unnecessary complexity if we add this one too.

Which means that basically we probably have no choice but to use `null` by default as any other format such as `LessEqualCompare` would set the depth compare mode to `gl.TEXTURE_COMPARE_MODE` and so break as a depthTexture will be considered as a `sampler2DShadow` in the shader.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLTextures.js#L616-L621
author: RenaudRohlinger
Mugen87

body:
@Mugen87

> I'm okay with adding `compareFunction` to the ctor signature

I think we should stop adding parameters to constructors.
It doesn't scale and ends up with a messy ordering...
author: mrdoob

body:
Okay, that makes sense. In this case, https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/27252 can set the value of `compareFunction ` as described in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/27251#discussion_r1406215208. Meaning:

>  If you do emptyShadowTexture.compareFunction = LessEqualCompare; right after the texture creation, you don't need to set needsUpdate to true.

body:
Hi @Mugen87 , thanks for having a look!

Have you tested your code? Your argument about descendants makes sense conceptually, however in the current configuration I believe it would cause world matrix updates regardless of `.matrixWorldAutoUpdate`, because:
1. Scene calls `updateMatrixWorld()` on its descendants
2. Descendant checks `descendant.matrixAutoUpdate` and performs `descendant.updateMatrix()`
3. `updateMatrix()` sets `descendant.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate = true`
4. The next statement `if ( this.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate || this.matrixWorldAutoUpdate === true || force ) {` passes, once again regardless of `descendant.matrixWorldAutoUpdate`

In other words, as long as object has `object.matrixAutoUpdate` set to `true`, it will override `object.matrixWorldAutoUpdate`. If we wanted to go the way you propose and keep `object.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate` relevant, we would have to either:
a) eliminate `descendant.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate = true` from `updateMatrix()` or...
b) in the second `if` statement `if ( this.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate || this.matrixWorldAutoUpdate === true || force ) {` have `this.matrixWorldAutoUpdate` somehow override the `this.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate` flag if necessary.

My use-case (which has led to discovery of this bug) was setting skinned mesh's `rootBone.matrixWorldAutoUpdate = false` (`rootBone.matrixAutoUpdate` is left to default `true`) to prevent skeleton matrices updates and perform it manually when needed. The current way is convenient because setting `.matrixWorldAutoUpdate` on the root object automatically prevents world updates of its descendants. You might say it makes `.matrixAutoUpdate` obsolete because the local matrix wont be updated either, but I think there is no reason to update local matrix if we dont update world matrix, since it is the latter that is sent to the shader anyway.

Thus I believe it is the `object.matrixWorldAutoUpdate` that should take precedence over `object.matrixAutoUpdate`.

It would be less convenient (although maybe more logical? ­¤żö) if traversal of descendants happens regardless (in the case of your change), as then in order to prevent all world matrix updates, you'd have to first traverse the entire object and set `.matrixWorldAutoUpdate` to `false` on each and every descendant instead of just the root object.
I am not sure if it even makes sense for descendant to update world matrix 
```js
child.matrixWorld.multiplyMatrices( parent.matrixWorld, child.matrix );
```
if the `parent.matrixWorld` stayed the same. But maybe it makes sense for descendants deeper in the graph?
What would be some use-cases for this?­¤żö
author: DolphinIQ

body: I assumed `matrixAutoUpdate` and `matrixWorldAutoUpdate` are set to `false` at the same time if an app wants to control the update matrix process.
author: Mugen87

body: Side note: This entire thread is another example of why the flag based approach is cumbersome and too complicated. At some point, we should revisit the entire world matrix update computation and implement a solution based on dirty flags and caches and remove the update flags altogether. Even if this means some breaking changes like making `Object3D.matrix` private.
author: Mugen87
DolphinIQ

body:
> How would that dirty flags and caches solution work?

The first step would be to introduce dirty flags in `Vector3` and `Quaternion` so you know when `position`, `quaternion` or `scale` has been changed. Only then you recompute the local matrix which means `matrixAutoUpdate` could be removed. 

> Also I would avoid making Object3D.matrix private.

You could still set a matrix with a new method called `Object3D.setMatrix()`. It's just important that the `matrix` property itself isn't writable anymore otherwise we can't properly detect a state change.

We have tried to implement something like this in #14138 but stopped because of `Object3D.matrix` is writable. Besides, at that time I wanted to explore caching first since it requires no changes to the math classes. Turned out that caching is slower than the dirty flag approach.

I'm not sure we need this kind of validation. IMO, `UniformsGroup` should be used like geometries or textures. After the first render, devs should not alter the number of uniforms in their group. If this is required, they can call `dispose()` and create a new group. 

Changing the layout of uniforms means the shader changes so a call of `dispose()` on the respective shader material is required as well.

Um, I guess I would return the data type that is expected in the intersection object (e.g. `Vector2` or `Vector3`). Probably with initial numbers or maybe `NaN`. But if there can't be an intersection anyway, it probably doesn't matter.

body:
I'm fine with making this change but we should clarify how to move on with cases when files in `jsm/libs` require a modification. 

Normally, these files are just copied over and considered as read-only. If we modify them, they get out of sync compared to the original module. In this case: https://github.com/mrdoob/stats.js. The ideal workflow would be to modify the source in https://github.com/mrdoob/stats.js, make a release and then use the new build in `three.js`.

This workflow could be enforce by just deleting `examples/jsm/libs/stats.module.js` and import it directly from https://github.com/mrdoob/stats.js with a CDN like other external libs.

The comment needs fixing. Actually the problem is that compute passes can be nested and indeed are in the compute_points example, which is where I hit this issue:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/bd33e679a5b634842bb39a377b9a37eb68bc0e2a/examples/webgpu_compute_points.html#L121
author: aardgoose

body: I just thought about this again and I think the current approach is completely equivalent to always calling `enable` in `compute` (the only possibility is that it could be called multiple times -- but I guess it wouldn't break?) and `disable` in `finishCompute` (it already does this). Can you please check this?
author: LeviPesin

body:
WIth nested calls you get the sequence, seen in practice in compute_points: I originally had enable in discard in beginCompute().

```
beginCompute()
   beginCompute() <- called from onBeforeRender()
      compute()
   endCompute()
   compute() <- discard disabled by previous endCompute()
endCompute()
```
author: aardgoose

body:
> compute() <- discard disabled by previous endCompute()

No -- I'm suggesting that it be enabled in compute() instead of beginCompute() (currently it is the same).

body:
This line of code is only executed if the audio is playing _and_ if the audio has playback control (which is not true for media elements, streams or nodes). In other words, the application calls `play()` without an audio buffer which is wrong to begin with.

TBH, I don't feel the suggested fix is much helpful for users since it hides an erroneous situation. A better fix would be to throw an error at this point.

body:
This isn't done in our other color conversion functions (see [P3 to Linear sRGB conversion](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/4acb78002425969f7c0cf46423ea5f728c41dfbc/src/renderers/shaders/ShaderChunk/colorspace_pars_fragment.glsl.js#L23-L25)). Is it something we should add there, as well?

That aside - is it important that the values be clamped to in-gamut after this conversion? It's very likely for the Linear sRGB values to come into this function with values far greater that 1.0 and some effects such as screen space bloom depend on those larger-than 1 values being output to the render target.

_edit_

I see now that AgX is clamped, as well. I guess I'm in part confused by this line in your original post:

> This is required when mapping from the larger Rec.2020 gamut to the smaller Rec.709 gamut.

Is it important that we clamp that values after the _color space conversion_ or after _tone mapping_? In tone mapping we seem to be clamping these values. In other color space conversions we are not.
author: gkjohnson

body:
> This isn't done in our other color conversion functions (see P3 to Linear sRGB conversion). Is it something we should add there, as well?

I prefer to think of the color conversion functions themselves as open-domain-to-open-domain. But certainly any particular invocation of the functions needs to decide whether to clamp, gamut map, or keep the full domain.

> ... some effects such as screen space bloom depend on those larger-than 1 values being output to the render target

I'm trying hard to encourage users to put HDR effects like bloom before tone-mapping. See [forum discussion](https://discourse.threejs.org/t/different-color-output-when-rendering-to-webglrendertarget/57494/7). But yes, a lot of people have done this. My preference would mainly be consistency, whichever result that means. It looks like our [existing tone mappers](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/shaders/ShaderChunk/tonemapping_pars_fragment.glsl.js) mostly do clamp before color space conversion.

It seems the tests developers usually perform with these coordinates happen in world space. If the vectors are now in camera space, users have to convert their data into camera space as well OR convert the frustum corner points to world space.

`getFrustumCorners( camera, distance, topLeft, topRight, bottomRight, bottomLeft )`  - Mess is in signature.

`getFrustumCorners( camera, distance, target )` - Mess is shuffled into the description. 

Personally lean towards the latter though could understand preferences for either option ­¤½Ā.

>> Side Idea ­¤ÆĪ : Something new like `THREE.Quad/THREE.Rectangle` that specifically contains four Vector3s could be ideal but don't want to bloat the number of native classes for *just* this method.

---

Wonder about:

```javascript
getFrustumCorners( camera, distance, target = { 
  topLeft: new Vector2(), 
  topRight: new Vector2(), 
  bottomRight: new Vector2(), 
  bottomLeft: new Vector2() 
} )
```

This way memory control is still fully in the developer's hands and they can *either* pass in their own target or optionally not include a target and let the method create Vector2s on their behalf.
author: Bug-Reaper

body: As I said, `topRight`, `topLeft`, have no meaning in world space. Return the values in camera space. Any user using this method knows how to convert a point from camera space into world space -- if that is what they want.
author: WestLangley

body:
See it as a stretch to say `topLeft` fully looses all meaning if the camera is rotated? Agnostic of camera rotation or reference coordinate space, the topLeft vector will still be the point that is rendered to the topLeft of the screen.

Agree that if we return the results in camera-space it blunts the utility meant to be added here. Think world-space is the right play and that the nature of the top/bottom/left/right is still easily apparent.

body:
I'm struggling a bit with supporting just two influences. I fear we could quickly end up in a situation where this is reported as a bug/limitations and devs request full support. 

How would a solution with an additional texture look like?
author: Mugen87

body:
We would load all the influences in a float texture, like a row per instance with NUM_INFLUENCES floats and then fetch all of them in the shader using gl_InstanceId. Obviously this would be way slower than the current approach and how much slower would depend on the number of morph targets. For the simple cases like in the example it would need to fetch 20 floats(I think that's the count for the horse) from the texture vs just 4 as a vec4 attribute.

In the case of simple animations like the horse all but two of the influences will be zero so it's a big waste of bandwidth and from my experience this kind of morphing is the overwhelming majority of cases which would benefit from instancing. Stuff like animated pointers and little visual details that repeat a lot in the scene and just loop. 

We could always support both ways and allow the user to select with a flag. What do you think?

I can't really tell the difference here, but I am confused, and I suspect there should be some accommodation for the log encoding. I've left a comment on the blog post asking about it.
author: donmccurdy

body:
@WestLangley I received a response to [my question about the luminance coefficients](https://iolite-engine.com/blog_posts/minimal_agx_implementation) on the Minimal AgX blog past, and did some further reading. The coefficients come from [ASC CDL v1.2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ASC_CDL), which is a standardized representation of color grading looks. I haven't managed to find the actual text of ASC CDL v1.2, but the luminance coefficients are constant and based on Rec. 709 regardless of the working color space. This is not AgX-specific.


While very possibly more correct, the practical benefits of matching the coefficients to the working color space here are unclear to me. I do believe the log space encoding means we are not strictly dealing with Rec. 2020 here, either. The benefit of using Rec. 709 coefficients is the ability to import looks from other tools. I'm inclined to leave a comment about ASC-CDL v1.2 but to keep the coefficients of Rec. 709.

body:
I can confirm your PR fixes the issue but the approach isn't ideal, tbh. We need to find a solution _without_ influencing how the world transformation matrix is computed. 

If not, this will cause issues when we rethink the update matrix process and lifecycle in `three.js` and try to find a more optimized implementation. Ideally, components just request a world matrix and do not modify their computation.

In other words, `TransformControls` has to work with an unmodified version of the scene's transformation hierarchy. Fixing the controls in combination with rotated ancestor nodes has to be done in a way that the rotation is correctly taken into account and not ignored.

author: hamzakubba

body:
IMO, it's sufficient to highlight that bounding volumes should be recomputed whenever you transform instances. I would not like to see a list of use cases bounding volumes are intended for. The purpose of the docs is to document the API and not explain everything. There are books and guides for that.

That said, the manual might be a good place for more in-depth explanation of `InstancedMesh` (or instancing in general): https://threejs.org/manual/#en/fundamentals
author: Mugen87

body:
I don't really understand your perspective on this. You're saying that the API reference for this field should not explain what it is used for? Even though it is important information about this specific field?

I'm not looking for a listing of all possible use-cases for bounding volumes. I am talking about default visibility culling behavior of a graphics library, and there have already been issues with the lack of this information.
author: hamzakubba

body:
When devs updating their geometry data without thinking about bounding volumes, they run into all sorts of issues. Failing view frustum culling is only one issue of many. Ray casting won't work as expected as well. Next collision detection tests. You have stumbled across one issue of series of issues but that does not mean view frustum culling is the most important thing of use case of bounding volumes.

We have the policy that guides in the manual are the best place for explaining such things.
author: Mugen87

body: I think something that causes objects to disappear is worth mentioning in a graphics library API reference. I don't think we have to list everything, I'm just suggesting we list this one important thing that happens by default that affects the core functionality (rendering) in a significant way.
author: hamzakubba

body: I understand your concerns. Let me think about where to document this topic. We have similar issue in other areas, see #27713.
author: Mugen87

body:
> The bounding volumes should be updated whenever instances are transformed.

This is not clear. What are the "bounding volumes"? And what do they have to do with the method being documented?

> Depending on how you transform instances, the bounds can be larger or smaller than before and the bounding volumes should reflect that.

That statement is not helpful, and it provides no useful information.

@Mugen87 is correct. It is best not to head down this slippery slope. The API docs are not intended to be a tutorial.
author: WestLangley

body:
It's understandable that devs who struggle with an issue for hours demand a more detailed documentation. 

Given that `BufferGeometry`, `InstanedMesh` and `SkinnedMesh` do not update their bounding volumes automatically, I think we should somewhere explain that it's the task of the app to recompute the bounding sphere and box when the underlying data (vertices, instance transformations or bones) change.

If we note add a note in the documentation pages for `BufferGeometry`, `InstanedMesh` and `SkinnedMesh`, where should we mention this detail? Maybe in the [Update resources](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html#manual/en/introduction/How-to-update-things) guide? 

AFAICS, there is already a note for the bounding volumes of `BufferGeometry`.
author: Mugen87

body:
>It's understandable that devs who struggle with an issue for hours demand a more detailed documentation.

Yes, that seems reasonable.

If you want to add a comment about the responsibility of "your app", I think it is appropriate to add it in the documentation of the `computeBoundingBox/Sphere()` method _iteslf_.

You can link to "How to update things", instead -- or in addition --  if you want.

 I would revert this PR. I think you can do better, and I hope the OP would find it equally-acceptable.
author: WestLangley

body: Okay, I have reverted the PR. I'll give it a new go tomorrow ­¤æŹ .
author: Mugen87

body:
> If you want to add a comment about the responsibility of "your app", I think it is appropriate to add it in the documentation of the `computeBoundingBox/Sphere()` method _iteslf_.
> 
> You can link to "How to update things", instead -- or in addition -- if you want.
> 
> I would revert this PR. I think you can do better, and I hope the OP would find it equally-acceptable.

I disagree with you, and I think this is the wrong direction. For me as a library user, there is no reason to go read the documentation for `computeBoundingBox` unless I already know that I need that method. I don't think most people read the API reference from top to bottom on every class they use. As a library user I come to find the things I need. If I need to transform an instance, I look at the setMatrixAt method, and hopefully at its documentation. So to me it makes the most sense to have this critical information about transforming instancing here. If my instanced mesh suddenly vanishes I _might_ look at the documentation of the methods and classes I have used, but it might not even be clear at that point what the problem is (e.g. could be camera, or a shader, or a lot of other things). So having this information readily available on the methods it impacts makes sense to me.

I disagree that the previous version "is not helpful, and it provides no useful information". I think it was helpful to someone who doesn't understand concepts like bounding boxes and frustum culling as well as the library maintainers. Ironically, I think your statement was not helpful nor did it provide any useful information; there was no attempt to understand the reason behind my suggested changes or any suggestions to improve my attempt.

body: AFAIK, there is no system that supports `EXT_color_buffer_float ` but not `EXT_color_buffer_half_float`. Since the transmission render target only uses `HalfFloatType` and not `FloatType`, I'm afraid this addition does not make sense.
author: Mugen87

body:
Confusingly the `EXT_color_buffer_float` extension may imply that half float types are supported. From the [spec](https://registry.khronos.org/webgl/extensions/EXT_color_buffer_float/):

> The following floating-point internal formats become color-renderable: R16F, RG16F, RGBA16F, R32F, RG32F, RGBA32F and R11F_G11F_B10F. A renderbuffer or a texture with a color-renderable internal format can be used as a rendering target by attaching it to a framebuffer object as a color attachment.

If a device reports that `EXT_color_buffer_float` but for some reason doesn't report `EXT_color_buffer_half_float` we should still support rendering to half float buffers.

body: Then let's merge for the case a device only reports `EXT_color_buffer_float` support.

author: RenaudRohlinger

body:
Changing the way `width` and `height` is calculated impacts all usage of `copyTextureToTexture3D`, not just the compressed ones. This PR breaks existing code that relies on specifying exact texels to `_gl.*texSubImage3D`.

If the idea is to handle non-integer inputs in `sourceBox`, then at least it should say `Math.round(sourceBox.max.x - sourceBox.min.x + 1)`. Also, `sourceBox.min` is given directly to `_gl.pixelStorei`, as well as `position` to `_gl.*texSubImage3D`, so it still doesn't handle non-integer inputs.

Having the `width` and `height` calculation work differently from `depth` doesn't look right either.

I'd argue for reverting these two lines, but I also do not have extensive experience with the three.js codebase, so I might have missed something. Any thoughts, @RenaudRohlinger @Mugen87 ?
author: eiriklegernaes

body:
> This PR breaks existing code that relies on specifying exact texels to _gl.*texSubImage3D.

Do you mind sharing a live example that shows how this PR breaks the previous usage of `copyTextureToTexture3D()`? A debugging possibility would make it easier to reevaluate this change.

> Having the width and height calculation work differently from depth doesn't look right either.

That is a fair point. I'm not sure why the computation needs to differ. 

@RenaudRohlinger Was there a specific reason to just update the width and height but not the depth one?
author: Mugen87

body:
I can write up a live example, but it's essentially an off-by-one bug. Most visible if existing code uses `copyTextureToTexture3D` to update a single texel:

```
const box = new THREE.Box3(new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, 0), new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, 0));
renderer.copyTextureToTexture3D(box, box.min, srcTexture, dstTexture);
```

Prior to this PR, that would have given a `width`, `height` and `depth` of 1. With this PR, `width` and `height` is 0, while depth is `1`. 

I'm fine with the `sourceBox.max` being either "inclusive" or "exclusive", but it should be consistent across its x, y and z component.
author: eiriklegernaes

body:
> Why clear something thatŌĆÖs not there? Considering how different GPUs might react, keeping things simple and logical feels like the better move.

I'll defer to other opinions here but my opinion is that defensively adding code to do things we don't know are needed isn't a great policy. It adds more code which means more to understand (and in this case the purpose or intent isn't obvious) and more points for potential bugs to arise, however simple the logic may seem. I think there _are_ good reasons to not just add things that seem simple without an apparent need.

Regarding driver issues - I think you do have to trust the drivers to do something reasonable until it's shown they don't in some case. Otherwise what's to stop you from adding defensive code everywhere?

body:
We have moved `FontLoader` and `TextGeometry` out of the core since they are not very common classes. So I don't think adding them back to core would be good.

I wonder if we could somehow register a type for deserialization in `ObjectLoader`. Something like:
```js
const loader = new THREE.ObjectLoader();
loader.registerType( 'TextGeometry', TextGeometry );
```
This could be done manually in the editor before the scene is loaded/parsed. In this way, the loader does not require a hard dependency but type information can be injected at runtime by calling code.

Geometries.registerType( 'TextGeometry', TextGeometry );

> Each mipmap level of an array texture is a series of images. Each image within a mipmap is called a "layer".

body:
One of the reasons I originally held off on doing this was the ambiguity around how many values in the data array encode a single texel

> The interpretation of the data depends on type and format: If the type is THREE.UnsignedByteType, a Uint8Array will be useful for addressing the texel data. If the format is THREE.RGBAFormat, data needs four values for one texel; Red, Green, Blue and Alpha (typically the opacity).
For the packed types, THREE.UnsignedShort4444Type and THREE.UnsignedShort5551Type all color components of one texel can be addressed as bitfields within an integer element of a Uint16Array.
In order to use the types THREE.FloatType and THREE.HalfFloatType, the WebGL implementation must support the respective extensions OES_texture_float and OES_texture_half_float. In order to use THREE.LinearFilter for component-wise, bilinear interpolation of the texels based on these types, the WebGL extensions OES_texture_float_linear or OES_texture_half_float_linear must also be present.

https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/textures/DataArrayTexture

Is there existing code in ThreeJS I could reference which would describe for a given color space, how many values in the data array are used for a single texel? Otherwise, it would be challenging to correctly splice the texture array's layers.
author: HunterLarco

body:
I'd say something like:
```js
let formatSize = 1

if ( glFormat === gl.RG || glFormat === gl.RG_INTEGER ) formatSize = 2
if ( glFormat === gl.RGB ) formatSize = 3
if ( glFormat === gl.RGBA ) formatSize = 4
```
should do the trick? Also there are more formats, you can refer to `getInternalFormat` in `WebGLTextures` for this. I don't think the type matter, only the format of the texture if I understand correctly the issue.

Also we could try to do this in another PR if you prefer.

body:
Given that the example uses boxes of 0.075, a convex radius of 0.05 is indeed too big (the debug version of Jolt will assert because it is bigger than half the size of the box). So this solution is better as it reduces the convex radius to 0.00375.

The default allowed penetration in Jolt is 0.02 (PhysicsSettings.mPenetrationSlop) which means the boxes can still sink into each other by this amount. Also, this will not help performance as the allowed penetration is bigger than the convex radius which causes a more expensive code path to trigger more often. You can update the physics settings through PhysicsSystem.SetPhysicsSettings.
author: jrouwe

body:
Previously the ray direction was being calculated with the "origin" being calculated as "0, 0, 0" which is used to calculate a delta vector in the `setFromCamera` function. With the change in this PR the origin is at the near plane which is 1 unit from the camera origin resulting in a higher epsilon which Number.EPSILON was not high enough to test for. Lowering the near value lowers the values used in the origin vector and therefore the error in the calculations. From the [MDN page on Number.EPSILON](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Number/EPSILON):

> The Number.EPSILON static data property represents the difference between 1 and the smallest floating point number greater than 1.
author: gkjohnson

body: The [documentation](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html#api/en/materials/MeshPhysicalMaterial.ior) states that `MeshPhysicalMaterial.ior` has a range of `[1, 2.333]`. However, there are glTF assets with a higher IOR value. E.g.  https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF-Sample-Assets/tree/main/Models/DispersionTest uses `2.42`. Should we allow a higher range in the editor e.g. `[ 1, 3 ]`?
author: Mugen87

body:
Do we know where that range come from? The [glTF docs](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/extensions/2.0/Khronos/KHR_materials_ior/README.md) don't seem to specify an upper bound:

> Valid values for ior are numbers greater than or equal to 1. In addition, a value of 0 is allowed.

But I agree that a max of something like 3 is good enough.
author: gkjohnson

body:
glTF allows IOR values of 0, disallows values between 0 and 1 (exclusive), and allows 1 to infinity (inclusive).

https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/ae1a90baf009203760304f06ed5645402de9dc09/extensions/2.0/Khronos/KHR_materials_ior/schema/material.KHR_materials_ior.schema.json#L12-L20

2.42 is a typical value for diamonds according the KHR_materials_ior spec, but values >2 are otherwise physically unusual. Occasionally I see glTF files containing ior=0 or ior=1000+, these are not physically-plausible but are occasionally useful to give ...

> "...full weight to layer, i.e., the Fresnel term evaluates to 1 independent of the view or light direction. It is useful in combination with KHR_materials_specular to seamlessly support the specular-glossiness workflow."

In other words, it's a trick that is very useful when converting materials from the spec/gloss PBR workflow to the metal/rough workflow.
author: donmccurdy

body: Thanks for the feedback. I'll merge the PR with the current settings so it aligns to the documentation. But it seems we should reconsider the current IOR range and think about adapting the glTF policy.

body:
It would be nice if we could add these methods without all the code duplication.

BTW: Enabling the usage of different coding pattern is fine but how is the performance of generators in the meanwhile? I remember it took a while until they got optimized but I wonder if the new methods are on par with the existing ones.
author: Mugen87

body:
I'm not sure how we could avoid the duplication here to be quite honest. Most of the business logic is inherently different due to the usage of generators so even though it might look similar (and be so for 2 lines), I fear we might incur overhead in trying to merge the regular and generator versions. I'm not the best JS developer though - far from it - so if you see a way to do so with no overhead I'd be interested.

Generators absolutely have worse performance in certain cases (last I remember for a full walk through a simple list of integers I was getting around +50% time with generators) but can be an invaluable time save if the calculation required per item is rather heavy. Not only that, but with generators we go from O(n) spacial complexity to O(1), which can be an interesting benefit in niche cases. Depending on the situation, going from the regular traversal to a generator-based one requires very little refactoring on the user's side for a possibly significant performance boost.
I'll run some performance tests again just in case and will comment the results.
author: HoloTheDrunk

body:
I've run two simple tests just as a proof of the easily obtainable performance benefits of generators in certain use cases.
Note that the `array` examples are not what the `traverse` method is doing since it does not return anything, but since this PR is about the benefits of stopping computation early in an ergonomic way I felt it was a more appropriate comparison (since afaik it's currently impossible to stop the traversal midway given that the callback's return value isn't used).
 
In a [simple case](https://jsbench.me/gmlus7oq9q) with barely any computation for each element the array version fairly obviously far outclasses the generator by about 60-80%.
However, in a [slightly more complex case](https://jsbench.me/g7lus9gsth) (that is still a far cry from real world applications) the time lost by the generator pattern is so negligible in comparison to the value mapping and checking that it ends up being ~15% faster.

Generators are not the right choice for all problems, but when they are the benefits are non-negligible.
author: HoloTheDrunk
donmccurdy

author: Mugen87
Mugen87

body:
> In context of transmission, the transmission render target should now have the same size as camera.viewport. So is the temporary removal of camera.viewport really necessary?

@Mugen87 The `camera.viewport` holds both the size _and_ offset. It's the offset that trips up the transmission rendering. The `Reflector` first restores the transmission render target, at which point the viewport is also (temporarily) correct. Directly afterwards it notices the `camera.viewport` and "restores" the viewport. For the left eye this should still all line up, but for the right eye the offset basically lies entirely outside of the bounds of the transmission render target.

In the `webxr_vr_sandbox` example it's quite difficult to see the difference. Since the viewport is only incorrect _after_ the Reflector is rendered in the right eye, the transmission render target for the right eye will already have the background rendered into it, so it doesn't look completely off.

Hope this clarifies it

body:
After a final review I see one issue. There is the line:
```js
if ( _clippingEnabled === true ) clipping.setGlobalState( _this.clippingPlanes, camera );
```
which was previously executed _before_ `renderTransmissionPass()` was called. With this PR global clipping planes are configured _after_ the transmission pass which isn't right, imo. If you define global clipping planes, opaque objects might be clipped in the normal pass but not when seeing them through a transmissive object. So I think the `setGlobalState()` call needs to be moved as well.
author: Mugen87

body: Since the call of `setGlobalState()` is camera-dependant, I would suggest to copy it into `renderTransmissionPass()`.
author: Mugen87

body:
You're right, it should be set beforehand. While I can see some use-case for only clipping in the normal pass, there's no reason for this PR to change the current behaviour.

Just moving this line won't be enough as the global clipping planes depend on the camera. For stereo rendering this means it'll have to be updated each time we switch eye. I'll replicating this line in `renderTransmissionPass()` as that should do the trick.

P.S. It seems global clipping planes are broken with nested render calls. Opened an issue for it: #28111

body:
You can use the merge or rebase command. Normally you start by fetching the latest commit from the upstream repository:
```
git fetch upstream
```
You can then merge the latest changes on `dev` into your branch:
```
git merge upstream/dev
```
Or perform a rebase:
```
git rebase upstream/dev
```
The are many resources online which explain the difference between merge and rebase. Both have their pros and cons. However, rebase is ideal for situations when you want to move the point where you have branched to a new starting point. This is exactly what you are looking for in this instance.

body:
ok. lets craft a poor server to demo the edge case :D

<details><summary>poor_server.js</summary>

```js
// poor_server.js
require('http').createServer((req, res) => {
  if (req.url === '/a.json') {
    res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' })
    res.write('{') // imcomplete json
    setTimeout(() => req.connection.destroy(), 2000) // disconnect for some reason
    return
  }

  res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'text/html' })
  res.end(`
  <script type='module'>
    import * as THREE from 'https://esm.sh/three@0.163'
    const fileLoader = new THREE.FileLoader()
    fileLoader.load('a.json', ()=>{}, ()=>{}, console.log) // console won't log (<<the edge case)
  </script>`)
}).listen(9999)
``` 
</details>

1. $ node poor_server.js
2. nav to localhost:9999
3. open console
4. it should prompt `Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: network error` in ~2s

`inherit` simply means 'uses the same value of my parent element's one'.

Its upside is that, in our case ... *the background property is now dominant by single element, the `#menubar`* ... good for maintenance. 

Without `inherit`, the background property will be copied and pasted many places, in our case, this background property **has to** set on `#menubar`, `#menubar title`, `#menubar options option` etc, note that [we have to repeat that for dark mode too](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/79c34909e6af9250c38182665e3bd4896149f0ea/editor/css/main.css#L574) (effort x2)

The thing is `initTexture()` is not supposed to be called with an instance of render target so I'm not sure about this change. You have mentioned this in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/28282#issuecomment-2094832435 but we have a long-standing policy to _not_ use render target as textures and I don't think we should change this policy. Hence, `copyTextureToTexture()` should not be used with render targets but with their texture property.

How about we add `WebGLRenderer.initRenderTarget()` instead? Devs can then call this method in their app before using `copyTextureToTexture()` with an uninitialized render target.

author: Mugen87

body:
> why changes to MTLLoader are necessary in the first place?

<details>
  <summary>Why changes to MTLLoader.js are necessary</summary>

In short, MTLLoader's `create()` doesn't bind filename to any Texture{} property, not even in userData, so UITexture has no ways to derive filename from a Texture{}. It can be solved by exposing filename to say `Texture{}.name` in MTLLoader.
</details>

<details>
  <summary>Why changes to Loader.js are necessary</summary>

  In short, MTLLoader cannot reach to the unzipped content holding in `zip`, a plain object; It can be solved by supplying LoadingManager to MTLLoader in Loader.js 
</details>

body:
The listing is just extracted from `case`s of this `switch`, i think its not too hard to maintain... : https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/79c34909e6af9250c38182665e3bd4896149f0ea/editor/js/Loader.js#L80

If those supported formats already documented somewhere (?), then I can `prompt()` users like this instead:
```
Failed to import file: Unsupported file format (xxx)

Go [that link] to see full supported formats?

[OK] [Cancel]
```
I prefer showing hint (list or link) alongside the reason in same prompt; otherwise, it wastes users time trail-and-error, googling, etc.
author: ycw

body:
> btw, is supported formats list documented somewhere?

No, and I don't think documenting is worth the effort. I understand it's desirable to have such things documented but according to my experience it's always a pain to keep these lists up to date (e.g. like supported WebGL extensions, supported WebGL 2 features, the Web APIs used by the engine). Eventually we have always removed such lists because they got out-of-sync. Especially since enthusiastic contributors who added such listings in the first placed vanished and didn't care about them anymore. So it was the core team that had to endure the complains from user side (why is your stuff out-of-sync?!?) and maintain all these things. After all, I prefer to have as less as support intensive documentation in the project as possible.

author: WestLangley

body:
Roger, happy to make the change. (I'm not an owner, your preference is my command)

The reasoning I use is this:

- for permanent required things, separate parameters is fine
- for optional things ("options") use a trailing last parameter with all the rest of the "options"
  - The advantage of this is that then we don't have this:
    ```js
    foo(requiredValue, undefined, undefined, someValue, undefined, otherValue)
    ```

body: I think for most models, you want it to be anchored to the floor. Having a model snap to the wall may be confusing if it was not intended, so having it true by default make sense to me. Having the model snap to the wall should be explicitly stated in my opinion. For model-viewer tag, default is ar-placement="floor", and you can change it to ar-placement="wall" (working currently for Android WebXR). For iOS using a-placement="wall" will set includeAnchoringProperties=false in a future PR I'll do once r165 is released.

body: Maybe the default should be configurable with a static property `Layers.DEFAULT_RECURSIVE`?
author: CodyJasonBennett

body: I'm personally not a fan of `DEFAULT_` static properties since one could argue to introduce them for all normal properties to configure their default values. That makes the code more verbose and unnecessary complex.
author: Mugen87

body:
I think the property has to be particularly notable and value contentious to be worth reconfiguring.

Ideally, this would work in user-land for cases like https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/18937#issuecomment-602252153:

```js
THREE.Layers.prototype.recursive = true;

console.log( new THREE.Layers().recursive ) // false
```
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
The problem is that this argumentation could be done for any other property as well. It's best if @mrdoob makes a design decision here. I'm not sure how he feels about the `DEFAULT_` properties and to what extend they should be used.

The intended code should also look like so since ideally devs do not access the `prototype` object:
```js
const layers = new THREE.Layers();
layers.recursive = true;
```
I understand you have to set `recursive` to `true` for each instance but that doesn't seem like a real burden to me, tbh.
author: Mugen87

body: Not every property has a highly contentious default value, so I have to disagree here on making this a general argument against static properties. This was my compromise for #18937 since I see a lot of resistance against changing the default to `true`, but if we can't allow this by configuration, then I propose making this the default instead.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
According to https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/19012#issuecomment-613777171, we want to keep `false` as the default.

This is also my expected behavior of layers, tbh.
author: Mugen87

body: I'd like to put three users on the happy path and find compromises which don't lead them into footguns undermining performance or ease-of-use, and I'm especially frustrated to fight tooth and nail to advocate for them. That entire thread is proof of disagreement around a default value, which suggests to me this should be configurable. Setting layers per object is only error-prone for their case, and this discussion alone infinitely more energy than adding a static member.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body: If this isn't even satisfactory for @gkjohnson, then I can simply retract this PR. I'm not aware of how this is handled in user-land today.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
TBH, I never use layers recursively and if so I would probably traverse through the object hierarchy once and copy the layers to all objects that require the same mask. Granted a property that makes things configurable is easier to use but the PR also proves an increase in complexity in _all_ components checking layers.

I'm not sure how common it is to user layers recursively in order to justify this complexity. I'm also not aware how common it is to mix recursive layer usage with non-recursive usage. If this is more a global setting then I agree a static `DEFAULT_RECURSIVE` makes sense.

However, I would really like to hear @mrdoob's opinion on this change and also how he feels about the additional checks in all renderers and raycaster.

body: Or we just always use the plural? I personally don't see `1` samples as an issue which should be fixed with a separate code path.
author: Mugen87

body:
The issue about using `(s)` for just 'Sample(s)' is that, that unit will be inconsistent with other units formatted in the same area:

```
1 object
1 vertex
0 triangles
1 sample(s)
```

The reason that we can't apply `(s)` for other units has been mentioned https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/28432#discussion_r1606522848

If handling of plural rules causes readability problems, then I prefer taking them into a function: **(update: this function is now implemented in [54a4957](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/commit/54a4957a3e9966857bb2a13929e801d013b66828))**

```
function setInfo( quantity, unit ) { // ... set UI text values with proper format ... }
```
author: ycw

body: Then I vote for always using plural (without brackets).
author: Mugen87

body: Note that the transition from 0 > 1 > 2 samples is noticeable in low-end devices, so 'always use plural' like `1samples` will confuse users that the quantity is incorrect.

body:
If you want to add your unit test, you have to revert some parts of #23352.

- Create an `addons/utils` folder in `unit` and place your tests there.
- Create `test/unit/three.addons.unit.js` and add `BufferGeometryUtils.tests.js`.
- Introduce a new script `test-unit-addons` in `package.json` which should look like so:

```
"test-unit-addons": "qunit -r failonlyreporter -f !-webonly test/unit/three.addons.unit.js",
```

- Add the new script to the `test` script.

```
"test": "npm run lint && npm run test-unit && npm run test-unit-addons",
```

author: donmccurdy

I realize it's implied by the word "unique", but just to spell it out: ```suggestion [page:Integer maxVertexCount] - the max number of vertices to be used by all unique geometries. Additional instances of a geometry do not contribute toward the vertex limit.
[page:Integer maxIndexCount] - the max number of indices to be used by all unique geometries. Additional instances of a geometry do not contribute toward the index limit.
```
author: donmccurdy

body:
Typo:

```suggestion
			Adds a new instance to the [name] using the same geometry from the given id and returns the associated index referring to it to be used
```
author: donmccurdy

body:
Wouldn't "geometry" be the right term in this case? Questions:

1. We're using the terms "index" and "id" interchangeably in this section, which do we prefer?
2. When calling `setGeometryAt` for an ID previously allocated with `addGeometry`, are instances of that geometry affected?
3. When calling `setGeometryAt` for an ID previously allocated with `addInstance`, is any other instance affected? 
4. Is an "instance of an instance" different in any way from an instance created from the same base geometry?

We could consider not supporting creation of "instances of instances" quite yet, if the preferred answers to these questions are non-obvious.
author: donmccurdy

body:
This line answers some of my questions above, but now I'm wondering. Is this by design, by technical requirement, or undecided?

Suppose a BatchedMesh represents a forest with instances of various tree species, each having a high-res and low-res geometry available. Trees within 100m use a high-poly geometry, and those further away use low-res geometries. When an instance crosses the 100m radius I might want to swap out the geometry of that instance alone, which may be difficult within this API. I expect it would be less common to want to the shared geometry of all instances at once, but I'm not sure.
author: donmccurdy
gkjohnson

body:
> In the LoD case you're referring to users could just hide and show associated with different LoDs but I agree that's not ideal if only because of memory usage ...

Yes, it's currently a similar API InstancedMesh. To support a similar use case there we need multiple InstancedMesh objects, and move the instance matrix between them to 'change' that instance.  I think it can be confusing that users must move the instance matrix around to change the geometry of the instance, and this feels similar.

I like the API direction above. Maybe in the interest of getting a first version out sooner, what do you think of saying...

1. `addGeometry` returns a geometry ID
2. `addInstance` takes a geometry ID and returns an instance ID
3. instance IDs and geometry IDs are not interchangeable (at least for now)

.... and perhaps we do a separate PR for `setGeometryForInstance` to keep things simpler. I think the API `setGeometryAt( id, geometry )` could also work, we'd just need to keep a `WeakMap<BufferGeometry, number>` for the lookups, maybe a little overhead involved.

> The downsides are that this will be a bit more verbose and be a breaking change. 

What is the breaking change compared to today? That calling `addGeometry` alone doesn't cause anything to render?
author: donmccurdy
donmccurdy

body:
> - addGeometry returns a geometry ID
> - addInstance takes a geometry ID and returns an instance ID
> - instance IDs and geometry IDs are not interchangeable (at least for now)

Yes this is what I would propose for now if we're going to change the API. We can add more functions later.

> I think the API setGeometryAt( id, geometry ) could also work

Perhaps as an option? But it shouldn't be required since once the geometry is in the batched mesh you should want to be able to discard / GC the original geometry to save memory. This can also be done by a user abstraction.

> What is the breaking change compared to today? That calling addGeometry alone doesn't cause anything to render?

Yeah - just this. The user must now call "addInstance" after "addGeometry".

> If the last instance of a geometry is removed, all references are lost, and it's eligible for some hypothetical future cleanup process.

This is basically what's happening now. But I don't think I love the idea of requiring that users keep geometry around once it's been added to the BatchedMesh. As I mention above I think one expected benefit is that you can GC it after.

I'm inclined to change the API to require calling `addInstance` if that sounds good to you?
author: gkjohnson

body: Especially given the requirement of instantiating each geometry, I think the term "instances" over "items" makes most sense.

body:
No changes here, but perhaps the future API is:

- `setGeometryAt( geometryId, geometry )` overwrites a geometry (up to the allocated vertex count), and affects all instances of that geometry
- `setInstanceAt( instanceId, geometryId )` changes the geometry instantiated by a specific instance ID.

author: WestLangley

body:
Can you explain how the previous commits were _not_ correct? It was sorting in the camera clip space (before w divide) which is arguably more correct since this is where the triangle projection and rasterization is actually happening. The fundamental issue is that dividing by w can flip the sign of the object origin used to sort.

This is what was done before the commits were overwritten:

> ```js
> _vec3.setFromMatrixPosition( object.matrixWorld );
>
> // applying matrix 4 to vec 3 implicitly divides by w
> _vec4.copy( _vec3 ).applyMatrix4( _projScreenMatrix ); 
>
> // sort on _vec4.z ...
> ```
author: gkjohnson

body:
One comment on the code here is that if we don't want to add a new function for vector4 to get the translation column is that this can still be written in one line like so, though I'll defer to others preferences on style here:

```js
_vec4.set( 0, 0, 0, 1 )
  .applyMatrix4( object.matrixWorld )
  .applyMatrix4( _projScreenMatrix ); 
```

body:
> Simply doing `texture = new Texture();` is better, IMO.


`const loader = this.manager.getHandler( `.${extension}` );` will return null for native images which are supposed to be handled without user adding default TextureLoader to the regex map for native extensions.
That's why there is this fallback : 
`texture = this.textureLoader.load( fileName );`
@mrdoob would eventually like to not return null for native images, but that would require prepopulating the regex map with default TextureLoader. My only fear that I can think of with that is that the list of native extensions would not be comprehensive enough, preventing handling images that the browser could have handled.
If we'd like to set default TextureLoader for native images (which I'm afraid of possible other implications) we have to make sure to not override an eventual existing loader if it was setup by the user
author: catalin-enache

body:
Couldn't we have a second set with native extensions intended for `TextureLoader` (or `ImageBitmapLoader`). Meaning `[ 'png', 'jpeg', ' jpg', 'gif', 'avif', 'webp' ]`. If an extension is not in that set, we check the non-native one ( `[ 'tga', 'tif', 'tiff', 'exr', 'dds', 'hdr', 'ktx2' ]`). If the check is `false`, we return an empty texture.

> My only fear that I can think of with that is that the list of native extensions would not be comprehensive enough, preventing handling images that the browser could have handled.

If we ever have an issue that something in the set is missing, we can add an entry.

body:
`listenToProperties` adds an on-read and on-write callback to named keys (not a deopt), which we use here to set `matrixNeedsUpdate` when transform properties are modified. On-read is used to convert quaternion/rotation like before, but only if their counterpart is dirty when read, preventing many unneeded conversions on mutation (which can be many times). Notably, if users stick to `quaternion`, they will avoid this conversion entirely. This is nice so we don't have to modify math classes like before.

`listenToMethods` I have as a special case for the local matrix whose members would be deopt if I just listened to all elements in its array. This is because array accessors are not named and unstable, so v8 would need to create a layer of indirection to make them stable. We want `matrixWorldNeedsUpdate` to be set whenever the local matrix updates to preserve existing behavior, so I instead handle the cases where local matrix is modified by its methods (which are named keys), and don't handle the case where `matrix.elements` is mutated directly (`matrixWorldNeedsUpdate` has to then be set by the user). This keeps us on the fast path for common usage without breaking APIs who expected a 1-1 relationship between local and world matrix updates.

body:
This identical checking here is to distinguish command path from regular path, and then decides that should point rows be re-generated or not. Blindly re-generating point rows will cause bug in regular path (see below).

Command path: The points value won't be identical there:

```
undo/execute SetGeometryCommand  # geometry is updated
   dispatch geometryChanged
      call refreshUI()
          call points.setValue(geometry.parameter.path.points # << not identical to points.getValue()
```
In this case, re-generating point rows in UIPoints is needed because UI out-synced its data.

Regular path: The points will be identical there:

```
on number input changed
  call update()
    execute SetGeometryCommand
      dispath geometryChanged
        call refreshUI()
          call points.setValue(geometry.parameter.path.points # << identical to points.getValue()
```

In this case, **re-generating point rows in UIPoints is not needed** because UI already synced its data, **otherwise, (BUG) users will slide a ghost input** (users are sliding an old input while UI is showing the new one): 

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/1063018/fddc96a8-881d-4608-a830-e4e263a531d3

To conclude, this identical checking is needed, not just for perf, but also avoiding abovementioned bug.

body:
In `Attribute.js` the buffer is updated if the version is different _or_ the usage is `DynamicDrawUsage `. Do you mind explaining why the check is here different? Shouldn't it be:
```js
if ( this.instanceMesh.instanceMatrix.usage === DynamicDrawUsage || this.instanceMesh.instanceMatrix.version !== this.buffer.version ) {
```
author: Mugen87

body:
The first condition `bufferAttribute.usage === DynamicDrawUsage` in `Attributes.js` is sufficient for InstanceNode to work if the usage of instanceMatrix is initialized with DynamicDrawUsage. This is because the usage is forwarded when the node is setup:
```js
const bufferFn = instanceAttribute.usage === DynamicDrawUsage ? instancedDynamicBufferAttribute : instancedBufferAttribute;
```

This means that forwarding the version in the `update()` method to the isolated interleavedBuffer is not necessary for the node to work when the usage is dynamic. That's why the condition uses `this.instanceMesh.instanceMatrix.usage !== DynamicDrawUsage` to forward the version only if the attribute is "static".

Also this code is necessary because the interleavedBuffer used in `InstanceNode` is isolated inside the node, and so bumping the version of `this.instanceMesh.instanceMatrix` manually with `needsUpdate = true` has no effect.
author: RenaudRohlinger

body:
`Lut3DNode` is an effect that comes _after_ tone mapping and sRGB color space conversion. Right now, I had to disable the settings on renderer level and apply them as TSL in this line. I have the feeling this is not the best approach.

Other passes like FXAA also require sRGB input so we need the same logic there as well. Making a separate `OutputNode` which extracts the renderer settings like the previous `OutputPass` could be one solution. However, if such a node is present, the default output tone mapping and color space conversion must be disabled.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/3e8a091b30baf2e4369145789db91127349f1ddb/src/renderers/common/nodes/Nodes.js#L394-L420
author: Mugen87

body:
It seems `Renderer` does currently _always_ a separate pass to apply tone mapping and color space conversion.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/3e8a091b30baf2e4369145789db91127349f1ddb/src/renderers/common/Renderer.js#L684-L688

Um, this is actually not ideal. When doing post processing, tone mapping and color space can (and should) be combined in the pass chain.

Besides, it is in general not preferable for the performance to abandon _inline_ tone mapping and color space conversion since we end up with an additional pass. What was the reason for doing this?
author: Mugen87

body:
As a first step I propose `OutputNode` so the renderer settings are honored and the node itself can freely added to the pass chain.

The inline tone mapping and color space conversion is a different topic and can be discussed separately. Doing it with a separate pass fixes #23019  but performance should not be neglected.
author: Mugen87

body:
> Um, this is actually not ideal. When doing post processing, tone mapping and color space can (and should) be combined in the pass chain.

This benefits refraction when used by avoiding double rendering of opaque objects improving performance, and increases the possibilities of sharing the same shaders as it reduces the final shader code for each material, consequently saves gpu memory. The benefits are quite considerable compared to the previous approach. https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/27880

I think some input needs sRGB we can do this conversion at the Node input, this would be even more reliable since all RenderTargers use the same color space.
author: sunag

body:
> The inline tone mapping and color space conversion is a different topic and can be discussed separately. Doing it with a separate pass fixes https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23019 but performance should not be neglected.

I believe that neglecting performance would be a point of view, for example, if we focus on performance with opaque objects running on the screen without post-processing, inline tonemapping would make sense (although I can't see how applying sRGB and tone-mapping separately could harm performance so much, especially in the context of webgpu where frame buffers are also textures and we don't use global states). On the other hand, if we focus on a scene where we will have PBR, transparent and opaque materials in the same scene, and with the possibility of effects accessing the already rendered buffer, maintaining performance even with a lot of objects in the same scene, and in an easy way as the examples of backdrop and `viewportTexture()` show us and with PostProcessing, this is the way. The difference in the end is that WebGPURenderer uses post-processing as default, nothing different from that. All these resources are aligned with this new architecture, and if we use PostProcessing with a custom tone-mapping this pass does not need to be necessary. The question I asked myself was what the renderer is prioritizing as performance.
author: sunag

body:
Thanks for sharing the reference to https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/27880. I recall now the motivation behind the new approach and indeed it makes sense. 

But the graphic from the post assumes sRGB/tone mapping comes after post processing which is not always true. I think this bit needs to be revisited.

> I think some input needs sRGB we can do this conversion at the Node input, this would be even more reliable since all RenderTargers use the same color space.

The problem is even if you ensure sRGB input for something like a LUT effect, the renderer must ensure that after the effect has been applied the output color space conversion and tone mapping is not executed anymore. In the example,  I had to manually set `THREE.NoToneMapping` and `THREE.LinearSRGBColorSpace` at the renderer to ensure this. Otherwise color space conversion and tone mapping is applied twice.

It seems more consistent to me to define output color space and tone mapping at renderer level and the post processing should honor these settings. The auto conversion of the node input that you have mentioned should be able to evaluate the renderer settings, right?
author: Mugen87

body:
> The difference in the end is that WebGPURenderer uses post-processing as default, nothing different from that. All these resources are aligned with this new architecture, and if we use PostProcessing with a custom tone-mapping this pass does not need to be necessary.

My point is if the app uses `PostProcessing` a separate output pass by the renderer is not really necessary. It's like always having a separately executed `OutputPass` in the pass chain with the pervious composer. My hope was that we can get rid of this extra render pass with the new system, tbh.
author: Mugen87

body:
> My point is if the app uses PostProcessing a separate output pass by the renderer is not really necessary. It's like always having a separately executed OutputPass in the pass chain with the pervious composer. My hope was that we can get rid of this extra render pass with the new system, tbh.

Make sense, that's a good idea, I think we can do that in the PostProcessing class.
