author: vegarringdal

body:
Coming back around to this -

> Dont have any real numbers to show in my app without a lot of rewrites, think it needed a additional attribute for the colors if I dont remember wrong

I think generally I expect something concrete to demonstrate why this is helpful. Comments like "I remember it being slow" makes a problem difficult to verify and ensure a suggested change is actually causing an issue. That said there shouldn't be any app changes required to provide what I asked for:

> "my batched mesh has X number of nodes so it's Y big, taking up Z MB"

The matrices texture requires 16 floats per instance which amounts to ***~22.4MB*** when 350K instances are used or when ***256MB*** there are 4 million instances even before the textures are forced to a power of two:

```js
instanceCount * 16 * 4 * 1e-6
```

Those numbers are fairly significant if the transformations aren't being used and to me seem like a worthwhile addition especially in cases like CAD or BIM rendering where memory is already tight. We will need to modify the WebGL code path as well, though. https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/29084 can be referenced for the WebGL changes - but perhaps they can happen in a separate PR.

Also I'm not familiar with the TSL code path but last I heard `BatchedMesh` wasn't quite fully supported, yet (or at least not performant). Perhaps that's changed?

body:
One issue is that XR rendering requires specific rendering setups that can't be configured with the usual renderer API. It becomes more complicated the more XR features (like layers, multi-view) the engine supports.

The renderer interface `XRManager` requires does not have to be public. We could name the method `_resetXRState()` and treat it as a pure internal method.

body:
I'm not sure about automatically use `GODOT_single_root` with just one 3D object. That might break the exporter for developers who rely on the current behavior. I would suggest 

- only update `GLTFLoader` for now.
- or enable usage of `GODOT_single_root` with a new exporter option
- or make use of `userData.gltfExtensions` which would be defined for the single 3D object. If that works with `GODOT_single_root`, that is probably sufficient for now.
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/5bf53eec7836b1dc4ebaff478929e1970c258d92/examples/jsm/exporters/GLTFExporter.js#L3569
author: Mugen87

body: Ah, that makes a lot of sense. I'll use the code in your suggestion, but it also occurred to me to use `const extensionsUsed = this.json.extensionsUsed || [];`.
author: aaronfranke

body:
I don't think `userData.gltfExtensions` is appropriate, because that would place `"extensions": { "GODOT_single_root: {} }` on a glTF node, which is incorrect.

I would argue it should be enabled by default, since it significantly improves behavior when the flag is recognized, has no effect when unrecognized, and is a flag users likely won't know to reach out for. But sure I can change this. I've pushed a commit that changes this to an exporter option, false by default.
author: aaronfranke

body:
Actually, I updated the PR again, this time completely removing the export code.

Three.js exports the root node as the last node, while GODOT_single_root requires that the node at index 0 is the root node. Before we can flag GODOT_single_root, we would need to change the export code to place the nodes in a different order. That is worth changing as its own PR separately from GODOT_single_root. The current status quo is that UnityGLTF and Godot both put the root at index 0, but Three.js and Blender put it as the last node.

After https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/31125 I can add this back in, or do it in a separate PR.

body:
Do you really need `_forceViewPort`? Since you are not changing the drawing buffer when resizing the renderer during `XRManager.renderLayers()`, the configured viewport (which uses the layer's width and height) should be different than the drawing buffer size and thus set `RenderContext.viewport` to `true`.

`RenderContext.viewport` only indicates the viewport update should use the value from the render context (and not the drawing buffer size).
author: Mugen87

body: What does AVP stand for?
author: Mugen87

body: I think you can move this line in the below `if` block. `setSize()` only matters if rendering into the default framebuffer. When rendering into a render target defined via  `setRenderTarget()`, the dimensions from the render target are used.
author: Mugen87

body:
I wonder if we should change the renderer so `setOutputRenderTarget()` behaves like `setRenderTarget()`. Meaning, when an output render target is defined, the dimensions of the internal framebuffer target is derived from it. Right now, we only do this for `depth`:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/2d978cb7ee65e4882ddfce48b761eb43893072fc/src/renderers/common/Renderer.js#L1246

However, keeping the dimensions of the internal framebuffer target in sync with the output render target could simplify things by making certain `setSize()` calls obsolete.

 We use `setOutputRenderTarget()` only in context of XR right now (to define a different output target than the default framebuffer) so if you think that design would be helpful we maybe can give it a shot.

author: cabanier

body:
The implementation of `getDrawingBufferSize()` is correct, imo. I had to read the code multiple times to understand why you need `_forceViewPort`.

I think you can remove it when the following lines:
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/2d978cb7ee65e4882ddfce48b761eb43893072fc/src/renderers/webgl-fallback/WebGLBackend.js#L455
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/2d978cb7ee65e4882ddfce48b761eb43893072fc/src/renderers/webgl-fallback/WebGLBackend.js#L611
are refactored to:
```js
const { width, height } = this.getDrawingBufferSize( _drawingBufferSize );
state.viewport( 0, 0, width, height );
```
If you don't update the DOM element when setting the size for layers, `gl.drawingBufferWidth` and `gl.drawingBufferHeight` are incorrect. Using `getDrawingBufferSize()` should report the correct values.

body: Why do we have `isArrayTexture` and `isTextureArray` now? Can't you use the existing `isArrayTexture`?
author: Mugen87

body:
I generally only use what is already available as a parameter, so I used isTextureArray.
screenshot from a StorageTexture node with r176 without my extension
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3e78233d-b5e4-43c9-9d3e-bb356db037c7)

If isArrayTexture were available, I would have used it. I can, of course, implement it, and that's why I'm asking. But then there would be two indicators for the array state in the StorageTextures, which I don't think is right, because then both would have to be set.
I assume there were reasons why isTextureArray was introduced, but I don't know them, and that's why I'm asking. One indicator is certainly better than two. 

I could trace isTextureArray back in the code to where it plays a role. Perhaps it was simply created but not used anywhere yet, since storage textures aren't array-capable yet. Then we could remove isTextureArray from the code and replace it with isArrayTexture.
author: Spiri0

body: I think the default should be 1? and array more than 1...
author: sunag

body:
The reason I chose 0 was because someone might come up with the idea of ŌĆŗŌĆŗwanting to define an array with a length of 1, for example, when providing an app with a dynamic image array length, because that's possible, and then complain that it doesn't work.

If you're working with a TextureArray on the Threejs side and the WGSLNodeBuilder suddenly wants to use a normal texture because of the length 1, it crashes because of the wrong shader texture format.

A value of 0 clearly defines a normal texture, and a depth > 0 clearly defines a TextureArray.

But if you'd like, I can set that to 1.
In this case, it can be argued that the user has to take this into account on the threejs side, but this significantly increases the effort.
author: Spiri0

body:
Following [this spec](https://www.w3.org/TR/WGSL/#sampled-texture-type), `3DTexture` does not support array, so the fact that it has `count` does not seem to make sense. `*ArrayTexture` and `*3DTexture` are distinct and should exist individually.

I think that https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/30959 needs to be reviewed or the other textures need to be, as there seems to be an incompatibility.

Textures
| Property | Description | Type |
| -- | -- | -- |
| *Texture | width, height | 2D |
| *ArrayTexture | height, height, depth | Array |
| *3DTexture | height, height, depth | 3D |

RenderTarget
| Property | Description | Type |
| -- | -- | -- |
| *RenderTarget | width, height, { count } | 2D |
| *RenderTargetArray | height, height, depth, { count } | Array |
| *RenderTarget3D | height, height, depth | 3D |

RenderTarget (Currently)
| Property | Description | Type |
| -- | -- | -- |
| *RenderTarget | width, height, { count, depth } | 2D/Array |
| *RenderTarget3D | height, height, depth | 3D |

| Property | Type |
| -- | -- |
| width | 2D |
| height | 2D |
| depth | Array/3D |
| count | RenderTargets count |

I have a feeling we should go back to `RenderTargetArray` and its logic but now preserve the functionality of the `count` property so we can have Multi Render Target Arrays.

So I would go back in the same way so that we have a `StorageArrayTexture` and soon `Storage3DTexture` too.

Changing texture properties I imagine could be a lot of work for us and the users, and this seems like it can be avoided.

@Mugen87 @RenaudRohlinger any thoughts on this?
author: sunag

The bit that might need refactoring is how `autoAllocateDepthBuffer` is implemented here. It's not a property of `RenderTarget` but `frameBufferTarget` gets it assigned.

body: A few days ago, I had this idea. Could we add the `dispose(type?: string)` method to the `EventDispatcher`
author: linbingquan

body: It seems more consistent if  `dispose()` always has the same semantics so I would not introduce a parameter. What you are asking for should be `removeEventListeners( type )` but then the `type` parameter is mandatory.
author: Mugen87
gkjohnson

body: Is calling "dispose" or clearing the listeners necessary here? When the LoadingManager is out of scope and garbage collected these callbacks should be GC'd, as well.
author: gkjohnson

body:
@linbingquan Can we elaborate on some practical use cases for this function? It seems more like a workaround for applications not managing their own event registration well.

It should be noted that adding this function means that EventDispatcher is becoming out of sync with the API of the browsers event system, which I believe was the original intent of the class. This might be okay but perhaps deserves some consideration.
author: gkjohnson

body: Since the PR is not using `removeEventListeners()` anymore, I have removed the addition to `EventDispatcher` as well. If there is no compelling use case, it's indeed better to keep the API in sync with the browser event system.
author: Mugen87

body: Totally right! I have reverted this change.
author: Mugen87

body: What's the value of this? Why not always register for an abort command in the Loaders?
author: gkjohnson

body: Think of a viewer application. If the app creates a loader instance every time an asset should be imported, the default loading manager will be polluted with callbacks. It seems more save to register abort handlers only with custom loading managers and if the app really wants that feature.
author: Mugen87
gkjohnson

body: I've tested this approach before I came up with the version of the PR. The major issue is that `AbortController` is now hardwired in `LoadingManager.abort()` which a problem for loaders which do not rely on `fetch` (like `ImageLoader`). We should head for a solution that makes no assumptions about how an abort operation in a loader is implemented. Hence, I vote for an event based mechanism.
author: Mugen87
gkjohnson

body:
But now the problem is when `loadingManager.abort()` is called twice (users might abort more than one request), the event listener in the loader does not work anymore. Calling `controller.abort();` two times only logs one `ABORTED!`.

https://jsfiddle.net/putyzebw/1/
author: Mugen87
gkjohnson

body: I'm confused about this logic since `FileLoader` uses a single instance of `AbortController` and just passes the same signal into every new request. Aborting once does not mean subsequent request are aborted as well.
author: Mugen87

body: Besides, can you please share some code how `ImageLoader` is supposed to register an event listener on the manager's abort event? It can't do it once in the ctor so...where would be the right place?
author: Mugen87

body: In any event, I have updated the loaders using `AbortController` according to your suggestion. `AbortController` is now recreated after the `abort()` call. That should be indeed more in line with the intended usage of `AbortController`.
author: Mugen87
gkjohnson

body: Updated to the new approach. What do you think?
author: Mugen87

body: It looks great to me! The only other consideration is that apparently `AbortSignal.any` is a bit new (I thought it had been around since AbortSignal was added). But it's been supported in [all major browsers since March 2024](https://caniuse.com/?search=abortSignal.any). I'm not sure what policy on this is but it won't break any build processes since it's not new syntax and should be able to be polyfilled by users if needed.
author: gkjohnson

body: Um, if the polyfill is compact we probably should embed it into the project. What concerns me is that Safari supports this feature only since 17.4 which is a quite recent release. If a platform does not support `AbortSignal.any`, most loaders won't work which is a bummer.
author: Mugen87

body:
Or we simply do this:
```js
signal: ( typeof AbortSignal.any === 'function' ) ? AbortSignal.any( [ this._abortController.signal, this.manager.abortController.signal ] ) : this._abortController.signal
```
And state aborting on manager level is only supported with latest browsers.
author: Mugen87

body: I have implemented this fallback now along with minor doc updates. Should we give the PR in the current state a try?
author: Mugen87
gkjohnson

body: I'm afraid this makes it all too complicated for me ­¤śģ . I see no issues to state that `LoadingManager.abort()` only works with latest browsers which support `AbortSignal.any()`. For now, it's an experimental feature but support will automatically improve over time. Web developers should be familiar with this pattern since Web APIs behave exactly the same. We just built a lightweight layer on top of `AbortController`/`AbortSignal`. If the app provides a polyfill for `AbortSignal.any()`, `LoadingManager.abort()` runs everywhere.
author: Mugen87
gkjohnson

Instead of keeping the function and moving it to `utils.js`, it's better to remove it and inline `performance.now()` in `Clock` like in `NodeFrame`.

body: Its ok for me. If we are going to remove the term transformed, I think it would be better to modify the description of nodes with the *Geometry suffix so that it is explicit that they are not modified with skinned or morph.
author: sunag

body:
Yes, that is why I removed the term.

And I mentioned this because with this PR `normalView` and `normalViewGeometry` now have identical descriptions -- as do `normalWorld` and `normalWorldGeometry`.

I do not know how to describe the accessors having the *Geometry suffix. With the exception of the three.js roughness calculation, I am not aware of any other use cases in the literature where one would need them.

This is why I asked if you would consider removing them, and using them only internally. That would be my preference.
author: WestLangley

body: It is definitely something very specific and I think each engine assigns a name to it, Unreal uses Pre-Skinned Normal for similar purpose and gives some examples using tri-planar texture [here](https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/en-us/unreal-engine/vector-material-expressions-in-unreal-engine). I think it is useful that we have it, as we saw this was used to optimize the code in some examples like `webgpu_tsl_earth`, `webgpu_reflection` and the background shader, so it is positive.
author: sunag

body: @sunag OK. Do you like the Pre-Skinned terminology? I can refer to it as the "pre-skinned vertex normal of the currently-rendered object in view space."
author: WestLangley

body:
IŌĆÖd like it to be automatic for the user to understand that the `Geometry` suffix does not process vertices for skinned, morphed, or custom transformations. `Pre-Skinned` seems to only refer to skinned meshes. Maybe we could add something like this?

```js
/**
 * TSL object that represents the vertex normal of the currently rendered object in view space.
 * 
 * Nodes with the *Geometry suffix do not process skinned, morphed, or custom vertex transformations.
 *
 * @tsl
 ```

This is present in other places as well as `positionGeometry`.

author: CodyJasonBennett

body: Do we not technically need "ReversedWebGPUCoordinateSystem" and "ReversedWebGLCoordinateSystem"? Or is this just for WebGPU?
author: gkjohnson

body: At some point reverse depth buffer will also be supported in `WebGPURenderer`. If the respective computations in `Matrix4` differ in WebGL and WebGPU, then we should indeed rename to `ReversedWebGLCoordinateSystem`.
author: Mugen87

body:
We only need one. `Matrix4` as implemented will just work in WebGPU (its derivation is based on how we make one for WebGPU normally), provided that depth comparisons and clear values are configured to be reversed. Reverse depth can only be implemented with a [0, 1] NDC range, which is what WebGPU and modern graphics APIs use and what we use in WebGL via `EXT_clip_control`, which is otherwise [ŌłÆ1, 1]. I explain why this range matters in floating point and the nature of it in previous comments, more recently in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/29445#issuecomment-2362709888.

Regarding changes, apart from the reversed range, they are the same as you would make when migrating from WebGL to WebGPU. The WebGPU side would only be concerned about the reversed range. The only other constant I would consider would be for an infinite reversed matrix, which only works for perspective #11755. Explicitly handling Infinity might be a better way, provided we document it.

Importantly, as we no longer automatically convert the projection matrix for a configured camera, it is possible to construct an infinite reversed projection matrix in user-land. I'd be happy to create an example for exploration purposes and show its effect for derivations in post-processing. I found it simplifies things tremendously when dealing with depth and occlusion.
author: CodyJasonBennett

body:
Reversed coordinate system?

The _depth range_ or _depth mapping_ is reversed, not the coordinate system.
author: WestLangley

body:
That is not true here, and yet the use is still incorrect, although not strictly, but this is the API we established to make WebGPU possible #26140, which uses modern D3D convention. Do we really need to be pedantic about this, or can you suggest another API that would make sense?

I must say that I am incredibly unhappy to entertain sweeping, breaking changes for the sake of correctness if not strictly (in)correct, and I think that notion is disillusioned to begin with. Unless you have another suggestion, I think we need to live with words not having a strictly encompassing or even correct meaning at all times.
author: CodyJasonBennett

This sentence, or something similar, should be the first sentence. What follows is the explanation.

I am not sure why the word "custom" is necessary.
author: WestLangley

body:
The width and height arguments passed to `RenderTarget.setSize()` define the values of `viewport` and `scissor`.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/300b0fa760832d62391007a8cf1aab0c0b58c11f/src/core/RenderTarget.js#L305-L306

That is not always wanted. When using a custom viewport and scissor, `PassNode` makes sure they are not overwritten.

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR refactors the USDZExporter to use a two-stage process: first building a tree of USD nodes, then converting that tree to final USD string output. The refactoring maintains the same final output while improving code maintainability and making future changes easier.

- Introduces a new `USDNode` class to represent USD nodes with properties, metadata, and children
- Refactors material, mesh, and camera building functions to create node trees instead of direct string output
- Consolidates string generation into the `USDNode.toString()` method for consistent formatting


<details>
<summary>Comments suppressed due to low confidence (2)</summary>

**examples/jsm/exporters/USDZExporter.js:679**
* [nitpick] The function name 'buildTextureNodes' is inconsistent with the existing naming pattern. Other functions in the file use 'build' prefix for single objects (buildMaterial, buildCamera, buildMeshObject). Consider renaming to 'buildTextureNodeSet' or 'createTextureNodes' to better reflect that it returns multiple nodes.
```
	function buildTextureNodes( texture, mapType, color ) {
```
**examples/jsm/exporters/USDZExporter.js:507**
* [nitpick] The function name 'buildMeshNode' is inconsistent with the original 'buildMesh' function it replaces. Since this is a refactoring, consider keeping the original name 'buildMesh' to maintain consistency with other build functions in the codebase.
```
function buildMeshNode( geometry ) {
```
</details>

body:
I'm trying to understand how the two render calls are interpreted differently, one as a workgroupSize and the other as a dispatchSize. Are two nodes in your mind more desirable than just one with the workgroupSize passed in the initial compute call where the computeNode is created and the dispatchSize passed in the render.compute call of this one computeNode?

I admit that I find this more clear
```js

const comp = computeUpdate();

computeParticles = comp.compute( [ 64 ] );   //clear relationship to the computeNode and its workgroupSize

renderer.compute( computeParticles, [ 700 ] );   //clear relationship to the computeNode and its dispatchSize
```
Especially if you work with multiple computeNodes like I do, it can quickly become confusing when the same notation is interpreted differently, one time as workgroup and the other time as dispatch.
But that's just my personal opinion and I respect it if you have a different opinion and want it the way you mean it. But I still have to understand how it works first.
author: Spiri0
sunag

body:
> We can go like this too, I agree with you and I think it can be useful
> ```js
> // pass work group size on kernel creation
> nodeFn().compute( [ 64 ] );
>
> // pass dispatch count on evaluation
> renderer.compute( computeParticles, [ 700 ] ); 
> ```

To chime in - my preference is to pass the "dispatch count" separate from work group size when evaluating the compute kernel (whether that the be renderer.compute or whatever future incarnation of the compute API).

Perhaps it's because I'm familiar with the underlying APIs here but my impression is that when calling `nodeFn().compute( workGroupSize )` the compute shader must be recreated (or dirty checked somehow) since the work group size values must be inlined in the shader string, which shouldn't be needed when changing the dispatch count. This means for a number of use cases you'd have to rebuild the compute node whenever the screen resizes, which seems wasteful. All to say I think the separation of operations feels valuable and mirrors the WebGPU API.

And thanks to both of you for the work here! The compute API is shaping up quite nicely, I think.
author: gkjohnson

body:
Thank you for participating too @gkjohnson :)

My concern with `workGroupSize`  be the first parameter like `nodeFn().compute( workGroupSize )` is that the `compute()` is a node, and can be used inside the material, being processed only when the object/material is rendered like [this](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/30768#issue-2936544460) example. We have the same `countOfPoints` equivalent to the number of instances(a good idea would be to automate based on the number of instances if no value is added), but this may not be the general case. The user could still overwrite the number of dispatches using `renderer.compute( computeParticles, 700 )` and maybe using `computeParticles.setCount()`.

In short, `compute()` can be used without the `renderer.compute()` call if the user binds to a `NodeMaterial`, so it needs a number of dispatches.

`workGroupSize` is also quite advanced for most and I hope we can automate it based on dispatchSize and nodes of `Fn` call if the user hasn't defined it.
author: sunag
sunag
Spiri0

body:
> My concern with workGroupSize be the first parameter like nodeFn().compute( workGroupSize ) is that the compute() is a node, and can be used inside the material, being processed only when the object/material is rendered like https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/30768#issue-2936544460 example.

I understand this is probably in part my lack of knowledge around how nodes work here but that example isn't super clear to me. It seems that "countOfPoints" is already implicitly defined by the "count" field on the "pointCloud" object so I would `instanceIndex` in that context to be available as long as something like an "instanceIndex" node is passed into the function if it's just mapping to each particle index (it's not clear where "instanceIndex" comes from in this case).

Even if calling "compute" is needed in that case for some reason, though, it feels a bit too much like trying to get two different APIs to look the same even though they're not. A "workgroupSize" isn't really relevant outside of compute kernels so I question allowing it as an argument when not running in a compute context. Similarly for compute kernels - I question having an argument like "dispatchCount" in two places (compute node, renderer.compute). As someone new looking at this it makes the goal and behavior of the "compute" function feel a bit muddy.

> You could also extend it without changing the current functionality like nodeFn().computeInstance( workGroupSize ).

Creating something like a `createComputeKernel( workGroupSize )` separate from a `compute( count )` function (if needed) would feel more understandable to me.

> workGroupSize is also quite advanced for most and I hope we can automate it based on dispatchSize and nodes of Fn call if the user hasn't defined it.

It can be automated but using the appropriate dimension and size for "workgroupSize" and "dispatchCount" can have a pretty significant performance impact so while it may be easier for new users to just pass in simple numbers (or nothing) it would be nice to encourage new users to select smarter values. Unfortunately as far as I unerstand the "best" values aren't something that can be automatically selected and depend on the shape of the data being processed.
author: gkjohnson
Spiri0

body:
Thank you @Spiri0 you have helped a lot and I am happy about that.

Node's philosophy is that everything is shared and reused(whenever possible). If `compute()` had a single use within `render.compute( .. )`, it wouldn't make much sense for it to be a node; it could even be a conventional class. The `compute()` node can be used on any material, and that's the important part of the `count` parameter being in the node, to make it explicit if it needs a specific dispatch size. The same goes for `pass()`. The `pass()` node is used in post-processing, but you could also use it in any material, like in the `webgpu_portal` example. I think it's at these times that we see the system automate tasks, as noted by mrdoob https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/30768#issuecomment-2759929640

I think the idea of using dispatches size in the `renderer.compute()` is interesting and would like to keep it as well, but we haven't found a middle ground yet, although some things have already been drafted. But the goal of this design should be to accommodate different approaches and not favor just one.

I think we could move forward creating a new TSL component https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/31402#discussion_r2208986871 but based on @gkjohnson's name suggestion I would simplify just `computeKernel( nodeFn(), [ 16, 16 ] )` and ` nodeFn().computeKernel( [ 16, 16 ] )`

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR refactors the SSAOPass visibility caching system to improve performance by replacing the Map-based cache with an array that only stores objects whose visibility is actually modified. The change eliminates the need to traverse all scene objects during restoration and reduces memory overhead.

- Replaces Map-based visibility cache with array for better performance
- Only caches objects that have their visibility modified (Points, Line, Line2 objects)
- Optimizes restoration by iterating only cached objects instead of entire scene

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR optimizes the GTAOPass visibility caching mechanism by replacing a Map data structure with an array to improve performance during visibility state management. The change focuses on reducing overhead when temporarily hiding specific object types (points, lines) during rendering.

- Replaces Map-based visibility cache with array-based storage
- Modifies cache population to only store objects that need visibility changes
- Updates restoration logic to use simple array iteration instead of scene traversal
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The condition logic has changed behavior compared to the original implementation. Previously, all objects had their visibility cached regardless of type, but visibility was only modified for specific types. Now only visible objects of specific types are cached, which could cause issues if an object's type changes between cache and restore operations.

body:
I'm afraid we can't merge the PR because of these two lines. We need a `GLTexture` class similar to `GLBufferAttribute` so you can do this:
```js
const glTexture = glBinding.getCameraImage( camera );
cameraTex = new GLTexture( glTexture ) ;
```
See https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/26452#issuecomment-1658152049 for more details. 

We require a special handling for `GLTexture` since if you would call `dispose()` on `cameraTex` on app level, you create an invalid operation. There might be more edge cases that we have to handle because `getCameraImage()` returns as so called _opaque texture_. E.g. what would happen if the user sets `needsUpdate` to `true` for `cameraTex`?

Not having `GLTexture` was the actual blocker why camera access has not been implemented yet. `WebXRDepthSensing` should also be updated to use the new `GLTexture` class.

> I have chosen not to implement any form of Opaque/Raw/External Texture specific class at this time as previously discussed in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/26891, as such a texture is technically also used by WebXR Layers and Depth Sensing modules, but are currently not present in either of their implementations.

I'm afraid when merging `WebXRDepthSensing` we have overlooked the `__webglTexture` hack. It's now a good opportunity to find a better solution for this use case. 

With `WebGPURenderer` in mind, we probably should name the class `RawTexture` instead of `GLTexture`. The class also needs an `isOpaque` flag that is set to `true` when using the texture from `getCameraImage()`.
author: Mugen87

After a closer look, we can simplify the code in `WebGLTextures`. Because of the enhancement of `setTexture2D()`, you can delete the modifications in `deleteTexture()`.

`deleteTexture()` is only relevant when `initTexture()` is executed which is not the case for raw textures (because of the new check in this line). So no dispose handler is registered and `deallocateTexture()` is never executed. That is the expected behavior for raw textures. `RawTexture` is also irrelevant for render targets so it seems were are safe here.

author: sunag

body:
I wanted to try moving away from manual indentation as much as possible, even if the current implementation is a bit of a half step in that direction. When attempting to move in this direction, I noticed that the addLineFlowCode function would invoke addLineFlowCodeBlock if the current node was passed in and the context's nodeBlock was set. addLineFlowCodeBlock doesn't append any code to the shader; instead, it modifies the node's cached flowCodes and flowCodes block property. I figured that if we want a node's cached data to be as accurate as possible, this data will be captured, which is not currently the case with addCodeFlow, which just appends text to this.flow. 

I was initially concerned that the conditional statements would be output twice (once in addLineFlowCode and then again in addFlowCodeHierarchy) but the if ( flowCodeBlock.get( nodeBlockHierarchy ) === true ) check prevents that from happening (flowCodeBlock will always contain nodeBlockHierarchy since nodeBlockHierachy would be set to builder.context.nodeBlock, which itself equals the codeBlock set in addLineFlowCodeBlock). 

Additionally, I didn't see any repeating code occurring when logging the shaders for existing examples that use select, which means the code is working as expected.

body:
A TAA performs some sort of denoising so I found a separate denoise pass is not necessarily required.

Also not that the way we apply AO at the moment means we attenuate the entire scene. A more physically correct approach would compute AO _before_ the beauty pass and then just attenuate the indirect light. This requires a more complex setup though.

Besides, as long as transparent object write into the depth buffer, they are affected by the SSAO computation. The given window is especially challenging since although light travels through it, it can receives shadows because of its dull/milky surface. I guess the issue could be mitigate somewhat with a directional light shining through the window but that would require AO only attenuates the indirect light.

body:
Are you okay with adding a bit more description to this blur approach? Something like:
```
Applies a hash blur effect to the given texture node.

The approach of this blur is different compared to Gaussian and box blur since
it does not rely on a kernel to apply a convolution. Instead, it reads the base
texture multiple times in a random pattern and then averages the samples. A 
typical artifact of this technique is a slightly noisy appearance of the blur which 
can be mitigated by increasing the number of iterations (see `repeats` parameter). 
Compared to Gaussian blur, hash blur requires just a single pass.
```

All three loaders in this PR return linear-sRGB as the default.

I filed this PR because I think only `KTX2Loader` currently honors the color space defined in the file.

Since three.js generally expects users to set the color space, I thought maintaining a consistent pattern for users to copy would be advisable.
author: WestLangley

body:
I've mentioned this multiple times in the past but I personally am not a fan to setting default values again since it makes the code verbose. The idea is to keep the code simple and compact and users should rely on proper default values.

If one color space is by far the most used in textures returned by the loaders, I think there is no reason the define the color space again.

body:
Indeed, that's what the PR does.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/7a1f975aca878c0c5c50bc74b051c1e6bb6269d3/examples/jsm/loaders/UltraHDRLoader.js#L326
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/7a1f975aca878c0c5c50bc74b051c1e6bb6269d3/examples/jsm/loaders/EXRLoader.js#L2405
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/7a1f975aca878c0c5c50bc74b051c1e6bb6269d3/examples/jsm/loaders/RGBELoader.js#L464
author: Mugen87

body:
The user should not be expected to comprehend the source code to know that it is necessary to set a correct color space.

Or, for that matter, to know that a loader, such as EXRLoader, does not honor the color space, even if it is defined in the file.

Or to know that the loader is guessing.

The other examples of this type define a color space upon loading. I think that is a good thing, because it is a pattern users copy.
author: WestLangley

body:
Personally ŌĆö I do think it's a good habit to explicitly assign color space when loading individual textures. For most formats (excluding KTX2) we don't have reliable metadata, so the loaders can't do more than guess with a reasonable default. For EXR things might improve eventually, see https://github.com/AcademySoftwareFoundation/openexr/issues/1783. 

Another example, when loading individual textures in R3F with TextureLoader, the default is sRGB. In three.js the default is NoColorSpace.<sup>1</sup> Not all users are subject to the same defaults.

<small><sup>1</sup> I prefer our choice, because at least when you forget to assign `THREE.SRGBColorSpace` to a color texture, it is visible as a problem _in color_, rather than a problem in occlusion or normals or metalness that may be much less obvious to diagnose.</small>
author: donmccurdy

body: FYI, that information about R3F is recently incorrect, and now defers to three.js defaults. We agree with Don here and made this change with v9. Both R3F and the three.js editor will assign sRGB if otherwise unset only if they see a texture applied to a known list of material fields (IMO it is confusing this isn't managed by three, as this can be done conservatively). It is valid usage to use something other than sRGB for color textures, as users may include P3-authored textures in the future (or another wide gamut color space like Rec 2020). That makes it impossible to guess the correct option and only possible to guess an incorrect option if the context is known.
author: CodyJasonBennett
